I hold 3h 7d (the weakest possible hand in poker) on a Th Ad 4c flop. This board completely misses my hand - no pair, no draw, zero equity. Minni checked from the small blind, indicating weakness. While her limp could represent a wide range, I have absolutely nothing to show down. With zero equity and out of position, checking is the only sound play. This keeps the pot small and allows me to fold without further loss if Minni bets the turn. Bluffing here would be spewing chips with no realistic plan, and I have no hand value to raise or call with. Checking gives me the option to see the turn cheaply while minimizing damage with the worst hand in poker.

I hold 7♥9♠ on Th Ad 4c 4d — no pair, no real draw. We’re out of position and the flop was checked through, so the pot is small (200) and I don’t want to bloat it with a marginal hand. A turn bet would need significant fold equity to be profitable (min-bet ~100 requires the villain to fold >33%); Claude’s check-range includes many hands that will call (A, T, small pairs, combos) so I expect poor fold equity. I’ll check to keep the pot under control, plan to fold to any meaningful bet on the turn, and reassess the river if checked to (look for a cheap bluffing spot on a blank river or realize showdown equity if I pair).

With 3h-7d, I have the worst possible hand in poker on a board of Th-Ad-4c-4d. I have zero equity - no pair, no draw, no showdown value whatsoever. Minni just checked the turn from the small blind, indicating weakness or a desire to see the river cheaply. 

At this point, I have three realistic options:
1. Bluff - This is terrible. There's no credible story here. I have no hand, no draw, no reason to bet. Minni's check suggests she has something that beats air (which is everything), and any bet would just lose more chips.
2. Call if she bets - Not applicable since she checked.
3. Check - This is correct. I minimize my losses by seeing the river for free.

Checking allows me to either get lucky with a 3 or 7 on the river, or fold if Minni bets the river. With zero equity and a weak opponent showing weakness by checking, there's no reason to voluntarily put more chips in the pot with garbage.

Check. The river (Th Ad 4c 4d Ac) leaves me with no pair — my best five is the board (A-A-4-4-T). Any opponent holding an Ace or a Four now has a full house and will happily call or raise a bluff, so my fold equity is poor. Claude checked flop and turn which slightly favors weakness, but he could be slow-playing a full house. The pot is tiny (200) versus deep stacks (~14k), so bluffing here is low-expected-value and needlessly risks chips. I’ll check and take the free showdown; if Claude bets a realistic value/raise I’ll fold to avoid losing to a full house.

River analysis: I hold 3h-7d on a board of Th-Ad-4c-4d-Ac. This is the absolute worst hand in poker with zero showdown value - no pair, no draw possibility on the final street. Minni has checked the river, indicating weakness or a hand she's willing to check down. The board is heavily coordinated with a pair of aces and a pair of fours - a dangerous texture for any bluff attempt. Betting here would be pure spewing, as I'd either lose more chips when called or unnecessarily fold the best hand. Since there's no bet to call and I have nothing, checking is the only logical play. I see the showdown for free and accept my loss with the worst possible hand. This minimizes further chip loss and keeps my decision-making sound - never bluff with 3-7 on a paired river when your opponent shows weakness through repeated checks.

On the button with 8♦4♥ — a weak, unplayable hand off-suit. The price to see a flop is tiny (100 into a 150 pot) and I have position on both opponents, so the correct exploitative play is to call and realize equity/see a cheap flop. Raising to 200 to steal is possible, but both SB and BB have shown willingness to defend and a small open will often get called or 3-bet; with this hand I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position postflop. Plan: call, evaluate the flop — continue with decent pair/draws or a good board; fold to strong aggression on dry misses.

Small blind with J4o facing a 50 chip call. Getting 5:1 pot odds (250:50) on a minimal investment from a deep stack (17500). Position post-flop is favorable. This is a standard profitable call in the small blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Five offsuit. Both the Button (Minni) and the Small Blind (Claude) have limped into the pot. There is no reason to raise with this hand out of position. Checking allows me to see a free flop and potentially improve my hand or play according to board texture without risking any additional chips.

J4o is Jack-high on a paired Kings-Five board. With no made hand, no drawing potential, and out of position, checking is the only prudent play. This controls the pot size while maintaining flexibility for future streets. Betting would be speculatively bad as a weak hand - either I get called and find myself dominated, or I win only the small pre-flop pot. Checking allows me to see the turn affordably and escape if opponents show strength.

I have bottom pair (fives) on a board that is paired with kings. In a limped pot, this hand has decent showdown value but is extremely vulnerable to any king in an opponent's range. Since Claude checked, I will check as well to keep the pot small and see how the button (Minni) acts. There is no need to bet and risk being raised by a king or better.

Flop is 5♦ K♦ K♠. I hold 8♦4♥ — no pair, only a runner‑runner diamond possibility (needs two diamonds to come). This is a three‑way pot and both opponents checked, but that doesn’t give me enough fold equity to profitably bluff — many hands that continue will call a c‑bet (any King, any pair, most draws) and Flash/Claude can be sticky. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand out of position on later streets. Plan: control the pot by checking, see how opponents proceed on the turn, and fold to any sizable pressure. If both check through we can reevaluate for a cheap steal or showdown on later streets.

Checking on the turn with J4o. I have jack-high with no made hand and no meaningful draws. The board is paired Kings with 5d and 8c - cards that don't help my holding. All three players checked the flop, indicating a weak action sequence across the table. I'm out of position and need to be cautious with marginal holdings. With 0 to call, checking allows me to see the river for free while keeping the pot manageable with my weak hand. Betting here offers no value - J4o cannot extract chips from worse hands and is vulnerable to better hands. The paired Kings board makes bluffing ineffective given multiple opponents. Checking is the disciplined play that controls variance while maintaining flexibility.

I have bottom pair (fives) on a board paired with Kings. The hand was limped pre-flop and checked around on the flop, indicating a lack of aggression. Since Claude has checked again on the turn, I will check as well. My hand has some showdown value in a small pot, but there's no reason to build the pot or risk being raised when I'm likely either way ahead of junk or way behind a King or a better pair.

Check. I turned second pair (8♦4♥ with board 5d Kd Ks 8c) but this is a weak, vulnerable hand in a three‑way pot. Any King, any 5, or sets/trips beat me and there’s little fold equity facing two opponents who both checked flop and turn. Betting here bloats the pot and just gets called or raised by stronger holdings. Pot control is the priority: see the river for free, fold to meaningful aggression (I only beat bluffs and very small pairs), and only consider betting/raising if I improve to two‑pair/trips or the river texture clearly misses opponents’ ranges.

River action with J4o on a paired King board (5d Kd Ks 8c Td). I have jack-high with no draws remaining. All three players checked through flop and turn, indicating weak collective holdings. Bluffing is ineffective on this paired board, and my hand has minimal showdown value. I'm out of position, so betting offers no control. The correct play is to check and see showdown, accepting that my weak holding is likely behind and taking the pot to conclusion without further investment. This is disciplined play that minimizes losses on a marginal holding.

The hand was limped pre-flop and has been checked on every street so far. I have bottom pair (fives), giving me two pair (Kings and Fives) with a Queen kicker. In a pot this small where no one has shown any aggression, my hand likely has showdown value. However, betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands and only risks getting raised by a Ten, an Eight, or a slow-played King. Checking is the safest and most logical play to reach showdown.

I have a weak second pair (8♦ with the 8♣ on the turn) in a three‑way pot on a K K 8 10 rainbow board. Both opponents checked flop/turn and checked the river as well, so I have very little fold equity against two players — a bet will often get called by any K, any 5, a 10, or small pairs and can be raised by value hands. Pot control and showdown are optimal: my pair has some showdown value but is too vulnerable to bloat the pot with. I’ll check to see a showdown and avoid turning this marginal hand into a big bluff. If either opponent bets, I’ll fold to meaningful pressure and only consider calling very small bets.

Raising from the button with K3o. While this hand is marginal in absolute terms, button position is critical in poker. A standard 2.5x raise (250) takes initiative, applies pressure to win the blinds outright, and keeps me in control if called. My stack size is healthy and position gives me a significant advantage post-flop. This is a profitable long-term play from the button even with marginal holdings.

In a 3-handed situation, K6o is a marginal hand to defend from the Small Blind against a Button raise. Playing out of position for the rest of the hand with a weak King-high hand often leads to difficult spots, especially against Claude who has shown significant aggression in previous hands. Folding is the most prudent strategy to preserve the stack for better opportunities.

Facing a button open to 250 heads-up with Jc6h in the big blind. This hand is marginal but defendable against a wide button opening. Calling 150 (250 total committed) gives me ~27% break-even equity (150/(400+150)=27.3%) which is likely below my equity vs Claude’s open range, so a call is +EV. A tiny 3-bet to the min (400) would have poor fold equity and create a tough OOP spot on deep stacks; folding is too tight. Plan: call to keep the pot manageable, evaluate the flop — proceed aggressively if I hit a jack/6 or good draws, otherwise be prepared to check‑fold to heavy aggression on dry A/K/Q type boards. If Claude 3-bets large I will fold preflop.

I have Jc6h on a Jh Js Qh flop — I flopped trips. This is a very strong, thin-value hand vs the preflop raiser (Claude). The board has two hearts, but the only real immediate threat is someone holding two hearts (rare) or runner-runner action; overall I want to maximize value and build the pot while disguising strength. Claude is an aggressive button opener and likely to continuation-bet here, so my plan is to check to induce a c-bet and then check-raise for value (or call a very large bet and re-evaluate). If he checks back I can still extract on the turn. Checking now gives me the best chance to get extra chips from worse Qx, heart combinations and bluffs while keeping control of the hand with deep stacks.

I raised preflop with K3o from the button and Minni called from the big blind. The flop of J♥ J♠ Q♥ is completely unfavorable for my hand - I have just king-high with no draws, no pair, no flush possibility, and a terrible kicker. Minni's check suggests she may not have a strong hand either, or she could be slowplaying a jack or queen. Given that K3o has minimal equity against most of Minni's continuing range on this coordinated board, checking is the prudent play. This allows me to: (1) control pot size with a weak hand, (2) get more information about her hand strength before committing more chips, (3) potentially improve on the turn or river cheaply, or (4) get to showdown if she also checks. Making a c-bet here with such a weak hand risks getting raised or called by a better holding, which would put me in a difficult spot.

I hold Jc6h and flopped trips on Jh Js Qh. Both of us checked the flop, so Claude’s range is wide and often includes single-pair hands (Qx, Tx) and broadways that will pay a turn bet. The Td on the turn is not a direct threat except for K9 straights or the extremely rare Jd quads; the real danger is a river pairing Q or T turning some holdings into full houses, so I should extract value now. I will bet a medium size (300 into a 550 pot, ~55%) to charge one-pair hands and worse two-pair combinations, keep worse hands in, and avoid gifting a free river. If Claude raises a small-to-medium amount I will call and re-evaluate; only very large, polarized action will make me seriously consider folding on this street.

I have K3o - king-high with no pair, no draw, and no equity on the J-J-Q-T board. Minni checked the flop and bet 300 on the turn after the Ten came, indicating her hand likely improved (QT, TT, QJ, A9 draws, etc.). With zero equity against her most probable continuing range, folding is the mathematically sound decision. No draw, no pair, no showdown value.

I am on the button with Ten-Trey offsuit. This is a very weak hand with poor playability and equity. In a standard strategy, T3o is not part of a button opening range. I will fold to preserve my chips for a more advantageous situation.

We’re effectively heads-up (SB vs BB) so I’ll take the initiative — small blind is the button postflop, so raising is standard. J♥2♥ is marginal but suited with a Jack blocker and some flush/backdoor potential; a min-raise to 200 (2x BB total) is a low-risk steal that applies pressure and gives me position postflop. If Claude folds I take the 150 pot. If he calls I’ll proceed with a continuation-bet plan on favourable textures and fold to substantial 3-bets (J2s is not a good 3-bet defense deep-stacked). Raise to 200 to open the pot and retain initiative.

I'm in the Big Blind with J8o (Jack-Eight offsuit) facing a raise from Minni in the Small Blind. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While J8o is a mediocre hand and I'll be out of position post-flop, the odds are too favorable to fold. My large stack (17,100) means this call is minimal in relation to my bankroll. I can easily realize equity in a heads-up pot and make decisions based on flop action. A 3-bet is not justified with this hand strength.

I was the preflop aggressor and I hold Jh2h on 4d 9h 7h — a four-card heart flush draw plus a Jack overcard. Claude called from the big blind, so his range is wide (pairs, hearts, broadways, suited connectors). A continuation bet here applies pressure, denies correct odds to many of his holdings, and builds the pot when I hit. I’ll bet ~50% of the pot (200 into 400) to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with a draw. If he calls I’ll reevaluate on the turn and continue on favorable cards (heart or J) or control/fold to heavy aggression if I haven’t improved.

J8o is too weak to continue out of position against an aggressive opponent. While pot odds are close to mathematically breakeven (~3:1 with ~25-30% equity), being out of position on the turn/river with a marginal hand creates unfavorable implied odds. I'll concede this marginal flop and wait for stronger holdings in better positions.

I’m on the button with Js9d, 3-handed and stacks deep. This is a good spot to exert pressure and steal the blinds — J9o plays fine in position but isn’t a hand I want to get stacked with out of line. I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300) to deny cheap odds to the blinds and either take down the pot or play postflop in position. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (no need to gamble here), and if called use position to c-bet dry/J‑high flops or control the pot and fold to heavy, non‑credible aggression when equity is low.

K2o is one of the worst hands in poker. While I'm in the small blind with pot odds and a chance to win against a button raise, the hand has minimal equity post-flop and plays poorly out of position. Even at decent odds, the hand's fundamental weakness makes folding the correct decision. With a large stack and many more hands to play, I should conserve chips for premium holdings.

I am in the big blind with 8s 6c, which is a weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the button, and Claude has already folded. Given my position and the weakness of my hand, I should not be calling or raising against a button open. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

J9 from the button is a solid hand with good playability. I have positional advantage as the button, and this hand has potential for straights and reasonable high card value. A standard 3x BB raise to 300 is appropriate here. This raise accomplishes several goals: (1) takes down blinds some percentage of the time, (2) gives me position post-flop with information advantage, (3) maintains balance in my button opening range. Looking at the stack depths and opponent tendencies (Minni aggressive in BB, Flash shorter stack), this is a profitable aggressive play from the button.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. Claude, on the Button, has opened with a 3x raise to 300. In a three-handed game, ranges are wider, but A4o is a weak hand to play out of position against an active opponent like Claude, especially with the Big Blind still to act. Calling often leads to difficult post-flop situations where I am dominated or playing a weak Ace out of position. I will choose the conservative route and fold here to preserve my stack for better spots.

I’m in the big blind heads-up with JJ facing a button open to 300. JJ is a strong, high-equity hand heads-up and I don’t want to play a bloated pot out-of-position by just calling. I’ll 3-bet for value and fold equity to narrow Claude’s range (weaker pairs, A-x and broadways), while using a size that discourages light 4-bets but still gets called by worse. A raise to 1,200 (≈4x his open) is a standard, balanced size here — if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on A/K high boards and extract value on safe textures; if he 4-bets very large I’ll re-evaluate and often fold to extreme aggression.

Calling Minni's 3-bet to 1200 with J9o from the button. Given Minni's aggressive tendencies shown throughout the session, her 3-bet range is likely wide. J9o has decent equity (~42-45%) and I retain positional advantage postflop. Stack depths allow for healthy postflop play. This is a marginal but profitable call against this opponent's tendencies. I can outplay postflop in position and take down pots when appropriate.

I 3‑bet preflop to 1200 so I’m the preflop aggressor and hold an overpair (Jc Jd) to this dry 7s‑2d‑4s flop. This board is low and uncoordinated — it hits very few of Claude’s likely calling 3‑bet combos (broadways, A‑x, medium pairs, small suited connectors). I want to extract value from worse pairs and A/high hands and deny free cards to his drawing combos, so a continuation bet is correct. I size to about half‑to‑two‑thirds pot to get called by Ax/broadways and to have some fold equity versus small pairs. Bet 1400 (~57% of the 2450 pot). Plan: call down relatively wide if he makes a modest raise, re‑evaluate on the turn if he shows heavy aggression (large check‑raise or shove) since only a few hands beat me (sets, 77/44/22, or very unlikely two‑pair). If he just calls I’ll continue extracting on safe turn cards.

Calling Minni's flop raise with J9o on 7-2-4 flop. Analysis: (1) Pot odds are favorable - calling 1400 to win ~5250, requiring ~21% equity to break even. (2) Hand strength: J9o has Jack-high with a gutshot straight draw (any King completes a straight), plus two overcards. Against Minni's likely range of overpairs, broadway cards, and drawing hands, my equity is approximately 30-35%. (3) Position matters: I'm on the button with position post-turn, which allows me to make better decisions with more information. (4) Stack sizes: Both players have reasonable stacks (me: 15,750; Minni: 12,400), so we can comfortably proceed. (5) Opponent tendencies: Minni has shown consistent aggression throughout this session, suggesting her flop bet range is relatively wide. (6) Implied odds: If I hit my gutshot or catch an overcard, I can potentially extract additional value on later streets. Folding would be overcautious given the odds and range advantage. Raising would be a marginal semi-bluff in a spot where Minni might stack off, risking J9o unnecessarily. Calling allows me to take a favorable risk-reward spot while maintaining flexibility.

Check. The turn (7♠-2♦-4♠-2♣) pairs the board and dramatically increases the chance Claude turned a very strong hand (22 -> quads, 44 or 77 -> full house). I have an overpair (J♣J♦) but this card helps his calling/flatting range more than mine. I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the dry flop to extract, but betting the turn risks a check‑raise and committing a large portion of my stack to hands that now beat me. Checking controls the pot, lets me see his intention, and keeps weaker Ax/Kx and medium pairs (the hands I beat) in the pot. Plan: check and call small‑to‑medium turn bets to realize equity versus bluffs/Ax, fold to very large shoves or aggressive check‑raises that almost always represent full house/quads, and re‑evaluate river for thin value or pot‑control as necessary.

Minni's turn check is a significant shift from her aggressive flop betting. With J9o, I currently have Jack-high with a gutshot straight draw (4 outs to a King) against her likely range of medium-strength hands (overpairs below the board, missed draws, or slowplayed premium hands). 

Analyzing my options:

1. **Betting the turn**: This is marginal at best. Against her range, my J9 is vulnerable. If she calls, I'm likely 30-35% to improve to a straight. If she raises with a stronger hand, I'm forced into a difficult decision with Jack-high in a large pot. The bet gains fold equity from weak hands she might fold, but costs equity when called by better holdings.

2. **Checking back**: Given the board texture (7-2-4-2), a blank for most holdings, and her check indicating potential weakness or medium strength, I can take a free river card with a gutshot draw. This preserves chips and maintains optionality. If I hit my straight on the river (16% probability), I'm in position to extract value. If I miss, I can evaluate her river action and fold if needed, saving chips.

With stack depths of 14,350 for me and 12,400 for Minni, this hand doesn't warrant a turn bluff. Checking allows the river to develop with better information about my draw's completion while avoiding unnecessary risk with a presently weak hand.

I hold JJ — an overpair to this final board (7s-2d-4s-2c-8s). Claude called my 3‑bet preflop and my flop c‑bet, then checked the paired turn, which makes heavy two‑pair/full‑house hands (77/44/22) and quads possible but not highly frequent. The river 8s completes some spade flushes, but overall my hand is still ahead of a lot of his continuing range (Ax, Kx, medium pairs, and occasional missed broadways). Checking gives up thin value from those worse hands; a small value bet will charge Ax/Kx and single pairs that will call, while keeping the price low if I face a raise. I’ll make a compact value bet (~1/3 pot) to extract from worse hands but avoid overcommitting to a raise — if Claude jams or makes a large raise I’ll strongly favor folding because those lines usually represent full houses/quads. Action: small value bet to get called by worse.

Folding Jack-high on the river. While Minni is aggressive, her turn check followed by river raise suggests a hand stronger than my Jack-high. The pot odds (4:1) require approximately 20% equity to break even. Against her likely range of overpairs, broadway cards, and completed hands, my equity is 15-20% at best - marginally unprofitable. J9o has completely whiffed; the gutshot never completed, and the paired board on the turn likely helped her range more than mine. This is a clear fold spot despite good pot odds, as the equity math doesn't support calling with this weak holding. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

On the button with King-Eight offsuit, I have a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. My stack is currently 8,000, and I need to be active to build it back up. Opening for 3x the big blind is a standard play to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot.

I’m in the small blind with K♣5♣ facing a button open to 300. K5s is marginal but defendable — suited king gives decent equity and useful blockers against a wide button opening. Calling (putting a total of 300 in) is the lowest-variance, +EV play here: it buys me a flop with good implied odds without committing out of position. A light 3-bet OOP would be too committal with a weak kicker and invites difficult play versus the big blind or an aggressive button. If Claude 3-bets big I’ll fold; if we see a flop I’ll continue only with a king, strong flush/straight draws, or a favorable board to bluff/c-bet selectively.

Folding 2c Qd in the big blind. This is a weak hand (queen-deuce offsuit) with several critical disadvantages: 1) Out of position postflop against two opponents, 2) Weak kicker makes top pair vulnerable, 3) Both Flash and Minni have shown strength on the action. While the pot odds (700 to call 200, or 3.5:1) might tempt a call mathematically, I don't have sufficient equity here and will be in a difficult spot out of position. Flash is short-stacked so could be widening, but Minni's call into two bets suggests some hand strength. Better spots will come with premium hands and better positioning. Preserving chips and folding weak hands unprofitably played is key to long-term success.

I flatted preflop with Kc5c to see a flop against a wide button range. Flop Ad 6h 5s gives me a pair of fives with a king kicker — a marginal made hand that loses to any Ace, 6x, pocket pairs above 5, or two pair/sets. I’m out of position and the pot is already 700, so my priority is pot control and to realize equity rather than bloating the pot where I’m likely behind. Checking keeps weaker hands and bluffs in, allows me to fold to clear strength, and gives a chance to improve on the turn.

Plan: check and evaluate the button’s action. If Flash checks back I can take a free turn card and reassess (lead if I improve to two pair/top pair with a favorable runout). If he c-bets small (roughly <= ~1/3 pot, ~225–250) I’ll call to extract value from worse hands and to keep his bluffs/draws in. If he bets large (> ~1/2 pot, ~350+) or raises, I will usually fold — he’s representing Ax or strong made hands and I don’t want to get committed with a vulnerable second pair. If he shoves, fold unless I have a very specific read that he’s wide-shoving. Overall: pot control, call small bluffs, fold to big aggression.

On a dry, Ace-high flop after raising pre-flop, a continuation bet is standard as I represent the Ace and can often take the pot down immediately. However, Minni called from the small blind, which is a range that often contains some Aces or pairs. Since I have King-high with some backdoor potential but no direct draw or pair, checking back allows me to see a turn for free and potentially realize equity if a King or running cards help me, while also keeping the pot small against a range that may have hit this board.

I hold Kc5c and the turn (Ad 6h 5s Ah) gives me two pair — Aces and Fives — with a King kicker. That’s a strong but somewhat vulnerable hand because any Ax (trip Aces) or a 6x (two pair A+6) beats me. Flash’s line — button open preflop, then check-check on the flop and turn — makes a big Ace or trips less likely (many Ax hands would have bet the flop). Given I’m first to act and improved, I should extract value from worse hands (Kx, single fives, medium pairs, missed broadways) while charging the few draws. I’ll make a value bet of about half the pot (350 into 700): big enough to get calls from worse hands and charge potential draws, but not so large that I’m overly committed if I face heavy aggression. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river; if he check-raises big I’ll assume trips/Ax and concede the pot.

After checking back the flop to control the pot, the turn brings a second Ace. Minni leads out with a small bet of 350 into a pot of 700. In a three-handed game, this lead often represents a 'probe bet' or a thin value bet with a small pair (like 5x or 6x) or even a complete air bluff trying to take advantage of the paired board. Since I have King-high, I still beat all of her total air bluffs, but I am losing to any pair or any Ace. However, with my King-high and the board pairing the Ace, I have some showdown value and can potentially induce further bluffs or improve on the river. Folding to such a small bet when I have a decent high-card hand in a short-handed game seems too weak. I will call to see the river and evaluate her next action, as the second Ace makes it slightly less likely she has one herself.

River is Ad 6h 5s Ah 6d; my Kc5c that was two pair on the turn is now effectively A A 6 6 K (board pairs A and 6). That means any Ace or any 6 in Flash’s range becomes a full house and crushes me; Flash’s line (button open, check on flop, call my 350 turn) suggests a lot of Kx/medium pairs/possible slowplayed 55 but not a strong weight toward Ax/6x — still, those combos exist and the river massively increases their value. Betting for thin value risks getting raised by a full house and putting a large portion of either stack at risk; checking controls the pot and lets Flash either show or bluff. Plan: check and take showdown if he checks; if he fires a small river bet (I’d estimate <~half pot) I call to get value from Kx and worse; if he bets large or jams I fold to the range that now beats me. Pot control and avoiding a tough river decision is the highest-EV line here.

The board has double-paired with Aces and Sixes (Ad 6h 5s Ah 6d). My best five-card hand is now two pair, Aces and Sixes, with a King kicker (A-A-6-6-K). When Minni checks the river after leading the turn, she is likely giving up on a bluff or holding a weak hand like a 5 or a small pocket pair. Since the board's fifth card is a 5, any pocket pair below 6 or a 5 in her hand results in a lower kicker than my King. I have significant showdown value here and there is very little value in betting, as I likely won't be called by worse hands and better hands (an Ace, a Six, or a pocket pair like 77-KK) will not fold. Checking is the most profitable play.

I’m on the button with 6♥3♠ — a weak offsuit hand, but in a 3-handed pot the button’s positional advantage justifies opening very wide. Pot is 150, call is 100, min-raise 200. I’ll open-raise to 300 (≈3×BB): this size applies pressure and narrows the field while keeping the investment small relative to our 17.7k stack. Plan: fold to a sizeable 3‑bet (6‑3o plays poorly vs 3‑bets); if I get one caller I’ll use position to c‑bet dry boards and control pot size on connected/paired boards. Raising maximizes fold equity and avoids playing a marginal hand passively multiway.

Calling 250 more with 7h8h from the small blind. Getting roughly 36% pot odds, and this hand plays well in multiway pots with straight and flush potential. The cost is small relative to my stack, and Button raises typically contain a wide range. Hoping Flash calls to create a favorable 3-way situation.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten offsuit. Minni opened from the button and Claude called from the small blind. The pot currently has 700 chips and it costs me 200 more to call, giving me 3.5:1 pot odds. KTo is a strong enough hand to defend my blind in a multi-way pot at these odds, despite being out of position for the remainder of the hand.

Checking on the flop with 7h8h on Qs7dJc. I have a pair of sevens and a strong open-ended straight draw (any 9 or 6 makes me a straight = 8 outs, plus backdoor flush potential). This is a good hand with draw potential, but not strong enough to raise in a 3-way pot when the original raiser (Minni) is likely to continue. Checking allows me to see Minni's aggression while controlling pot size and gaining more information from Flash's action. I have ~16 total outs and want to realize equity on the turn while managing my stack pressure (13,950 chips).

I have an open-ended straight draw (needing an Ace or a Nine for a straight) on a board of Q-J-7. With a King and a Ten in my hand, I have 8 outs to improve to a very strong hand. Since Claude (Small Blind) has checked to me and I am out of position relative to the pre-flop raiser (Minni), checking is the standard play to see if Minni bets and how the action develops.

I opened a very wide range on the button preflop, so I still have initiative and a lot of missed hands in my range. The Q♠‑7♦‑J♣ rainbow flop is dry and both opponents checked, which is a good spot to continuation‑bet as a bluff. My 6♥3♠ has virtually no showdown value, so I want to take the pot now rather than give free cards. I’ll make a small, value‑targeted bluff (~1/3 pot) to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. Plan: fold to a sizable raise, and if called re‑evaluate the turn — only continue barreling on very blank turns or if I pick up equity/blockers.

I have 7h8h on Qs7dJc - a pair of sevens with an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: any 9 or 6). I'm getting 5:1 pot odds (300 to call into 1,500 pot), which is excellent for a hand with multiple ways to improve. Flash's check on the flop indicates weakness, and while Minni's 300 raise could represent various holdings, my equity is strong here. With 13,950 chips, the call is low-risk relative to my stack. I want to see the turn while keeping the pot manageable - I'm in position relative to Flash and can make better decisions postflop with my draws. Folding would be unnecessarily tight given my equity.

I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: any Ace or Nine) on a board of Qs-7d-Jc. The pot currently contains 1500 chips and it costs me 300 to call, giving me immediate pot odds of 5:1. Since I need roughly 16.6% equity to call and my 8 outs provide approximately 16% equity on the next card alone (plus implied odds if I hit), this is a mandatory call in a multi-way pot to see the turn.

On the turn with 7h8h on Qs7dJc6c, I have a pair of sevens with 8 clean outs to make a straight (any 5 or 9). The 6c is a blank that doesn't drastically change the dynamic. I'm first to act in a 3-way pot. My pair is decent but vulnerable, and I need to be cautious given my modest stack of 13,650. Checking allows me to: (1) gather information about Minni's holdings by seeing if he continues aggression, (2) avoid putting in chips with a potentially dominated hand, and (3) keep the pot manageable while I still have equity with my pair + draw. If Minni bets, I can make a disciplined fold or call decision depending on bet size. If Minni checks behind, I gain additional information and might get to showdown or take the pot on the river. This is more prudent than betting and potentially building a large pot when I may not be ahead.

I currently have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs for an Ace or a Nine) on a board of Qs-7d-Jc-6c. I checked and called a small bet on the flop. Now on the turn, Claude has checked again. Since I am out of position and playing against the original pre-flop aggressor (Minni) who is still to act behind me, checking is the standard play to see how the action develops and to potentially realize my equity if I get a good price or a free card.

I opened wide pre and c‑bet the Q♠‑7♦‑J♣ flop and got two callers. The 6♣ on the turn pairs my 6 (I now have bottom pair with a 3 kicker), but this is still a very marginal holding in a 3‑way pot. Both opponents called the flop, so their ranges contain a lot of Qx/Jx, middle pairs and some draws — hands that will rarely fold to a turn stab and often outkick me at showdown. The pot is 1,800 and stacks are deep, so building the pot with a weak pair is unnecessarily risky. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity; I can call reasonable river bets (beat floats/bluffs) and fold to large raises or overbets that represent Q/J/overpairs. If everyone checks through I get to showdown cheaply. Checking preserves flexibility and avoids committing with a marginal hand.

River decision with 7h8h on Qs7dJc6c4d. I have a pair of sevens, which is a marginal hand on this runout. The 4d is a complete blank that doesn't improve my holding.

Action analysis:
- Minni showed strength preflop and on the flop (300 raise), then checked the turn (possibly weak or slowplay)
- Flash has been passive throughout, suggesting a weak holding
- I'm first to act in an out-of-position situation

My hand strength: A pair of sevens is vulnerable on this board. Minni likely has QQ, JJ, QJ, AQ, or KQ that beats me. Flash's passivity suggests he may have missed a draw or has a marginal hand himself.

Betting options are poor:
- A value bet makes no sense since I'm not beating the hands that call
- A bluff bet is ineffective since Minni showed clear strength and will likely call with most holdings
- I'm out of position with no pot control

Checking is the correct play:
- Allows showdown with a marginal hand
- Lets me realize equity against Flash's likely weaker holdings
- Avoids putting in chips with a hand that's likely dominated by Minni
- If Minni bets, I can make a disciplined fold knowing he likely has me beat
- My stack (13,650) can handle folding here without significant damage

This is a prudent, low-risk play that controls variance on the river with a marginal hand.

On the river, the board is Qs-7d-Jc-6c-4d. I have King-high and missed my open-ended straight draw. Claude has checked to me, and Minni (the pre-flop aggressor) is still left to act behind me. In a three-way pot, King-high is unlikely to be the best hand at showdown, but it does have some marginal showdown value against missed lower straight draws like 98 or 85. Betting as a bluff into two opponents is risky here, as Claude's check-call on the flop suggests a marginal made hand he might not want to fold, and Minni could still have a pair. I will check and hope for a cheap showdown or a check-around to see if my King-high can miraculously win.

Board: Q♠-7♦-J♣-6♣-4♦. I hold 6♥3♠ — bottom pair with a weak kicker. I opened extremely wide preflop and c‑bet the flop, but both opponents called, so their calling ranges contain a lot of Qx/Jx and medium pairs that beat me, plus some floats. The turn paired my 6 but that doesn’t meaningfully improve my relative strength vs two callers; the river 4♦ is a blank. With two callers the fold equity of a river bluff is low and, given my poor kicker and marginal showdown value, building the pot here is high risk for limited upside. Best play is to check and realize equity — I’ll only call reasonable river bets (small probes) but fold to sizable aggression that represents a Q/J or a better pair. Therefore: check and go to showdown cheaply.

Calling 100 with J9s from the button in 3-handed play. This hand has excellent postflop playability and position equity. The pot odds (1.5:1) are favorable for a speculative hand with high card potential and wheel draw possibilities. Position advantage allows me to control the pot postflop and outplay opponents on subsequent streets.

I have 7d 5d in the small blind. Claude limped on the button. I am getting 5:1 odds to complete the bet and see a flop. 7-5 suited has decent post-flop playability. Calling is a low-risk way to potentially hit a strong board or a draw.

5♠4♦ is a weak, unsuited, low connector and this is a 3‑way limp pot (Claude called 100, Flash called 50). I have no reason to build the pot or try to isolate out of position — raising here would be thin and often just get called, leaving me OOP with poor equity. With 0 to call and a 300 pot, the correct play is to check and see a flop cheaply. Postflop plan: fold to significant aggression on dry high‑card boards, continue only with a pair or strong draw, and use pot control given my marginal hand and multi‑way action.

I have flopped a flush draw with 7d 5d on an Ad Kd 6d board. This is a very strong draw, though there are two overcards that could give others a higher flush. Given that the pot is small (300) and I'm first to act, I will check to see how the other players react. If there's a bet, I have the direct odds to call or potentially check-raise to represent a made hand. Checking is a standard way to play draws in multi-way pots to control the pot size and see another card.

Flop is A♦ K♦ 6♦ and I hold 5♠4♦. This is a 3‑way limp pot and I have only a single diamond and no pair — at best a one‑card flush draw (≈35% to hit by the river) and two weak pair outs. The board is very wet for diamonds and multi‑way increases the chance someone already has or is drawing to a better diamond combination (or even flopped a made flush with two diamonds in hand). Out of position versus both opponents, I don’t want to build the pot with such a marginal holding. I’ll check to control the pot, see how the others act, and only continue if I get correct pot odds to chase or improve on the turn (or if the turn pairs me or gives me a stronger draw). If I face significant aggression I’ll fold.

Position continuation bet on coordinated board where both opponents checked. J9s has outs (Q for straight) and fold equity is strong. Bet sizing of 175 is reasonable given the 300 pot size and need to take down weak holdings or build the pot with equity advantage.

I have flopped a flush draw with the 7 of diamonds and 5 of diamonds on an Ace-King-6 all-diamond board. Claude has bet 175 into a pot of 300 (roughly 58% pot). I need to call 175 to see the turn, with a pot that will then be 650. My immediate pot odds are about 27%, while my chance of hitting a diamond on the turn is roughly 19%. However, given the implied odds and the fact that I am closing some of the action (depending on Minni), a call is justified here. I must be cautious if a diamond hits, as my flush is only 7-high and could be beaten by the Queen, Jack, or Ten of diamonds, but I cannot fold a flush draw for this price in a limped pot.

Board A♦ K♦ 6♦; I hold 5♠4♦ — one diamond and no pair. Claude raises to 175 into a 650 pot and Flash calls, so two players have shown interest on a three‑diamond flop. Raw odds to hit a diamond by the river are ~35% (≈51% to hit a pair or diamond by river), but much of that equity is thin: any opponent with two diamonds already has a made flush, and opponents with a single diamond will generally have a higher‑card diamond than my 4♦, so my potential flush will often be second‑best. To call 175 into a 825‑chip pot I need ≈21% equity to breakeven — realistic equity vs a betting/calling range here (A/K, strong diamonds, big pairs) is well below that. Raising as a bluff OOP into two callers is too risky. Conclusion: preserve stack and fold rather than chase a dominated draw in a multiway pot.

The turn card is the 6 of clubs, which pairs the board. I still have a 7-high flush draw on an Ace-King-6-6 board. The pairing of the 6 makes my draw slightly less valuable as full houses are now possible, and my potential flush is already very low. Since I am out of position and the previous aggressor (Claude) is still in the hand, I will check to see how he reacts. I want to see the river as cheaply as possible to potentially hit my flush, but I must be extremely cautious given the strength of the board.

Checking the turn with J9s on Ad Kd 6d 6c. I have an OESD (Q or 8 for straight) with 8-12 outs. Flash's check indicates weakness, so taking a free river card is the mathematically optimal play here. With two-card equity and a weak opponent, checking preserves equity and avoids unnecessary investment while still having drawing potential. The river will define the hand more clearly.

The river brings a fourth diamond to the board (Ad Kd 6d Qd) and also leaves the board paired with sixes. My hand is now a 7-high flush (using the Ad, Kd, Qd, 7d, and 6d). While I have a flush, it is very low. Any opponent holding the Jd or Td has a higher flush, and the paired board makes full houses a possibility (though unlikely given the action). Claude limped pre-flop and checked the turn, which suggests he might not have a powerhouse, but betting here is risky as I'm unlikely to get called by worse and could be raised by better. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown.

Flash checked the river after a weak line (called flop, checked turn/river), indicating he's taking a hand to showdown. With only Jack-high and no made hand, I have no showdown value. Attempting a bluff here is unnecessary and risks chips. Checking takes a free showdown and minimizes losses on a weak holding. Position advantage is outweighed by my hand's lack of showdown equity.

In the button position with 10h 9c, I have a solid connector. The pot is currently 150 (small blind 50, big blind 100). Opening with a standard raise to 300 is a good way to exert pressure from the button and potentially take down the pot or play a pot in position. My stack of 7825 is smaller than my opponents, but this hand is strong enough to open for 3x.

Qc3h is a very weak offsuit queen and is frequently dominated by a button opening range. Calling 250 into a 450 pot requires roughly 27% equity; Q3o has well under that versus a standard BTN open and plays poorly out of position (and Claude in the BB can still call or 3-bet). 3-betting to isolate with this hand is irresponsible — it has poor fold equity and terrible postflop playability. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left to play, preserving chips and folding is the highest EV line.

Folding 3-4 offsuit from Big Blind. This is one of the worst hands in poker - dominated against nearly any raising range. Out of position against an aggressive opponent (Flash) makes this even worse. Even with decent pot odds, hand equity is insufficient. Clear fold.

I’m on the button in a 3-handed pot with K♠3♥ — a marginal hand but position is very strong. Blinds are 50/100 (pot 150, to-call 100) and there’s been no raise, so opening is standard. I want to take the initiative and thin the field while keeping the pot manageable, so I’ll open to 3x the BB (300 total). If either opponent 3-bets I’ll respect the strength of that action and fold K3o (it’s easily dominated); if I get heads-up or see a favorable flop I’ll c-bet when appropriate and play for value or fold to heavy resistance.

Folding 7-8 offsuit from small blind against Minni's button raise. This hand has poor playability out of position and doesn't warrant calling 250 chips, especially with Flash still to act in the big blind. The weak hand strength combined with positional disadvantage makes this a straightforward fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Js 6s. Minni (Button) has raised to 300. This is a standard button open. While Js 6s is a suited hand with some playability, I am out of position against a very large stack. Looking at previous hands, Minni has been quite active. However, calling 200 more into a 450 pot gives me decent odds, but with a marginal suited hand out of position against a big stack, folding or occasionally defending is standard. Given the need to protect my stack and the quality of the hand, I will fold this time to wait for a better spot.

Raising to 300 from the button with Tc Qd. This is a standard 3x raise that takes advantage of my positional edge and hand quality. The raise shows strength, may win the blinds immediately, and gives me good postflop position if called. Against short-stacked Flash and chip-leader Minni, this is aggressive but reasonable button play.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten of Hearts and Deuce of Clubs, which is a very weak hand. Claude has raised to 300 from the Button. Given that I am out of position and my hand has very little playability or potential against a raising range, there is no reason to call or bluff here. Folding is the most prudent move to preserve my stack.

Claude opened to 300 from the button; Flash folded so it’s heads-up. I have A♠J♠ in the big blind — a strong, suited ace that blocks a lot of the button’s strongest aces and plays very well postflop. With deep stacks (~170bb) I prefer to take the initiative and isolate the opener rather than just calling out of position. A standard 3‑bet vs a 300 open is about 900–1000 total; I’ll raise to 900 total (3x his open). This size gives solid fold equity, builds pots when I’m ahead, and keeps dominated hands in his calling range. Plan: if he calls I’ll proceed with standard c-bet lines; if he 4-bets large I’ll fold to avoid getting in a dominated spot against a tight 4-bet range.

Calling Minni's 3-bet with Tc Qd. The button position is crucial here - I'll act last postflop and can apply pressure or fold based on board texture and Minni's actions. My hand has decent equity against a range that includes both premium hands and lighter 3-bets. The 3:1 pot odds justify the call. This preserves my stack while maintaining optionality, and I can outplay Minni postflop with positional advantage.

I 3‑bet preflop with A♠J♠ to isolate Claude and we ended up heads‑up. Flop A♦ 8♠ 3♣ gives me top pair with the best kicker and a backdoor spade possibility. This is a very good spot to C‑bet: the board is fairly dry (no straight draws, only one spade), Claude’s calling range to my 3‑bet will include worse A‑x, pocket pairs and broadways that I want to charge, and a half‑pot size keeps SPR reasonable for later streets. I’ll bet about half the pot (~900) to get value from worse aces and medium pairs, deny equity to any overcards, and fold out hands with some equity but no showdown value. If he raises I’ll re‑evaluate (he could have a set or strong two‑pair), but the default plan is to build the pot and continue on most turn cards.

Folding Tc Qd on the 3c 8s Ad flop. My hand is Queen-high with minimal equity against Minni's strong 3-bet and flop-raise line (likely AA, AK, AQ). The Ace on the flop is disastrous for my range, and calling will likely result in further bets on the turn where I'm typically behind. At 14,125 chips, preserving stack for better spots is the winning approach. The 600 already invested is a sunk cost.

I am on the button with pocket nines, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I will make a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand with positional advantage.

I’m in the small blind with 3h3c. Flash (button) opened to 300; pot is 450 and I must put in 250 more (300 total). With deep stacks (I have ~180bb, button ~75bb) this is a standard set‑mining spot — a small pocket pair has strong implied odds vs a single raiser. 3‑betting OOP with a low pair is unattractive: little value when called, risk of a 4‑bet, and I’ll still be out of position post‑flop. So I’ll flat‑call to keep the pot manageable and preserve the chance to hit a set; if I hit a set or a favorable draw I’ll extract value, otherwise I’ll proceed cautiously or fold to heavy action on adverse boards.

Calling with QJ from the big blind. I'm getting excellent pot odds (3.5:1) with two opponents already invested in the pot. QJ is a premium broadway hand that can make strong top pairs, straights, or dominate if I hit an overcard. The call allows me to see a flop in position (acting last) with a healthy pot already built. With my slightly above-average stack position, this is a low-risk, high-potential continuation. Flash has a shorter stack (7,525), making him more vulnerable, and Minni is the chip leader. Calling preserves flexibility and allows me to play well post-flop.

I flat-called preflop with 3♣3♥ as a set-mining spot. The flop J♣ 6♣ T♦ completely missed me — I only have an underpair and one club (no real draw). This is a multiway pot (button open + BB caller) and I'm out of position, so leading as a bluff into two wide ranges is a poor play; they have many J/T combos and club draws that will call or raise. I will check to control the pot and preserve my deep stack. Plan: give up to heavy aggression, call a very small probe (<= ~1/3 pot) to realize my ~8.4% chance to hit a set by the river, and if I do hit a 3 later extract value; otherwise fold to large bets/raises.

I have QS JH on a board of JC 6C TD, holding top pair with a solid kicker. After Minni checked the flop, I'm in an excellent position to bet. This flop isn't particularly threatening, and my pair of Jacks is likely the strongest hand at this moment. I should bet for value to:

1. Build the pot and extract value from weaker pairs or draws (any hand with Ace-X, King-X, or smaller paired hands)
2. Price out flush draws (there are two clubs on the board)
3. Control the action and dictate the narrative going forward
4. Keep Flash honest—he raised pre-flop from the button and still needs to act

A bet of 450 (approximately 50% of the current pot) is well-balanced. It's aggressive enough to build value and exert pressure, but not so large that it commits me too heavily to the hand. With my stack of 13,825 against weaker stacks (Minni at 17,750 and Flash at 7,525), I want to build a pot while maintaining flexibility for future streets. This sizing also allows me to fold to aggressive resistance without significant loss relative to my stack.

On a board of Jc-Tc-6c, my pocket nines are significantly weakened as they are now third pair and there are many straight and flush draws available. Claude, leading out for half the pot into two players after the pre-flop raiser (me) has yet to act, shows significant strength or a very strong draw. My hand lacks a direct path to improvement besides hitting a set, and being caught between Claude and Minni (who has yet to act after the lead) is a precarious position. I will fold to preserve my stack and look for better spots.

I checked the flop with 3♣3♥ to control the pot and realize my set-improving equity. Claude bets 450 into the current 900 pre-bet pot (pot now 1350) — I must call 450. That 450 call gives me ~25% pot odds (450 to win 1800), but my naked equity to improve to a set by the river is only ~8.4% (plus a tiny backdoor club flush chance). Claude’s c-bet line is consistent with Jx/Tx/pocket pairs or club draws that dominate my hand, and this bet is larger than the “small probe (≤1/3 pot)” I was willing to call. With such poor immediate equity, OOP and likely further pressure on later streets, calling is a losing long-term play despite deep stacks. I fold and preserve my deep stack for better set-mining or postflop spots.

I’m on the button with pocket fives (5♠5♦), deep stacks (~177 bb). The pot is 150 and calling 100 is workable, but raising to seize initiative and isolate the blinds is standard and maximizes implied odds for set-mining. I’ll open to a normal 3x-ish size (300): big enough to get value and discourage limp-calls/min-raises but not overcommit. Plan: proceed postflop in position if called; call small 3-bets for set-mining but fold to large, committed 3-bets (e.g. >~3x my raise or very large sizing) since 55 plays poorly against heavy preflop aggression without a set.

King-Ten offsuit from the small blind facing a button raise is a solid 3-bet candidate. Minni's raise to 300 is a standard steal attempt - she's been quite aggressive throughout the session. By 3-betting to 850 (roughly 2.8x her raise), I accomplish several things: (1) I leverage position and fold equity, taking down the pot immediately a meaningful percentage of the time; (2) If called, I have a decent hand with two overcards and strong playability postflop; (3) This is an appropriate spot to play wider from the small blind against a button raise. Flash's short stack (7,425) is unlikely to 4-bet here, so the decision primarily rests with Minni. The math favors an aggressive 3-bet in this spot given my table image has been fairly tight and selective.

I am holding 6c 7s in the big blind. Minni opened from the button and Claude 3-bet from the small blind. 6-7 offsuit is far too weak to defend against a 3-bet, especially as I would be out of position against two aggressive opponents. Folding is the only sensible play here.

I’m on the button with 5♠5♦ facing a small 3-bet to 850 after my 300 open. With deep stacks this is a classic set-mining spot — Claude’s 3-bet sizing is relatively small (just under my 3x threshold), I have position, and calling 550 is cheap enough given the implied odds if I hit a set. I’ll call to see the flop and re-evaluate; if I hit a set I’ll extract value, and if I miss I’ll proceed cautiously (fold to heavy pressure on dry boards, or continue selectively on favorable textures). I won’t 4-bet here with a medium pocket pair.

I have K-T on a 7h-Th-7c flop, giving me top pair with a strong kicker. This is a favorable holding on this texture. While the paired board presents some risk (opponent could have a seven or be drawing to a set), my K-T is a solid hand that should win immediately a reasonable percentage of the time against Minni's wide calling range. Being out of position on the flop, I prefer to play this proactively rather than passively checking and allowing Minni to control the action with her positional advantage. A bet of 700 (approximately 39% of the pot) establishes the narrative, applies pressure to hands worse than mine (like undercards), and extracts value from medium-strength holdings. This sizing is appropriately sized given the stacks and is consistent with my image as a tight, selective 3-bettor—Minni will respect this bet.

I have 5♠5♦ on a 7♥ T♥ 7♣ flop after calling Claude’s 3‑bet preflop. I missed my set and the board is paired with two hearts — not a great texture for an underpair. Claude’s line (3‑bet preflop, then a small c‑bet 700 into a 2500 pot) is consistent with a mixed range: some strong value (77, TT, overpairs) but also lots of high‑card/float combos and heart draws. Raising as a bluff here is too risky — I’ll be building a bigger pot with almost no showdown equity vs his value hands. Folding immediately throws away my set‑mining equity, but calling is relatively cheap and keeps the pot controllable with position.

Concrete math and plan: calling 700 into a 2500 pot requires ~22% equity to justify a pure call; my raw chance to hit a 5 by river is only ~8.3%, but I also currently beat many missed overcard combos (AK/AQ/KQ) and can realize implied odds if I hit a set. With deep stacks and a small c‑bet, the correct line is to call the flop, see one more card, and fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve. If I hit a 5 on the turn I will extract value; if the turn bricks and Claude barrels large (especially >~50% pot) I’ll fold; against smaller turn bets or checks I can continue to realize equity and potentially get to a cheap showdown.

Turn card (9s) is safe and doesn't complete most draws. With K-T top pair/best kicker on a paired board, I should continue applying pressure through value betting. Minni's call patterns (pre-flop 3-bet call, flop call) suggest a medium-strength holding or draw rather than a monster. Betting 1,100 (roughly 34% of current pot) maintains my aggressive image while extracting value from her range. This is more effective than checking and giving her positional control on the river.

I have 5♠5♦ on a 7♥ T♥ 7♣ 9♠ board. Pot is 4300 and Claude has bet 1100 — a very small turn bet after a preflop 3‑bet and a small flop c‑bet. Calling 1100 requires ~1100/(4300+1100)=≈20.4% equity to break even. I only have 2 outs to make a set on the river (2/46 ≈4.3%), so my direct improvement equity is small, but Claude’s line and tiny bet sizing are consistent with a mixed range that includes both thin value (77, TT, 99, some overpairs) and a lot of air/float combos. Raising as a bluff is spewy here — an underpair has poor playability if called and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position against many hands that’ll continue. With position and a cheap turn call I can realize my (small) equity, see the river, and fold to large aggression if I miss. Plan: call 1100, fold to sizable river shove/big bet unless I hit a 5 or get clear signs of a bluff; if river is checked or bet small I can reevaluate for a thin call or showdown.

River value bet with top pair/top kicker (K-T) on a paired, dry board. Minni's call patterns (3-bet pre-flop, flop call, turn call) suggest a medium-strength holding that will call a final value bet. The 4♦ is a blank that favors my range. Betting 1,600 (30% of the 5,400 pot) extracts value from worse pairs while remaining appropriately sized for my stack. This continues my aggressive narrative while maintaining strong hand hierarchy.

Board: 7♥ T♥ 7♣ 9♠ 4♦. I’m on the button with 5♠5♦. Pot = 7000, to call = 1600, so I need ~1600/(7000+1600) ≈ 18.6% equity to justify a call. I have no river outs — I only win if Claude is bluffing or value-betting worse. Claude 3‑bet preflop and has used small, consistent bet sizes on flop and turn, which is consistent with a line containing a lot of strong value (77, TT, overpairs, 99, etc.) but also with thin value/float combos and missed heart draws that can bluff or bet small on the river. Raising as a bluff is too risky — I have poor blockers and will be called by the hands that beat me. Folding surrenders the pot when the required bluff frequency is relatively low and the call is small relative to the pot and my stack. Given my earlier plan to call small river bets to catch bluffs and realize equity, and given the cheap price (≈18.6% required), I will call to see showdown and avoid bloating the pot with a bluff raise.

7-2 offsuit is the worst hand in poker. Despite being on the button with excellent position, this hand has virtually no equity and will lose money if played. Even with positional advantage, the hand is too weak to justify a call or raise against two opponents who have demonstrated active play. Folding is the mathematically correct decision here. With 70 hands remaining and a solid 19,075 chip stack, there's no need to take marginal spots with dominated holdings.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 5d. This is a weak hand and I am out of position. The Pot is small and I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, but Minni in the Big Blind is an aggressive player. Folding is the safest and most standard play to conserve chips for better spots.

I am on the button with 6c 5d. This is a very weak hand in a 3-handed game. While the button offers positional advantage, this hand is not strong enough to merit a raise, and calling the blind is generally suboptimal as it invites raises from the blinds. Folding is the standard disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

4♦5♠ is a weak, unsuited hand and I'm out of position. It only costs 50 more to see a flop (pot 150 → 200) so I’ll take the cheap equity rather than inflate the pot by raising and committing myself OOP against Claude, who can call/3‑bet and put me in difficult spots. Plan: see the flop; continue only with a real piece (pair, two pair, good draw) and fold to sizable aggression otherwise. Call 50 more (total committed 100).

With T-Q offsuit in the big blind facing Minni's call of the small blind (Flash folded), this is a good spot to check and see a flop. T-Q is a marginal hand that plays better in position post-flop and doesn't warrant aggressive action here. The pot is already 200 with just two players remaining. By checking, I get to see a flop cheaply and can reevaluate based on how the board develops. Minni's passive call from the small blind suggests a weaker holding, so I shouldn't need to apply unnecessary pressure.

I have 5♠4♦ on 3♣ 3♠ 5♣ — second pair (pair of fives) with a weak 4 kicker. Claude checked preflop so his range is wide (lots of overcards, small pairs, some suited holdings); the board is paired (two 3s) so trips are possible but relatively uncommon. This is a dry flop with some two‑club combos that can have a flush draw, so I want to charge draws and get value from worse pairs/overcards while denying free cards. I'll make a half‑pot value/protection bet of 100; if Claude check‑raises large I can fold (he likely has a 3 or a very strong hand), if he calls I’ll reassess on the turn.

This is a marginal spot with T♠-Q♥ on a paired flop (3♣-3♠-5♣). I have no made hand, no flush draw, and only queen-high at best with two overcards. While Minni's 100 raise from the small blind could be a continuation bet, the board texture is unfavorable for my hand - paired boards often favor stronger holdings. To profitably call 100 to win 300, I'd need approximately 25% equity, but my hand likely has less given the board composition. Even accounting for Minni potentially cbetting light, the risk-reward doesn't justify continuing. T-Q plays better in position post-flop, not out of position facing aggression on a dry, paired board. Folding preserves my stack for stronger opportunities.

I'm on the Button with Q♠4♥ in a 3-handed pot. This is a marginal hand, but Button position gives strong stealing opportunity against the blinds. Pot is 150 and the cost to see a flop is only 100, with a reasonable min-raise size available. I want to apply pressure and pick up the blinds rather than limp and allow callers; a raise to 300 total is a standard, credible isolation/steal size here that preserves stack depth while giving good fold equity. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (Q4o is usually dominated), but if called I'll proceed cautiously postflop — c-bet on dry, favorable flops to continue the steal; check or give up on coordinated, hostile boards. Overall this raise maximizes long-term EV from position while limiting tough postflop decisions with a weak holding.

Folding Td8s from the small blind against Minni's button raise. This is a weak, gapped connector with poor playability from out of position. Even at 1.8:1 pot odds, the combination of weak hand strength, positional disadvantage, and my healthy chip stack makes this a clear fold. I'll wait for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Seven offsuit. Minni opened with a raise to 300 from the Button, and Claude folded. While the pot odds are roughly 2.25 to 1, Q7o is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raise in a three-handed game. My goal is to maximize my stack over 150 hands, and defending with such a weak hand here likely has a negative expected value.

Button position with Ad9c is a strong spot to apply pressure. A standard 3x BB raise to 300 takes the initiative, likely wins blinds against weak holdings, and builds a pot when ahead. Flash's short stack (7,225) makes him vulnerable. This aggressive button play maximizes equity and controls the action from the best position.

I am in the Small Blind with T7o, which is a weak hand. The Button (Claude) has raised to 3x the big blind. Calling out of position with a weak hand against an aggressive player is generally a losing play, and folding is the standard move here to preserve my stack.

Fold. 5♣7♦ offsuit is a very weak, unplayable hand out of position versus a button open to 3bb. I’d have to call 200 into a 450 pot (need ~30.8% equity) and 5‑7o has well under that against a typical BTN raising range, with poor post‑flop playability and low realized equity OOP. 3‑betting as a bluff is also unattractive — I have no meaningful blockers and risk getting isolated or 4‑bet. With deep stacks and plenty of hands to choose from, I’ll conserve chips and wait for a much better spot.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand, even in a three-handed game. With both blinds having larger stacks and being relatively active, there is no reason to open this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I’m in the small blind with 3♦4♥ — a very weak, unsuited hand out of position. However, facing only the big blind and with the button folded, calling an extra 50 to see the flop is cheap and standard — pot is 150 so I get roughly 3:1 pot odds to call. Raising is pointless with this holding (no playability, no fold equity vs a deep-stacked, capable opponent). Plan: call to see a flop, fold to significant aggression if I don’t connect, and only continue if I hit a meaningful piece or get a favorable board/line.

I'm in the big blind with 6s Td, a weak hand with no immediate strength. Minni called from the small blind rather than raising, suggesting a weak holding. With this marginal hand, I should check and take a flop to see if I can hit something before committing more chips. Checking keeps the pot small and allows me to exercise my post-flop positional advantage (I'll act first post-flop). A raise here with 6-10 offsuit would be speculatively aggressive and likely create a larger pot when I'm not in a good spot. The conservative line is appropriate given my hand strength and stack depths.

I completely missed this flop with 3♦4♥: no pair, only one heart so no backdoor flush, and only remote runner-runner straight possibilities. I’m out of position to Claude (BB) and the board 7-7-Q heavily favors his continuing range (7x, Qx, overpairs, most pairs). Betting as a bluff here is low EV — Claude calls or raises too often and I have almost no fold equity. Best line is to check and fold to any significant bet, preserving chips and seeing a free turn if he checks back to reassess.

Checking with 6-10 offsuit on a 7-7-Q flop. My hand has no made strength and limited equity. Minni checked from the small blind, indicating weakness. Betting would only build the pot with a marginal hand. By checking, I get a free card to potentially improve on the turn while keeping the pot size manageable. This conservative line is appropriate given my hand weakness and the need to avoid marginal confrontations.

I have 3♦4♥ on 7♣7♠Q♥A♦ — no pair and no real draws. I'm out of position vs Claude (BB) and this board strongly favors his continuing range (7x, Qx, Ax, overpairs). Betting as a bluff here has very little fold equity against a deep, competent opponent and risks giving up chips with no equity. The standard, highest-EV line is to check and fold to any meaningful bet; if Claude checks back I can reevaluate the river for a possible bluff or a shove if the card is extremely favorable. So I check and conserve my stack for better spots.

Checking on the turn with 6-10 offsuit on a 7-7-Q-A board. My hand has zero equity and no outs. Minni has shown extreme weakness with back-to-back checks, but bluffing here is unnecessary variance - if called, I'm almost certain to lose. Checking gives me a free river card and keeps the pot small with a marginal hand. No reasonable river card will help my hand significantly, so I'm just seeing if I get lucky while minimizing risk.

River: I have 3♦4♥ on 7♣7♠ Q♥ A♦ T♣ — I missed completely: no pair, no real showdown value, no backdoor draws. Claude (BB) checked both flop and turn, which suggests either a missed hand or a plan to call down with any pair (Qx/Tx/Ax) or a slow-played strong hand. The pot is tiny (200) relative to our stack; a bluff here would require at least 100 and would be called too often by the hands that beat me. With essentially zero fold equity and a lot to lose, the highest EV line is to concede this pot: check and fold to any bet. Action: check now and preserve chips for better spots.

River showdown with a pair of tens (Td on Tc). Minni has checked the entire hand (flop, turn, river), demonstrating extreme weakness. While I have a made hand, my pair of tens is marginal and vulnerable. Betting would likely only be called by stronger hands or result in Minni folding weaker holdings that would have called a check anyway. Checking down locks in the win with my pair and avoids unnecessary variance. This is a value protection play against an opponent who has shown consistent weakness.

Three-handed on the button I should be opening a wide range — T7o is marginal but very playable in position. Raising takes the initiative, gives fold equity vs the blinds, and lets me play postflop in position rather than defending passively. Standard sizing here is ~3x the big blind; I raise to 300 to apply pressure while keeping 3-bet pot sizes manageable. If I face a large 3-bet I'll fold and preserve my stack; if called I'll C-bet favorable textures and use position to extract value or fold when behind.

K4o from small blind is a clear fold. Although I'm getting 1.8:1 odds to call (250 to win 450), this hand has poor playability out of position. Minni raised from the button, and the big blind (Flash) hasn't acted yet, meaning I could face further pressure. K4o plays badly out of position in multi-way pots and lacks strong equity against Minni's raising range. Folding weak hands from out of position is a fundamental principle, and this is a textbook example. I have a 19,125 chip stack, so I can afford to be selective and wait for better opportunities.

In the Big Blind with 8h 2h, I'm facing a 3x open from Minni on the Button. While 82s is a suited hand, it is incredibly weak, even against a standard Button opening range. Given the relatively high open size (300) and the fact that I'm out of position for the rest of the hand, I should simply fold. My stack is over 70 big blinds, so there's no need to defend such a marginal hand.

I have Q7s on the button, which is a weak hand despite the positional advantage. While I do have the best seat at the table, Q7o (queen with poor kicker) lacks the strength needed to enter the pot, even against reduced stacks. Flash is short at 7,075 chips, but that doesn't justify playing marginal hands. With 19,125 chips and 64 hands remaining, I should maintain discipline and wait for stronger holdings. The risk-reward of trying to steal blinds with a hand that plays poorly post-flop is not favorable. Folding preserves my stack and keeps me in position for future profitable spots.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 4s. This is a very weak hand. Claude has folded on the button. Minni is in the Big Blind and has a healthy stack. While I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, 9-4 offsuit plays poorly out of position. Given the stack depths and the fact that I want to minimize losses with marginal hands to save chips for better spots, folding is the standard and safest play here.

I am on the button with 7h6h, a very strong speculative hand in a 3-handed game. My stack is around 70 big blinds. I will open for a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand and capitalize on the playability of my suited connectors.

3♦8♥ is a very weak, unplayable hand in the small blind facing a standard BTN open to 300. I’ve already posted 50 and would have to call 250 more; that yields poor pot odds (~2.8:1, needing ~26% equity) and 3♦8♥ has far less equity vs a BTN opening range. If I continue I’ll be out of position postflop and vulnerable to isolation or multiway pots. A 3-bet bluff is unattractive here (no meaningful blockers, BTN and BB likely to call or shove). Preserve the stack and fold.

I'm in the big blind facing a button raise from Flash. With Q-2 offsuit (one of the weakest hands in poker), this is a clear fold despite favorable pot odds. Q-2o lacks both straight and flush potential and is easily dominated by most raising ranges. Even though Flash has a shorter stack (6,775) and is on the button, Q-2o is too weak to defend profitably. I have a substantial chip lead (19,025 vs 6,775), so I don't need to play marginal hands to build my stack. Folding preserves my equity and allows me to win chips in better spots.

Q♠2♥ is a very weak, often dominated hand, but I'm on the button with both blinds to act and the price to see a flop is cheap (pot 150, call 100). Raising to 200 as a light steal is an option, but both opponents have shown willingness to defend and 3‑bet; I don't want to inflate the pot or face a shove/3‑bet out of position with Q2o. I’ll call 100 to see a flop in position, fold to strong preflop aggression, and otherwise play postflop cautiously — look to realize equity if I hit a Queen or good texture, otherwise check/fold to sizable bets.

I'm in the Small Blind with Jack-Four offsuit against two opponents in a three-handed game. The pot is already 250, and I'm getting 5:1 odds to complete my blind with only 50 more to call. While J4o is a weak hand, the pot odds are favorable, my stack is healthy (18,975), and in three-handed play ranges are wider. I can see the flop cheaply and make a post-flop decision based on the board texture and action. Folding here would be unnecessarily tight given the favorable price and stack dynamics.

I am in the Big Blind holding AJ offsuit. Minni (Button) has limped and Claude (Small Blind) has completed the blind. AJ is a very strong hand in a three-way limped pot, significantly ahead of the average range of a limper and a small blind completion. I want to raise for value and to narrow the field. Raising to 400 total (an additional 300) is a standard size to punish limpers and build a pot with a superior hand.

I called the 100 originally to see a flop in position, but Q♠2♥ is a very weak, often-dominated hand. Flash’s raise to 400 turns this into a spot where I’d need ~33% equity (call 300 into a 600 pot) to break even, and with the SB still to act I’m likely to be multi‑way and out of position postflop. Three‑betting as a bluff here is too risky against Flash’s stacks and tendencies. Consistent with my preflop plan to fold to strong aggression, I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

Folding J4o to Flash's raise. While I was justified calling to complete the small blind at 5:1 odds, Flash's raise to 400 significantly changes the situation. J4o is too weak to call out of position against an aggressive raise. My equity against Flash's range is approximately 20-30%, which is insufficient for 3:1 pot odds. In three-handed play, profitable plays require either better hand quality, position advantage, or significantly better odds. This hand meets none of those criteria, so folding is the optimal play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

7-3 is one of the weakest hands in poker (seven-high with poor kicker). Despite being in button position (normally the strongest pre-flop position), this hand has virtually no value and poor equity against any reasonable opponent range. Flash is short-stacked (7,375) and Minni has adequate chips (13,550). At 89 of 150 hands, I should be selective with my hands and preserve chips for better opportunities. Folding weak hands in position is still the fundamentally correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with 2c 4h, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Claude on the Button has folded, leaving only me and Minni in the Big Blind. While I only need to put in 50 more chips to see a flop, 24o plays very poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Minni has shown a willingness to be aggressive throughout the session. There is no strategic reason to commit more chips to this hand given its extremely low equity and my positional disadvantage.

I am on the button with Q3o, a very weak hand even in a three-handed game. While the button is the best position to steal blinds, Q3o lacks post-flop playability and strength. Given that both Minni and Claude have shown aggression and are currently the big stacks at the table, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.
