I have 4♥5♠ in the small blind, the button (Flash) folded so it’s heads-up vs Claude in the big blind. The pot is 150 and I need to put in 50 more. 4-5o is a marginal but playable hand heads-up — calling 50 to win 150 gives ~3:1 pot odds and reasonable equity vs a wide BB defending range. Raising to 200 bloats the pot out of position and invites 3-bets from Claude; folding surrenders 50 for no information. Plan: complete the hand cheaply, see the flop, and proceed with pot control — continue only with strong pairs or good draws, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards. Therefore I’ll call the 50 (total commit 100).

Checking with As3s in the big blind. This is a weak hand that doesn't warrant aggressive play out of position. The small blind called the blinds and I have no additional bet to call, so checking takes a free flop and keeps the pot manageable. I can make better decisions postflop based on how the board connects with my hand and opponent's action.

I completed preflop cheaply and this flop (6♠ 8♣ T♥) misses my 4♥5♠ except for a gutshot to a 7 (4 outs) and a remote backdoor spade. That’s ~8.5% to hit on the turn and ~16.5% by the river. Out of position with such thin equity, I want to keep the pot small and realize my equity rather than bloating the pot with a weak semi-bluff. I’ll check — if Claude fires a small c‑bet I can call for the implied odds and see a turn, but I’ll fold to heavy aggression on this dry board. If I pick up a 7 or a useful spade on the turn I can become more aggressive.

Betting 120 on the flop with As3s. I have Ace-high and a 4-card flush draw (9 outs) which gives me solid equity. Minni's check indicates weakness, and betting here allows me to win the pot immediately or build value with a strong draw. This bet size (60% of the 200 pot) is proportional and not overcommitting. I'll win immediately against very weak hands and have good equity if called given my outs.

Claude leads out for 120 into a 320 pot after I checked. I have 4♥5♠ — I only have a gutshot to a 7 (4 outs) and some backdoor possibilities; by the river the 7-straight is ~16.5% and pairing a 4 or 5 by river is ~24%, so my combined chance to improve is in the high-20s (roughly around the pot‑odds threshold). Calling 120 here gives roughly 2.67:1 pot odds (~27% required) and is cheap relative to both stacks, so I’ll realize equity and see a turn. Raising OOP with such a marginal draw is unnecessary and folding surrenders too much given the price. Plan: call; if the turn is a blank and Claude barrels aggressively I’ll fold, but if I pair or hit a 7 I will proceed more aggressively.

I hold 4♥5♠ on 6♠ 8♣ T♥ 2♠. After calling Claude’s 120 c-bet on the flop the turn is a blank for my hand — I haven’t improved. My only clean outs to a winner on the river are four 7s for the 4-5-6-7-8 straight plus three 4s and three 5s to pair (10 outs total), ~10/46 ≈ 21.7% to improve. I’m out of position and my range after a flop call is very weak, so I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than bluff-launch OOP into a deeper-stacked opponent. Checking keeps the pot manageable and lets me fold to a concrete turn bet; if Claude checks back I get a free river to hit my 10 outs. If he fires a bet on the turn I will only continue to call very small bets — roughly ≤120 chips (because b/(440+b) ≤ ~22% => b ≲ 120) — otherwise I’ll fold. If I do hit a 7 or pair on the river I can take a more aggressive line. For now: check and control the pot.

Checking the turn with As3s on a 6s8cTh2s board. I have approximately 12 outs (9 spades for a flush, 3 aces for a pair), giving me roughly 24% equity. Minni's check on the turn after calling my flop bet suggests weakness. By checking, I can see the river for free while preserving my modest showdown value with ace-high. Out of position, getting a free card with a draw is optimal. If Minni bets the river, I'll have additional information for a final decision.

I have turned a weak but real hand — a pair of 4s (4♥ with 4♣ on the river) on a 6♠ 8♣ T♥ 2♠ 4♣ board. Pot is 440 and Claude (BB) led the flop then checked the turn. His line is consistent with a wide range: missed overcards, medium single-pair hands, or a capped top-pair/minor made hand. Heads‑up, many of those missed hands will call a small river bet rather than fold, so there is thin value to be harvested. Checking gets to showdown for free but also allows hands that beat me (T/8/6) to win without paying; betting small extracts value from A‑high/K‑high/Q‑high and other showdown‑willing hands while keeping cost low if I’m raised. I’ll make a modest value bet (~1/3 pot = 150) to maximize calls from worse hands and keep the pot controllable. If Claude raises, I’ll assume he has a stronger made hand and fold — calling a raise is too thin with second pair. If he calls, I’m happy to go to showdown.

I have As3s on a board of 6s8cTh2s4c, which is ace-high. Minni's action pattern is concerning: she called my flop bet of 120, checked the turn, and now bets 150 on the river. This line strongly suggests a made hand (likely a pair given the board texture) rather than a bluff. My ace-high has very limited equity against hands that fit this betting pattern. I'm getting 1:5 odds (150 to win 740), but my win rate here is almost certainly lower than 20%. Minni's passive turn check followed by river bet indicates either she improved on the river (which is unlikely) or she had a made hand all along and was extracting value. Against a player who bets with reasonable strength, ace-high is a clear fold. Folding preserves my stack for better spots.

I’m on the button with pocket eights (8♠8♣) against only the blinds — a very playable, ahead-of-the-field hand that plays well in position. With a deep stack (~139 BB) I want value and fold equity but also keep the pot manageable against likely 3-bets from Claude or Flash. A standard 3x BB open to 300 accomplishes that: it pressures the blinds, reduces multiway callers, and lets me play postflop in position. If I face a small 3-bet I can continue or call; if I face a large, polarized 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (often folding to heavy aggression to preserve my deep stack). Min-raising to 200 is too small here and invites cheap flops, while a much larger open isn’t necessary. Raise to 300.

K8o is too weak to play out of position against a raise. The hand lacks the necessary equity, I'm in the worst position (small blind), and will be dominated post-flop. This is a standard fold to preserve chips for better situations.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Jack offsuit. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300, and the Small Blind has folded. KJ is well ahead of a typical Button opening range. While I could 3-bet for value or to take down the pot, calling allows me to see a flop and utilize the hand's post-flop playability while keeping the pot size controlled against an aggressive opponent.

On this 10-6-5 rainbow-ish flop, I have King-high. Minni opened pre-flop from the button and I called from the big blind. This board texture hits many of the middle-card combinations and drawing hands that a button opener might have. However, it doesn't contain many overcards to my King or Jack besides the Ten. Checking is the standard play for the big blind after calling a raise pre-flop. I'll check and see how Minni acts. If she bets, I'll evaluate based on the size, as I still have two overcards and some backdoor straight potential.

I have pocket eights (8♠8♣) on a 5♥ T♠ 6♠ flop — a single pair that’s ahead of many of BB’s calling hands but behind tens/sets and vulnerable to two-spade flush draws. Flash checked, so a continuation bet is standard: it extracts value from worse pairs, gutshots and some draws while denying free cards to spade-heavy and overcard parts of his range. I’ll make a half‑pot c‑bet (≈1/2 pot = 325) to balance value and protection. Plan: if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (proceed cautiously on spades or a T); if he check‑raises big I’ll give up most of the time since my hand is only one pair and can be dominated.

On a board of 10-6-5, my King-Jack high still has decent showdown potential against a button opening range that might be continuation-betting with smaller overcards or straight draws. Minni's bet of 325 into a pot of 650 is a half-pot sizing, which is standard. Folding immediately with two overcards and a backdoor straight draw seems too weak, especially since I'm in the big blind and getting decent odds to see a turn card. I will call the bet and evaluate on the next street.

I have improved to top pair (Kings) with a Jack kicker on the turn. Since I am out of position and Minni has been the aggressor throughout the hand (raising pre-flop and betting the flop), checking is the most standard and effective play. This allows Minni to continue bluffing with missed draws like spades or straight draws, and it also prevents me from bloating the pot against a potentially stronger hand like AK, KQ, or a set of Tens, Sixes, or Fives. If she bets, I have a very comfortable call.

I hold pocket eights (8♠8♣) on a 5♥ T♠ 6♠ K♥ board. Flash called my half‑pot c‑bet on the flop, so his calling range likely includes Tx, mid pairs, smaller pairs, and spade/straight draws. The turn K is a scary overcard for my pair but it also looks like a card many of his float/call combos will check. I want to charge spade draws and get value from worse pairs/top pairs that will still call, while denying a free river. A half‑pot turn bet accomplishes that without overcommitting: I can fold to a sizable check‑raise (he’ll often have Kx or sets in that line) but will get paid off by a lot of his weaker holdings and draw combos. Bet 650 into the 1300 pot; if he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate and likely fold.

I have turned top pair with King-Jack offsuit on a board of 10-6-5-K. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor from the button, has continued betting on the turn with a 650 bet into a 1300 pot (1/2 pot). While the board has some draws (spades and various straight possibilities), my hand is strong enough to call. A raise here would likely only be called by hands that beat me (like AK, KQ, or sets) and would force out the bluffs or weaker hands (like a Ten or a draw) that I want to keep in the pot. Calling allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate the river, where I can potentially reach a showdown or catch a bluff.

On the river, the Ace of Diamonds is a significant scare card. As the Big Blind who has been check-calling, I am out of position. The Ace hits a large portion of a Button's opening and continuing range (hands like AQ, AJ, AT, and even air that turned into top pair). My pair of Kings, which was strong on the turn, has now been demoted to second pair. Betting here serves little purpose as I am unlikely to get called by worse hands and will almost certainly be called or raised by better ones. Checking allows me to potentially see a cheap showdown if Minni checks back with a marginal hand or a missed draw, and it gives me the option to evaluate a bet if she chooses to bluff with her missed straight or flush draws.

Board completed A♦ K♥ T♠ 6♠ 5♥. I opened, c‑bet the flop and turned a half‑pot bet — my line represents a medium‑strength made hand or a blocker/denial bet. Flash called flop and turn, which narrows his calling range to lots of Tx, middle pairs (6/5), and missed spade draws. The river A is a very concerning overcard for my pair of eights: it improves many hands that beat me (A‑x, sometimes K‑x turned into a more confident hand) and also makes some Tx hands more cautious. Flash checked the river, which reduces the likelihood he has an Ace (he would often bet for value), but many of his hands that call down (T‑x, 6/5, missed draws) will either check back or call a small bet — and a small bet also opens the door to a check‑raise with any Kx/Ax or a bluff shove that I don't want to face with a single pair.

Given the river texture, my hand strength, and the opponent’s call‑down tendencies, the least exploitative and lowest‑variance play is to check back to showdown. I preserve my stack, avoid an expensive bluff‑catcher decision against a possible river raise, and still win when he missed his draws or had a worse pair. Betting thin for value risks being raised off my hand or folding out worse hands that would call. Action: check.

Strong position advantage on the button with a premium hand (A8o). From the button, I should be playing aggressively against the blinds. Ace-Eight is a solid raising hand in this spot, especially with favorable pot odds and positional advantage. A standard 3x BB raise (300 total) is appropriate here to build the pot, apply pressure, and take down the blinds or narrow the field to a heads-up situation where my positional advantage is maximized.

I am in the Small Blind with 4d 6s, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude opened for 3x from the Button. Defending such a weak hand out of position against the chip leader is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Claude opened to 300 from the button and SB folded. I'm in the big blind with QsQc — a premium hand heads-up against a single opener. With ~125BB effective behind this is a clear 3-bet spot: I want to build the pot for value, deny equity to speculative hands (suited connectors/small pairs), and take the initiative post‑flop. A 3x sizing is standard at this table and keeps worse queens/aces/broadways and many suited hands in the calling range while folding out the weakest holdings. I will raise to 900 total (an additional 800 chips from my BB) — big enough to extract value and protect, but not so large that I only get action from AA/KK. If Claude 4-bets big I will re-evaluate based on sizing; for now take the initiative with a standard value 3-bet.

Calling the 3-bet to 900 with A8o on the button. Position is critical here - I can play flop poker with better information and exploit my positional advantage. A8o has reasonable equity against Minni's 3-bet range, and the pot odds (3:1) justify the call. 4-betting would overcommit with a marginal hand. Playing in position allows disciplined post-flop decisions.

I 3‑bet preflop to 900 with QQ and Claude called — heads‑up on a K♠ T♠ 9♦ flop. My queens are an overpair to the T/9 and still ahead of most of his 3‑bet‑calling range (broadways, Ax, medium pairs), although any K or a set beats me. The board is somewhat wet and there are straight/connector combos (QJ) but my Q blocks a lot of those. I want to take the initiative: pressure weak broadways and pocket pairs, charge any J/T/8 draws, and deny free cards to hands that outdraw me. With the pot at 1850 I'll c‑bet for value/protection around ~2/3 pot — large enough to fold equity and to build the pot when I'm ahead, but not so large I only get action from absolute monsters. If Claude folds I scoop; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (pot‑control or barrel depending on texture); if he check‑raises big I’ll give him a lot of credit (K/set) and fold most of the time, calling smaller raises to probe further.

Folding A8o on Ts 9d Ks. Without any draws and facing strong continuation betting from Minni, A8o lacks the equity needed to justify a call. The flop heavily favors her 3-bet range (overpairs, broadway cards), and my marginal ace-high is vulnerable. With insufficient pot odds (~2.5:1 when I need ~3:1) for a speculative hand, folding preserves my stack for better opportunities. Even position doesn't justify continuing with such weak equity against a strong indicator bet.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game. Q9o is a solid hand to open with for a steal or to build a pot against the blinds. Given the stack sizes and the aggressive nature of the opponents (Minni and Claude), a standard 3x raise is a balanced play to take control of the hand early.

I’m in the small blind with 6♥7♣ — a weak, offsuit connector — facing a standard button open to 300. To call I must put in 250 into a 450 pot, which requires ~35.7% equity. This hand rarely has that equity vs a BTN opening range, and the big blind still acts behind me so I’m likely to face multiway action or a 3-bet out of position. 3-betting as a bluff here has poor fold equity and is unnecessarily risky with deep stacks. Prefer to preserve my healthy stack and wait for a better spot in position. Fold.

Q2o is one of the worst hands in poker with minimal equity against Flash's aggressive range. Although I'm getting 3.25:1 pot odds and have a healthy stack, the hand strength is simply insufficient. Queen-deuce offsuit lacks flush potential and has poor winning chances. This is a clear fold - I should wait for better spots with my strong chip position.

Q♥3♥ on the button is a marginal but playable suited queen with position and backdoor equity. With only the blinds to act, the +EV line is to take the initiative and attempt to steal — I’ll open-raise rather than limp to preserve fold equity and play the pot in position. A standard 3BB sizing (raise to 300) is big enough to apply pressure yet keeps the pot manageable against the two fairly active stacks (Claude is deep, Flash is aggressive). Plan: fold to a substantial 3-bet, call smaller defenses and use position to c-bet favorable flops and pot-control on wet boards.

Folding A6o from small blind facing a button raise. This weak hand is out of position and lacks sufficient equity to call profitably. A6o plays poorly in 3-way pots and the weak kicker is problematic against Minni's range. With 56 hands remaining, I can afford to be disciplined and wait for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind holding Queen-Five of diamonds. The button (Minni) has raised to 300. With the small blind folding, I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650. This gives me pot odds of roughly 3.25 to 1. Suited hands, even with a weak kicker like a five, have enough playability and equity against a standard button opening range to justify a defense from the Big Blind.

I have flopped a flush (Queen-high) on a monotone board of Jack-Four-King of diamonds. I am first to act on the flop. Standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) to give her the opportunity to continuation-bet with her range, which includes many strong hands I can get value from. If she bets, I plan to raise to build the pot.

Flop: J♦ 4♦ K♦. I opened Q♥3♥ from the button and Flash (BB) just called. This is a very wet, polarized board for my range — three diamonds and two broadway cards — and I have nothing (no diamond, no pair, no relevant backdoor). A c-bet here would be a pure bluff with very little fold equity because many of Flash’s defending hands contain a diamond or at least a strong pair; he can also check-raise with made flushes. With position and deep stacks the prudent +EV line is pot control: check and evaluate his action. Plan: check to avoid getting bluffed/raised off the hand, fold to any sizable barrel, consider calling a very small probe for pot control, and look to take cheap turns or bluff on non-diamond, favorable runouts if he shows weakness.

I have already flopped a Queen-high flush. On the flop, both I and Minni checked. The turn is the 5 of clubs, which doesn't change the strength of my hand significantly but does give me a small pair if for some reason the flush isn't the best hand. Since Minni checked back the flop, her range is likely capped or contains some marginal showdown value. By checking again, I am hoping to induce a bluff or a bet from a hand like the Ace of diamonds, a King, or a Jack. If she checks behind again, I will lead the river for value. If she bets, I will raise to extract value from her strong-but-not-flush hands or smaller flushes.

I have Q♥3♥ with no pair and no diamond on J♦ 4♦ K♦ 5♣. I checked the flop and Flash checked back both streets — his range likely contains diamonds, top pairs or pot-control hands, so my bluff has poor fold equity and I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position. With deep stacks and a small pot the +EV line is pot control: check and re-evaluate the river. Plan is to fold to any significant aggression, consider calling a very small probe, and only attempt a delayed bluff on a river that removes diamond/likely-made-hands or gives me a strong blocker.

I have a Queen-high flush on a board that didn't provide any further flush or full house possibilities on the turn and river. Since the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) checked back both the flop and the turn, it is highly unlikely she holds the Ace of diamonds, as she would typically bet for value or protection on such a wet board. Her range likely consists of marginal made hands like a King, a Jack, or perhaps a lower diamond she's trying to get to showdown cheaply. By leading out now, I can extract value from these hands that would likely check behind if I checked again. I'll bet roughly two-thirds of the pot to maximize value while still looking like a bet she might call with a one-pair hand.

I opened Q♥3♥ to 300 and was called by Flash (BB). Board runs out J♦ 4♦ K♦ / 5♣ / 8♥. I checked flop and turn for pot control; Flash checked both streets and now makes a small river bet of 400 into a 1,050 pot. I have queen-high with no diamonds and no pair — essentially zero showdown value. The flop was highly diamond-heavy (three diamonds), so a large portion of Flash’s defending range that called my open contains two diamonds (a made flush) or strong K/J holdings; his check‑check line into a small river bet is exactly the line for thin value or a slow-played made hand. To call 400 into a 1,050 pot (total 1,450) I need ~27.6% equity — I don’t have that against his likely value-heavy range, and his bluff frequency is unlikely to be high enough to make a call +EV. Raising as a bluff here is too risky (he can call/raise with hands that beat me). Fold and preserve chips for better spots.

Raising to 300 with Kh9s from the button. This is a standard 3x raise that applies appropriate pressure to the blinds. K9 is a solid broadway hand that plays well in position. Both opponents have shown aggressive tendencies but will need legitimate hands to continue. Position is strong here - I can take down the pot immediately or proceed with favorable odds post-flop if called.

In Hand #95, I am in the Small Blind holding K2s. Claude has raised from the Button to 300. This is a standard button open. K2s is a hand that can be played in the Small Blind, but it is relatively weak when facing a raise and acting out of position for the rest of the hand. However, my stack is deep enough to see a flop if the price is right, and suited Kings have some playability. Given the pot odds and the potential to flop a flush draw or top pair, calling is a reasonable option to defend the blind, though folding would also be safe. Raising would be a bit ambitious here. I will call the 250 to see a flop.

I’m in the big blind with J9 suited hearts facing a standard button open to 300 and a small blind call — 3-way with stacks deep enough to see a flop. J9s has good playability and equity multiway (flush/straight/backdoor combos) and calling preserves stack flexibility while realizing equity. A 3‑bet here as a bluff is too thin with a caller in between and will often put me out of position postflop; folding is too tight given pot odds and the hand’s potential. I’ll call the extra 200 (300 total committed) and proceed cautiously on the flop: look to continue with hearts/straights/top-pair, otherwise check-fold to heavy aggression or float/see turn with reasonable draws.

I am in the Small Blind with Ks2s. The flop is 3d-7h-3c, a paired board with low cards. There was no pre-flop action beyond a raise and two calls, leading to a 900 chip pot. I have nothing but a King high, but on this board, it is possible that everyone missed. However, acting first against two opponents, checking is the most standard and prudent play to see how they react to this board. If someone bets, I will likely fold, but checking allows me to potentially see a free turn or evaluate based on the size of any bets made.

Check. Preflop I called and planned to proceed cautiously — this flop (3♦ 7♥ 3♣) is very dry and unfavourable for a multiway bluff. I have J♥9♥ with no pair: only backdoor heart possibilities (need both turn+river; ~3.3% to make a flush by river) and 6 clean outs to pair (≈12.8% on the turn, ≈24.2% by river). Against a button open and a small‑blind caller, my fold equity as a c‑bet bluff is low and a bet here would bloat a pot where I’m behind most of the button’s value range. With stacks deep, pot‑control and seeing a turn for free (or at cheap cost) is the correct line — check and evaluate: call small stabs to see the turn with my pair outs/backdoors, fold to sizable pressure if I don’t improve, and only turn/river continue when I hit or pick up strong fold equity.

Continuation bet on a dry, paired board after both opponents checked to me. K9 plays well as a bluff candidate given my position and range advantage. The board texture (3-7-3) is not particularly dangerous and both checks suggest weakness. A 500 bet (~55% of pot) applies meaningful pressure while maintaining good pot control. This is standard aggression in position with a moderate-strength range.

On a paired board of 3d-7h-3c, Claude has followed up his pre-flop aggression with a 500 bet into a 900 pot. Holding only King-high with no draw and having to worry about Minni still to act behind me, calling is not a profitable long-term play. My hand has very little equity to improve, and even hitting a King on the turn doesn't guarantee I have the best hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Claude opened and I defended with J♥9♥. I checked a dry, paired flop (3♦7♥3♣) and Claude now bets 500 into a 1400 pot; Flash folded so it's heads-up. Calling 500 (800 total committed) gives me about 26% break-even equity (500/(1400+500) ≈ 26.3%). I have ~6 outs to make a pair (~24% to hit by river) plus a small (~4%) runner‑runner heart chance — versus a button c‑bet range my two overcards and backdoor hearts have roughly borderline/acceptable equity, and deep stacks give good implied odds. Raising is unattractive (little fold equity and it would bloat the pot with a drawing hand); folding is too tight given the price. I’ll call to see the turn, keep pot control, and fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve to a pair or pick up a strong draw.

We checked the dry, paired flop and called Claude’s 500. The turn (T♥) gives me a real hand — J♥9♥ now has a 9-card heart flush draw plus three non-heart eights that make a straight: ~12 clean outs to a strong made hand (~26% to hit on the river) and another ~6 cards to pair (≈39% to pair or better). Pot is 1,900 and I’m first to act on the turn. Checking gives Claude a free river and surrenders fold equity; leading is the standard semi-bluff to both take the pot now and build it when I hit. I’ll bet about half the pot to balance fold equity and keep my price reasonable if called. If Claude folds I win the pot; if he calls I have good equity to improve on the river; if he shows significant raise I can fold and preserve a healthy stack. Bet 1,000.

Folding is the correct play here. I raised K♥9♠ pre-flop from the button and made a reasonable continuation bet on the flop (3♦7♥3♣) after both opponents checked. However, Minni's turn raise to 1000 on a Ten that hit the board suggests she has acquired strength on this street. My K9 is essentially king-high with a weak kicker on a paired board with limited draw possibilities. Minni's action sequence (calling flop, raising turn) is more consistent with hands like made pairs (pocket Tens, TT, JJ, QQ), two pair (T3, 37), or strong kickers rather than a bluff. Even accounting for her aggressive tendencies, the risk-reward doesn't justify calling 1000 to potentially lose more on the river with a marginal hand. With a 16k+ stack, folding here allows me to preserve chips and play better spots from a position of strength. The pot odds (2.9:1) require roughly 26% equity, which I likely don't have against her range given the card that came off.

I am on the Button with Ten-Six offsuit. This is a weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially with both blinds being fairly active and having larger stacks than me. Opening with this hand is generally not profitable in this game's current dynamic. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Heads-up vs the big blind, Q♥5♠ is a marginal but openable holding. With only Claude left to act I want to seize initiative and steal the blinds or play a defined pot when called. A minimum open to 200 (total) keeps the raise small relative to our deep stack, gives fold equity, and lets me fold to a sizable 3‑bet without bloating the pot with a weak kicker. If Claude calls I can continuation-bet favorable flops or proceed cautiously when checked to; if he 3‑bets big I’ll let this one go. Overall a standard min-raise is the +EV, low-variance play here.

K2o is a weak hand that doesn't warrant further investment. While we're in a heads-up situation with Minni (Small Blind) and have pot odds of approximately 4:1 to call the 100, the hand quality is too marginal. King-deuce unsuited has limited equity and poor playability post-flop, especially out of position. We have weak kicker issues if we hit the king. Minni has been aggressive throughout the session and this raise could be a steal attempt, but we should not be calling marginal hands just because our opponent might be stealing. Better to wait for a stronger hand to engage from this position. The call would only make sense with stronger holdings or much deeper stacks.

I’m on the button with pocket 4s and both blinds live. This is a perfect spot to open-raise: I have position postflop and deep stacks (14.4k) give excellent implied odds for set-mining if I’m called. A standard 3x BB-style open (300) is the right size here — it’s large enough to fold out weaker blinds and isolate, but small enough to keep weaker hands and dominated callers in. It’s also consistent with my table image (I’ve been opening to ~300 frequently), which reduces the chance of getting exploited. If I face a reasonable 3-bet I’ll call to set mine or continue based on sizing and opponent tendencies; I’ll fold to an obviously huge 3-bet that offers poor odds. Overall, raise to 300 to take the initiative and maximize EV from this playable pocket pair.

I'm holding 4s Jc in the small blind, which is a weak unpaired hand with a gutshot potential at best. Minni has raised to 300 from the button - a standard 3x raise. I'll be out of position postflop and facing pressure from two opponents. While the pot odds (250 to call into 450) are reasonable numerically, the hand strength doesn't justify playing J4o in this spot. My stack of 16,655 is healthy enough that I can afford to be selective with marginal hands. Folding allows me to preserve chips for better spots where I have stronger equity.

I am in the Big Blind with Q4o. Minni (the Button) has raised to 300. While the pot odds are roughly 2.25:1 to call, Q4o is a very weak hand to defend out of position against a standard button open. Defending with such a wide, weak range typically leads to difficult post-flop scenarios where I am likely dominated or forced to fold to continuation bets. Given the stack sizes and the need to preserve my stack, folding is the most prudent play.

I'm on the button with Td7d, a speculative suited connector. The cost to call is only 100 chips into a 150-chip pot, giving me favorable implied odds. Position is a significant advantage post-flop - I'll act last and can make decisions based on opponent actions. Both Flash (short stack at 8700) and Minni (14495) are in the blinds and may check, allowing me to see a flop cheaply. If I hit the flop (pair, gutshot, or flush draw), I can play aggressively from position. Given the table dynamics where opponents seem to play actively post-flop, this hand plays well when I can see community cards and maintain positional advantage. A small speculative hand in position with decent implied odds is a profitable call here.

I am in the Small Blind with 4-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Claude has limped from the Button. While the pot odds to call the remaining 50 chips are 5:1, this hand performs very poorly post-flop, especially out of position. It is better to fold and wait for a more profitable spot than to waste chips on a hand that will rarely improve or win at showdown.

I’m in the big blind with 7♥5♥ facing a single limp from the button (Claude). This is a speculative, suited hand but I’m out of position, pot is already 250 and there’s nothing to call. A preflop raise to 200 would cost me extra chips to play OOP with marginal equity and invite calls from a wide limping range. With deep stacks I prefer to see a cheap flop and realize equity — check and evaluate the flop. If Claude bets a small amount I’ll call with strong draws and proceed; fold to large pressure unless I improve. Therefore I’ll check.

Flop: 7♣ 5♠ 4♣. I hold 7♥5♥ — two pair. This is a strong made hand OOP but vulnerable to sets and unlikely two-card straights; Claude limped preflop so his range is wide (many single-pair holdings, some pockets, some connectors). I want to extract value from one-pair hands (7x, 5x, mid pairs) and charge any speculative hands while not overcommitting out of position. A half-pot to slightly under half-pot bet gives good price for worse hands to call while denying free cards. Plan: bet ~1/2 pot (125) to get value; if Claude raises big I’ll strongly consider the set possibility and re-evaluate (call smaller raises, fold to a large shove depending on sizing).

Calling 125 into 375 with top pair (sevens) and a gutshot straight draw. Pot odds of 3:1 favor a call, and my positional advantage on the button allows me to make better decisions on later streets. Minni's small raise suggests moderate strength, and my hand has decent equity. The flush draw with diamonds adds additional outs.

I have 7♥5♥ for two pair on 7♣-5♠-4♣-Q♣. This is a strong made hand but the Q♣ completes a club flush (three clubs on board) and also brings Qx hands into contention. Claude limped preflop and passively called my small flop bet, so his range is wide and includes club draws, single pairs (7x/5x and small pairs), some Qx and a few pocket pairs. With deep stacks and OOP, I don’t want to bloat the pot where a single club or a set crushes me — pot control is the priority. I’ll check the turn to see what Claude does: call small-to-medium bets for value (he’ll often have worse pairs or Qx without a club), but fold to large raises/shoves that strongly represent a made flush or set. If he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river and extract thin value on safe runouts.

Betting 250 on the turn with top pair (sevens with a ten kicker) after Minni checks. My previous flop call already established my hand's credibility, and Minni's check suggests weakness. This bet accomplishes multiple goals: (1) extracts value from weaker queen holdings, marginal pairs, and missed draws, (2) gets paid on the river by worse hands, (3) maintains position control, and (4) sets up a clearer river decision. A 250-chip bet (half pot) is appropriately sized to get calls from marginal holdings while not committing too much with a medium-strength hand. If Minni raises, I can reassess, but their turn check strongly suggests my pair is good.

I have 7♥5♥ for two pair on 7♣-5♠-4♣-Q♣. Claude limped preflop and called my small flop bet, so his range is wide (one-pair hands, some pockets, Qx, and some two-club combos that now complete on this Q♣). His turn bet is small (250 into a 750 pot) — a sizing consistent with a probe or thin value. Raising OOP here would bloat the pot and force tough decisions against sets or a made club flush; I prefer pot control. Calling is cheap: I need roughly 20% equity to break even on a 250 call, and my two pair should have that vs his likely calling/raising range. Plan: call to see the river, extract thin value on safe runouts, and fold to a strong reraise/shove that clearly represents a set/flush.

I have 7♥5♥ for two pair on 7♣-5♠-4♣-Q♣-K♦. This is a strong but vulnerable hand — beaten by sets (77/55/44) and any two-club combo that made a flush on the turn. Claude limped pre and called my flop bet, then raised the turn only to 250, which shows a wide range (single pairs, Qx, some pocket pairs, and some club combos). Betting OOP on the river would bloat the pot and force very difficult decisions against sets/flushes; I want to keep the pot manageable and induce bluffs. I’ll check back to control the pot and either see a showdown or call a small-to-medium river bet. If Claude bets a small amount I’ll call for thin value; I will fold to a large shove that clearly represents a made flush or set.

Betting 350 on the river for value. Minni's two consecutive checks (turn and river) indicate weakness - likely a missed draw or weak hand. My top pair (sevens with ten kicker) is a strong holding given the action. The King on the river doesn't scare me because if Minni held AK/KQ they likely would have continued after my turn bet. A 350-chip bet extracts value from worse hands like missed draws, weak pairs, or lower kickers while being sized appropriately to avoid overcommitting with a medium-strength holding. This maximizes value and maintains the aggressive posture I established on the turn.

I hold 7♥5♥ — two pair on 7♣-5♠-4♣-Q♣-K♦. Claude limped pre, called my 125 flop bet, then check‑raised the turn to 250 (I called). His line is wide: single pairs (7x/5x/other), Qx, some pocket pairs (44/55/77) and a few two‑club combos that would have made a flush on the turn. The river king is a relatively safe card vs most of that range and his river bet (350) is small — consistent with thin value or a probe rather than an obvious shove. Calling 350 gets ~20% equity to break even (pot ≈ 1700 after a call), and my two pair is ahead of most of his continuing range; raising would bloata the pot and fold out worse hands. Decision: call and see showdown.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit. This is one of the weakest hands in poker and generally does not warrant an open-raise, especially with two active opponents in the blinds. My stack is currently the shortest at the table, and while I need to find opportunities to accumulate chips, this hand lacks the equity or playability to justify an investment. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have 4c8h in the small blind facing only the big blind. I’ve already posted 50 and can call the extra 50 for a total commitment of 100 — excellent pot odds to see a flop. 4-8 offsuit is a weak, marginal hand with poor playability out of position, so a preflop raise would be unnecessarily aggressive (Claude tends to defend/call preflop) and likely gets called, leaving me OOP in a tough spot. I’ll call cheaply to see the flop and proceed cautiously: check-fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, but continue (bet or call) if I pair or pick up a reasonable draw.

I'm holding 3h-6h in the big blind against Minni who called from the small blind. This is a weak hand that doesn't warrant aggression pre-flop. Since I'm already in for 100 chips and there's no raise, checking allows me to see the flop for free. The hand has minimal value and relies on hitting the board or catching lucky cards post-flop. Minni has already committed chips and is unlikely to fold to a raise, so checking is the prudent option to control pot size with a marginal holding.

I have middle pair (8s) with a very weak 4 kicker on a dry, rainbow K‑8‑2 flop. Pot is tiny (200) and both stacks are deep (≈15k). Claude checked preflop and is generally a sticky caller postflop, so a small flop bet will rarely fold out Kx and will mostly get called by worse or better hands — and a larger bet invites a raise that I don’t want to face with a vulnerable one‑pair hand. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity from position. Plan: call small bets on the flop/turn but fold to large, committed aggression unless I improve; if Claude checks back I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and look to extract value or pot‑control depending on the next card.

I'm holding 3h6h in the big blind on a Ks8c2h flop. This is a very weak hand with minimal equity - I have no made hand, no straight draws, and only a backdoor flush draw. Minni checked from the small blind, indicating weakness or a marginal hand. Given my hand's lack of value and the pot is only 200, checking is the correct play. I can control the pot size with a marginal holding and see the turn cheaply. If a heart comes, I'll have a strong draw to work with. Betting here with no hand and no draw would be speculative and fold out better hands while getting called by worse ones.

Check. I have top pair (8s) but with a very weak 4‑kicker on a dry K‑8‑2‑3 board and the pot is tiny (200) while stacks are deep (~15k). Claude (BB) checked both streets and is a sticky caller — a small bet will rarely fold better hands (Kx, pocket pairs) and a larger bet just invites a raise I don’t want to face with a marginal one‑pair hand. I’ll control the pot and realize equity: check to the river, plan to call a small turn/river bet but fold to large, committed aggression; if I improve to trips/two‑pair on the river I’ll extract value, otherwise keep pot small and avoid risking a big portion of my stack for thin value.

I flopped a pair of threes on the turn and Minni has checked twice, indicating weakness. My pair is likely ahead in this heads-up situation. Betting 150 extracts value from worse hands while charging any draw. This is a standard value bet sizing given the pot dynamics and board texture.

I have top pair (8♥) with a very weak 4♣ kicker on a dry K♠-8♣-2♥-3♣ board. Claude leads for 150 into a ~350 pot (~40% pot) — a small, non‑committing bet. Per my pre-/postflop plan I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than bloating the pot or folding decent showdown hands: raising would be reckless with this weak kicker and Claude is a sticky caller who will call with Kx and pocket pairs; folding would be too tight given the price. Calling 150 gives correct pot odds to continue and lets me re‑evaluate on the river (fold to large river jams, call small river bets or extract value if I improve).

Check. The river runout K♠‑8♣‑2♥‑3♣‑6♠ gives me top pair (8♥) with a very weak 4 kicker. Claude’s small turn lead (150) into a tiny pot looked like medium strength (Kx or a small/mid pair) and he’s a sticky caller — he’ll call small bets rather than fold better hands. With deep stacks and a marginal one‑pair holding, I want to keep the pot small and realize equity to showdown. Betting risks getting called by better hands (Kx, pocket pairs, better 8x) or raised by a hand that already beats me; folding would be too nitty. Checking is the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line here.

I have two pair (threes and sixes) on a Ks-8c-2h-3c-6s river. This is an extremely strong hand given the board texture. Minni has checked consistently throughout the hand - checking the flop twice, then calling my turn bet, and checking the river. This pattern indicates a weak or marginal holding, likely something like a medium-strength king, a missed draw, or complete air. With two pair, I'm well ahead of most hands in Minni's range. Betting 250 into the 500-chip pot is a standard value bet that should extract chips from weaker holdings (missed draws, weak pairs, ace-high) while maintaining balance and avoiding bet-folding. The sizing is aggressive enough to get value but not so large as to only get called by value hands that beat me. This is a clear value-betting situation where I have a strong made hand and my opponent has shown weakness.

I have top pair (8) with a very weak 4-kicker on a dry K♠-8♣-2♥-3♣-6♠ board. Claude has taken a small value line (bet 150 on the turn, now 250 on the river) — a sticky opponent who often bets small with Kx or mid pocket pairs. The pot shows 750 and it costs 250 to call, so I’m getting ~3:1 (≈25%) pot odds — I only need ~25% equity to break even. My hand beats bluffs and worse pairs and has reasonable showdown value versus Claude’s likely range, while a raise would be thin (it bloats the pot when I’m often behind and folds out worse). Folding here is too nitty given the price. I’ll call 250 to realize equity and go to showdown, and I’ll re-evaluate only if Claude jams for a very large amount.

Button with Q♠J♦ — a clear open-raise candidate in 3-handed play. I want to exploit position, pick up the blinds, and keep post‑flop options with a hand that plays well when it connects. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) — bigger than the min to discourage marginal callers/limpers and get reasonable fold equity against Claude/Flash, but not so big that I’m committed out of position if I miss. Plan: if everyone folds I take the blinds; if I get single‑called I’ll play aggressively in position (c‑bet on favorable or dry textures, control pot when checked to). If Flash (the aggressive BB) 3‑bets large I’ll usually fold QJ offsuit; versus a small 3‑bet I may call and rely on positional advantage. This sizing maximizes EV while keeping pots manageable deep in the stack.

Folding Q7o from small blind against button raise. This is a weak hand played out of position. I will act first postflop and lack the equity to justify calling 250 more chips. Better spots will come with my strong stack position.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 8c, which is a strong suited connector. Minni raised to 300 from the Button, and Claude folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650, giving me excellent pot odds. Suited connectors play well post-flop, especially in a heads-up situation where I can capitalize on various board textures. Given the stack depths and the position, calling to see a flop is a standard and profitable play.

I have an open-ended straight draw (7-8 on a 5-2-6 board) with eight outs to a straight (any 4 or 9). This is a strong drawing hand. Being out of position (Big Blind) against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni on the Button), the standard play is to check to the raiser and see how they react to the flop. Check-calling or check-raising are both viable options depending on Minni's bet size, but leading out (donk betting) here would be unusual and would likely weaken my overall range's protection.

I opened to 300 from the button and got one caller from the BB. The flop 5♣‑2♦‑6♠ is very dry and misses a lot of Flash’s calling range (small pairs, suited connectors, weak broadways). Q♠J♦ has two overcards plus a backdoor spade and backdoor straight potential, so it has reasonable equity versus a wide calling range and plays well in position. With position and a checked pot, a continuation bet ~1/3 pot is the highest EV line: it folds out many single‑pair/air hands, keeps the pot manageable if called, and gives us the initiative going forward. I’ll bet small (225 into 650). Plan: if Flash folds I take it down; if called I’ll evaluate the turn — continue on safe blank turns or controldown if facing aggression; facing a large raise I’ll fold QJo most of the time (only continue with very favorable sizing or a turning Q/J).

I have an open-ended straight draw (OESD) with 7c 8c on a 5c-2d-6s board, giving me 8 outs to a straight (any 4 or 9). The current pot is 875, and Minni has bet 225. This gives me pot odds of approximately 3.9:1 (or 20.4%). The probability of hitting my straight on the turn is about 17%, and the total equity by the river is about 32%. Given these odds and the potential implied odds if I complete my straight, calling is a very profitable decision. Check-raising is an option as a semi-bluff, but check-calling allows me to realize my equity and see a turn card cheaply against the button aggressor.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 8c, holding an open-ended straight draw on a board of 5c 2d 6s 5d. The turn card paired the 5, which is somewhat of a neutral card for my range unless I had a 5. As the out-of-position player, I will check to the pre-flop aggressor, Minni, to see her reaction. If she bets, I will likely call to realize my equity and attempt to hit my straight on the river, especially given the pot odds.

Turn pairs the 5 (5♣-2♦-6♠-5♦). I have Q♠J♦ — still no pair, only two overcards and very limited improvement chances (≈13% to hit a pair on the river). Flash checked after calling my small flop c-bet. That check is ambiguous: it can be pot‑control with a marginal pair or air, but it can also be a slowplay/induce with a made hand that benefited from the turn (A5 -> trips, pocket fives/quads, 66/22 sets, or two‑pair combos). Betting here would expose me to a check‑raise on a board that now heavily favors villain's calling range and gives me almost no fold equity vs the hands I'm worried about. With effective stacks deep relative to the pot a check‑raise could get large and put me in a fold-or‑call‑all-in spot with air. Pot control in position is the higher EV line: check and see the river for a final evaluation. If Flash fires a moderate/large bet on the river I’ll fold; if he checks through I can consider a small bluff on a safe card or a value bet only if I pair up.

I have completed an 8-high straight on the river (4-5-6-7-8) with my 7c-8c. The board is paired with fives, which introduces the possibility of a full house; however, Minni's decision to check back on the turn suggests she is unlikely to have trips or a boat. Since I am first to act on the river and have what is likely the best hand, I will lead with a value bet of 750 into the 1100 pot to extract value from lower straights (any 3) or pairs she may call with.

The river bet is 750 into a 1,850 pot (I must call 750 to win 2,600) — I need roughly 750/2600 ≈ 29% equity to justify a call. I hold Q♠J♦ with no pair and very limited showdown value. Flash’s line (check-call on the flop, check on the turn, small-ish river bet) is exactly what I’d expect from a lot of medium-to-strong value hands that now beat me: one-card 5s that made trips (A5, K5, etc.), pocket 22 or 66 sets, pocket 44 that turned a pair, some A4/K4 type hands that paired the river, and occasional 3x straights. There will be some thin value and a few bluffs, but the betting range is heavily weighted toward hands that beat a lone QJ. My hand doesn’t block the key value combos, and I very likely have substantially less than the ~29% equity required. Raising as a bluff is unattractive — a min-raise just builds a big pot against a player who will call with many hands that already beat me, and I’m not sufficiently nailed-on to have fold equity or blockers to make that profitable. Given the pot odds, board texture, his line, and the lack of showdown value in QJ, the correct play is to fold.

2-3 is the worst starting hand in poker - it has almost no equity against any opponent. Even on the button with favorable position, this hand is far too weak to play. I would need to call 100 chips into a 150-chip pot, but my hand is dominated by virtually every possible holding my opponents could have. Folding weak hands from any position, even on the button, is a fundamental part of solid poker strategy. My stack is healthy at 16,280, so there's no need to take unnecessary risks with trash hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Six suited, which is a strong starting hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Since Claude has folded, it is just me and Minni. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take the initiative and either win the pot immediately or build it with a range advantage.

I have pocket sixes (6d6h) in the big blind facing a 3x open to 300 from the small blind. Pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call (total 300 in the pot for me). Stacks are deep (me ~143bb, Flash ~90bb) and Flash has been opening to 300 frequently, indicating a fairly wide range. With a small pocket pair vs a wide opener and deep stacks, the +EV play is to call and set-mine — I keep the pot manageable and retain strong implied odds to extract when I hit a set. A minimal 3-bet to 500 gives little fold equity and a large 3-bet bloats the pot unnecessarily with a hand whose primary value is hitting a set. Postflop plan: if I miss I’ll use position to evaluate – check-fold to heavy, polarized aggression on high/connected boards, but check-call or float on dry boards and extract maximum if I hit a set. Action: call to 300 total.

On the flop of Ac-9c-3s, I have top pair with an Ace. Although my kicker (6s) is weak, I am currently ahead of most of Minni's calling range from the Big Blind, which likely includes many drawing hands, smaller pairs, or weaker Aces. I will place a standard continuation bet of half the pot (300) to extract value from those hands and protect my equity against potential draws like clubs or straight possibilities.

I have 6♦6♥ on 9♣ A♣ 3♠. I called preflop to set-mine against Flash’s wide 3x open and he’s continued with a 300 flop bet. Pot is 900 and it’s 300 to me — calling keeps the pot manageable and preserves my implied odds to hit a set. My raw chance to make a set by the river is only ~8.4%, so without implied odds this is a poor call, but Flash opens and barrels frequently and stacks are deep, so the potential payoff when I connect justifies the small investment. A small 3-bet would have little fold equity and a larger 3-bet risks bloating the pot with a hand that wants to hit. Plan: call, see the turn; fold to heavy, polarized aggression if I completely miss the turn and face big pressure, but extract when I hit a set or realize showdown value versus missed overcards/bluffs.

On the turn, the board pairs with a second Ace, giving me three of a kind. While my hand is strong, my kicker (6s) is relatively weak, meaning I could be outkicked if Minni also holds an Ace. By checking, I control the size of the pot and provide Minni an opportunity to bluff with her draws (like the club flush draw) or bet with a marginal hand like a 9. If she checks back, I can still go for value on the river. If she bets, I plan to call to see a river card.

I called preflop and the flop to set-mine with 6♦6♥ against Flash’s wide 3x opens and c-bets. The turn paired the board with A♦ (board now 9♣ A♣ 3♠ A♦). Flash checked the turn after c-betting the flop. With an underpair to the board my raw chance to improve on the river is tiny (only 2 outs ≈ 4.3%), so I have almost no showdown equity if Flash actually holds an Ace or a better pair. Betting the turn would be a pure bluff that can be easily raised off me or called by many hands that still beat me (A‑x, 9x, bigger pocket pairs, or even some floats), and a small bet buys very little fold equity given his line. With deep stacks and the goal of maximizing EV over many hands, the correct play is to control the pot and see the river for free. Plan: check behind, evaluate the river — call small bluffs, fold to large value bets from hands that logically continue (trip Aces, sets, or strong two‑pair).

On the river, the board has paired Aces and also a King has appeared. My hand is three of a kind Aces with a Nine kicker (the board's King and Nine are the two highest kickers). In this situation, I am either ahead, chopping with another Ace that has a kicker lower than 10, or behind a stronger Ace (AT+) or a full house (A9, A3, AK, 99, 33). Minni's check on the turn suggests she might not have a very strong Ace or a monster hand, though she could be trapping. By betting 400 (1/3 pot), I am looking to get value from a King or a Nine that she might have called the flop with, and I'm also putting out a 'blocker' bet that is easy to call if we are simply chopping with another weak Ace. If she raises significantly, I will have to consider that she likely has the full house or a superior kicker.

Pot is 1,600 and it’s 400 to me (I need 20% equity). I hold 6♦6♥ on 9♣ A♣ 3♠ A♦ K♦ — an underpair to a paired‑Ace board with essentially no backdoors. Flash opened wide and c‑bet the flop, then checked the Ace‑turn and now posts a tiny 400 river probe. That line/sizing is consistent with a mixed range: many A‑x and better pairs that beat me, but also thin value bets and some bluffs/missed hands given his frequent aggression and small sizing. Raising would bloat the pot with very little fold equity vs Ax/TT+ and folding here is too nitty because the price is small and he bluffs often. Calling is the lowest‑variance +EV play: it realizes showdown when he is bluffing or betting thin and keeps my risk controlled when he has value. Action: call 400.

I am on the button with a suited Queen. In a three-handed game, the button opening range should be quite wide (approximately 40-50% of hands). Queen-four suited is a reasonable hand to open for a standard raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I have consistently used a 3x open (300) in previous hands from this position.

Fold. Facing a standard 3x button open to 300 while I'm in the small blind with 8♥7♦ offsuit. This hand is marginal off‑suit and will be very difficult to play OOP postflop against both the button and the big blind. Calling 250 into a 450 pot often creates a multiway, OOP pot with little equity or backdoor potential; a small squeeze to 500 as a bluff is thin and likely to be called or 3‑bet by the deeper stacks. Preserve chips and wait for a better, positionally favourable spot.

Analyzing this hand from the Big Blind:

Hand: T6o (Ten-Six offsuit) - a weak, unplayable holding
Position: Big Blind (worst position)
Situation: Flash raised to 300 from the button, Minni folded

Key considerations:
1. T6o has very poor equity against Flash's raising range from the button
2. I'm out of position, which compounds the problem significantly
3. Flash is an active, aggressive player based on the hand history
4. Calling 200 more would put me in a difficult spot post-flop out of position with a marginal hand at best
5. This hand doesn't have the strength to raise and win it preflop reliably

This is a straightforward fold. While I have 16k chips and can afford to call, poker is about making profitable decisions, not just having chips available. T6o is simply too weak to continue with from the big blind against an aggressive opponent in this spot.

I’m on the button with K♣9♦ in a three-handed spot. With blinds 50/100 and the pot only 150, this is a standard open-raise situation — K9o is a good button opener heads-up and I want to take the initiative, isolate and avoid giving the big blind a free or cheap flop. A 3x BB-sized open (to 300 total) is standard here: it’s large enough to fold out lots of weaker hands and small speculative holdings, but not so large that I overcommit out of position if I face a big 3-bet. Plan: open to 300; continuation bet favorable flops in position, and fold to very large 3-bets (or re-evaluate depending on sizing).

TJ of hearts is a premium hand - strong broadway holding with excellent straight and flush potential. Minni has raised from the button (a position where she can have a wide range), and I'm in the small blind. Rather than passively calling 250, I should 3-bet to build the pot when I have a strong hand and apply pressure. A raise to 800 (from my current 250 obligation) achieves multiple objectives: (1) gets value from my premium hand, (2) gives me positional advantage post-flop, (3) applies pressure on both opponents, and (4) narrows the range of hands that will call me. This is a standard aggressive play with a top-tier broadway hand in position against a button raiser.

I am in the Big Blind holding 4d 3d. Minni (Button) opened to 300, and Claude (Small Blind) 3-bet to 800. While 43s has some speculative value, it is far too weak to continue against a 3-bet, especially with the original raiser still to act and being forced to play a bloated pot out of position. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

Fold. Claude’s 3-bet to 800 after my 300 button open is a fairly large, value-heavy sizing. To continue I must call 500 into a 1200 pot (pot becomes 1700), so I need ~29.4% equity. K♣9♦ is a marginal offsuit king that’s often dominated by the kinds of hands Claude 3-bets (AK, KQ, QQ+, etc.) and it doesn’t make a good 4‑bet bluff candidate with deep stacks. Without a solid read that he’s 3‑betting very light, calling is borderline and a small 4‑bet is unlikely to fold out enough equity while getting called by better. Preserve my stack and wait for a clearer, more profitable spot — fold.

Raising with pocket queens from the button is standard. QQ is a premium hand that should build value pre-flop. A raise to 350 (250 more on top of 100 to call) is appropriately sized - strong enough to build the pot and potentially win it outright, while remaining reasonable relative to the pot size. This allows me to extract value from weaker hands that call while taking advantage of my position and hand strength.

I am in the Small Blind with 3h 5d, which is an extremely weak hand. Claude raised from the button to 350. Playing such a low-equity hand out of position is a recipe for losing chips in the long run. There is no reason to defend or bluff here; folding is the standard and most profitable play.

Fold. 7s3s is one of the weakest hands — poorly connected and easily dominated. Claude opened to 350; calling requires 250 more into a 500 pot, so I need roughly 33% equity to make a break‑even call. In a 3-handed game, opening ranges from the button should be relatively wide. I'm in the small blind with K♥6♣ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450). Raising with premium AK in the big blind vs button raise. This is a standard 3-bet that builds the pot, extracts value, and applies pressure to Flash's wide opening range. The raise size of 800 (roughly 2.7x Flash's raise) is reasonable given stack depths and board dynamics.

I opened to 300 from the button with Jack-Four suited (J4s), which is a light open designed to take the blinds. Claude in the Big Blind has responded with a 3-bet to 800. Button with Ah2h (A2s) in a 3-handed pot is a hand I want to play aggressively — it has the nut-ace, suitedness and great postflop playability in position. Raising to 300 balances extracting value, denying cheap multiway flops, and leaving plenty of stack behind to exploit postflop.

Folding Q7o from small blind after a button raise. The hand is weak and easily dominated, I'm out of position post-flop, and while pot odds are reasonable at 2.8:1, this hand doesn't have sufficient value to warrant a call. Standard strategy indicates folding marginal hands in poor position.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of fours. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300. This is a standard late-position open. Claude has folded. I am first to act on the flop after calling a pre-flop raise from the Button. I’m the preflop aggressor (button) with A♥2♥ on J♦-8♥-7♣. I have the nut-ace and a backdoor heart possibility, plus positional advantage, so this is a good spot to continuation-bet thin. I have an underpair of fours on a highly coordinated flop of 7-J-8. This board texture heavily favors the button's opening range, which includes many jacks, eights, sevens, and straight draws. 6c3c is a very weak hand - low, unconnected cards with minimal equity. While I'm on the button with positional advantage, this hand is below the threshold of even loose button play. I am in the Small Blind with 3h 4d, a very weak hand. Claude has already folded from the Button. I'm in the small blind with Q♦9♥ facing a button open to 300. Calling costs me 250 more into a 450 pot (break-even equity ≈ 250/(450+250)=35.7%), which is cheap relative to my deep stack (13,295). Raising with AT (premium hand) from big blind against button raise and small blind call. Action reaches me on the button. I originally raised to 300 with A4o. Minni called from the SB, and Claude 3-bet to 900 from the BB. I'm on the button with 5h6h — a speculative, positionally strong suited connector. 4-10 offsuit is a weak hand with poor equity. Out of position against Minni's button raise with Flash still to act behind me. Clear fold - no value in calling to chase a bottom-tier holding.

On a flop of 7s-4d-2s, I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker (Deuce of Diamonds). I am first to act against Minni. This board is relatively disconnected but has a spade draw. Flash (SB) has c-bet 300 into a 900 pot (~1/3 pot) on 4♦-7♠-2♠ — a small, standard continuation bet that often represents a wide range. Calling costs 300 to win ~1200, so I only need ~25% equity. On the turn, the board is 7s-4d-2s-6h. I have a pair of Deuces with an Ace kicker. Flash checked the turn after a small flop c-bet and my call — a clear sign of weakness or pot control. On a river board of 4d-7s-2s-6h-8c, my pair of Deuces is very weak. After the turn went check-check, it's possible Minni has a marginal hand or a missed draw. The river 8♣ completes a 6-7-8-9-10 straight — my T♥9♣ is now a made straight (10-high) which is almost always the best hand on this board. I am holding bottom pair (Deuces) on a board that finished 4d-7s-2s-6h-8c. Minni called pre-flop and on the flop, then checked the turn. I am holding pocket tens on the button in a three-handed game. This is a premium starting hand. I will put in a standard raise to 300 chips to build the pot and take advantage of my position throughout the hand.

Folding K3o to Minni's 3-bet. This is a weak hand without the equity to defend the blinds against an aggressive 3-bet. Even with decent pot odds, being out of position with this unpaired hand against likely strong holdings (TT+, AK, AQ) makes this a fold. Better spot waiting.

I have pocket tens on the button. I opened for 300 and Minni 3-bet from the small blind to 1200. Flop is 7c 7d 6d and I hold As Ah. A modest c‑bet of ~40% pot (1,000 into 2,500) accomplishes that: it gets called by worse pairs and many Ax/Kx hands, denies free cards, and avoids bloating the pot where a set can trap me. Plan: if called, continue to extract on safe turn cards and re‑evaluate; if I face a large check‑raise I’ll give significant credit to 7x and proceed cautiously.

I have an overpair (pocket tens) on a paired board of 7-7-6. Minni, who 3-bet pre-flop, has led for 1,000 into a 2,500 pot. This is a standard continuation bet on this texture. My hand is strong, only losing to higher overpairs (JJ-AA), a rare 7 (like A7s), or a set of sixes. By calling instead of raising, I keep her range wide—including her bluffs like AK, AQ, or diamond draws—while maintaining position to evaluate the turn. Folding is out of the question given the strength of my overpair in a 3-handed game.

Turn pairs the board (7c 7d 6d 6c). My As Ah is now two pair (Aces & Sevens) but is crushed by any 7 or 6 in Flash’s hand (those become full house/quads). Flash called my 3‑bet preflop and my modest flop c‑bet, so his continuing range can include overpairs and Ax hands but also combos containing a 7 or 6. I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a hand that is largely dead to those combos — a bet here just invites a check‑raise or commit against full houses while only getting value from worse two‑pair/overpairs. I’ll check to control the pot and let Flash show aggression if he has the nuts. Plan: if he bets a small-to-medium amount I’ll call to get to river and reevaluate; if he fires a large overbet (represents full house/quads) I will fold. If he checks back I’ll seek a small thin value bet on safe rivers or check for showdown on dangerous rivers.

The board has double-paired (7-7-6-6), which significantly changes the hand value. My pocket tens now constitute a two-pair (10-10-7-7-6). I am still ahead of her likely 3-betting bluffs like AK or AQ (which have 7-7-6-6-A) and pocket pairs like 88 or 99. However, I am behind any higher overpair (JJ-AA) and any hand containing a 7 or a 6, both of which would have a full house. Minni's check on the turn could be a trap or a realization that the board is dangerous. By checking behind, I exercise pot control, avoid being check-raised by a monster, and can evaluate the river with 4,500 already in the pot.

Board is 7c 7d 6d 6c Kc; I have AsAh — Aces & Sevens (two pair) but any hand with a 7 or a 6 in Flash’s hand (or pocket 77/66) turned into a full house/quads on the turn and crushes me. There are still many worse hands in his continuing range (Kx, Qx, medium pairs) that will call a small value bet on the river. Checking is safe but concedes thin value; a small bet (~1/3 pot) extracts from those worse hands while keeping the pot controllable if Flash shows strength. I’ll bet small for value (1500 into 4500). If he check‑raises big, I’ll give significant credit to a full house and fold; if he just calls I get value from Kx/medium pairs.

The board has double-paired (7-7-6-6) and a King fell on the river. I am holding pocket tens, which means my best five-card hand is two pair, Tens and Sevens (10-10-7-7-K). Minni 3-bet pre-flop, led the flop, checked the turn, and is now leading for 1,500 into a 4,500 pot on the river. Her turn check followed by a river bet after the King appeared is suspicious; she could be betting a King (like AK/KQ) that just improved, or she could be value betting a higher overpair (JJ-AA) that she played cautiously on the turn. However, she could also be bluffing with missed diamond draws or hands like AQ. Given the pot odds (calling 1,500 to win 6,000), I only need to be right 25% of the time. My tens beat all her bluffs and lower pocket pairs. While the King is a bad card for me, the price is too good to fold in a 3-handed game against an aggressive opponent.

I'm on the button with K♠9♠ — a playable, suited K with good position in a three‑handed pot. A-2o is one of the weakest hands in poker. Playing it out of position against an aggressive raiser creates a difficult, unprofitable situation post-flop. I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit (A7o). In a three-handed game, this is a standard opening hand from the button. J♣9♣ on the button in a three-handed pot is a clear open-raise spot. I am in the big blind with 2d 3h, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Call 100. 9♥7♣ is a marginal hand but it's on the button in a 3‑handed pot — position is valuable. Calling 50 to see the flop with Queen-high in a 3-way pot. While 6h Qd is marginal, the pot odds are favorable (5:1) and the cost is minimal relative to my stack. Checking with Q♦6♥ on J♦5♣3♥. Out of position with a marginal queen-high hand. Betting would be value-destroying since I'm likely behind or at best flipping against villain holdings. In a limped pot with three players, the board of J-5-3 rainbow does not hit my Queen-high hand. I have two overcards to the lower board cards but no real equity or draws. Both opponents checked, so betting into two players here buys me little fold equity — if called I have almost no real equity to continue. I want to keep the pot small and use my position to see a free turn where I can decide whether to bluff or give up based on actions and the card. Plan: pot control and retain flexibility — check and reassess on the turn.

Betting 150 on the turn with top pair (Queens) after both opponents checked the flop. The turn brought the beneficial Q♣, and with limited aggression from Minni and Flash preflop/flop, I have a strong likely-best hand. This bet extracts value from draws (K♣T, A♠K variants, two-card diamonds) and weaker pairs while avoiding overcommitting. The sizing is appropriate given the pot size (300) and allows me to stay flexible on the river. Flash's short stack (4825) means he may fold or commit soon, and Minni's check on the flop combined with his call/fold tendencies on the turn will inform the river action.

On the turn, the Queen of Clubs gives me top pair with a nine kicker. The Small Blind (Claude) led out for 150 into a 300-chip pot after the flop was checked through. This bet could represent a Jack, a Queen, or a draw. Given that I have top pair in a limped pot, my hand is too strong to fold to a half-pot bet. However, raising is unnecessary as it would likely only get called by better hands (like a Queen with a better kicker or two pair) and might force out bluffs. I will call to see the river and keep the pot manageable, while also being mindful that Minni is still left to act behind me.

Board J♦-5♣-3♥-Q♣, I hold 9♥7♣. Claude bets 150 into a 600 pot and Flash calls. If I call 150 I’d be trying to win ~1,050, so I need roughly 150/1,050 ≈ 14.3% equity to break even. My only clean river outs are the three remaining 9s and three remaining 7s = 6 outs → ~13% to hit on the river. There are no realistic straights or flushes available with one card to come, and multiway action (bet + call) makes it likely I’m up against at least a pair (or Q/J) that still beats a single pair. Raising as a bluff into two players is too risky and unlikely to get enough folds. I checked turn to control the pot as planned; folding here preserves my deep stack and avoids a negative‑EV call.

Checking on the river with pair of Queens (Qd-6h) on Jd-5c-3h-Qc-9c. Flash shows consistent weakness (checked pre-flop, called turn rather than raised), and the 9 on river doesn't improve his likely holdings. Being out of position, checking maintains pot control and allows Flash to check behind with weaker pairs (I win showdown) or bluff (I can evaluate). Flash is short-stacked, so a river bet won't produce much fold equity and risks getting called by better hands. Pair of Queens should win at showdown in most scenarios.

On the river, I have improved to two pair (Queens and Nines). Claude, who led the turn after the Queen hit, has now checked. This check often indicates a one-pair hand like a Queen with a weak kicker or a Jack that is looking to get to showdown cheaply. Since I now beat all of Claude's one-pair hands, I will place a value bet of 400 (two-thirds of the pot) to extract more chips from a weaker Queen or potentially a stubborn Jack.

Flash is extremely short-stacked (4,275 chips) and his action pattern suggests weakness: checked flop, called my turn bet with the Queen, and now raising on the river. With pair of Queens (Q♦6♥) on Jd-5c-3h-Qc-9c, I'm likely holding the best hand. Flash's river raise from a short stack is more likely a desperation play, steal attempt, or marginal hand (like J9 or 99) than a premium holding he would have played more aggressively earlier in the hand. I'm getting 3.5:1 pot odds (400 to win 1400), meaning I only need to win ~22% of the time to break even—a clear positive expectation given the strength of my hand and Flash's likely range. Calling maximizes value against his short-stack plays while remaining cautious against the small possibility of a stronger hand.
Premium hand (KJs) on the button in an unopened pot. Standard aggressive raise to 3x BB (300 chips) to take control, build the pot with a strong hand, and leverage position advantage.

I am holding 3h 6d, which is an extremely weak hand. Claude has raised from the button, and I am in the small blind, meaning I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Given the strength of my hand and my position, it is best to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Facing a standard button open to 300 from Claude with Flash (SB) folded. I’m in the big blind with 4♠6♠. Pot is 450 and I must put in 200 more (300 total) — that’s about 200 to win ~650 (≈30.8% equity needed). Claude’s button raising range is likely fairly wide here, and with deep effective stacks I have good implied odds to realize equity with a suited connector/one-gapper. 4♠6♠ is too weak to 3‑bet for value and I don’t want to isolate out of position with a marginal hand, so I’ll call and see a flop. Plan: fold to heavy, committed aggression on dry flops; proceed aggressively (raise/call) if I hit a pair, two‑way straight/flush draws, or favorable runouts.

Flop J♣ 7♣ 5♥ — I hold 4♠6♠. This is an open‑ended straight draw (3 or 8 = 8 outs, ~32% to hit by the river) with only remote backdoor spade potential. Claude opened wide from the button, so his c‑bet range will be broad. I’m OOP and don’t want to inflate the pot with just a draw, so I’ll check to control the size and let him take the lead. Plan: call a standard c‑bet to realize equity and consider a check‑raise only as a semi‑bluff against small bets; fold to heavy, committed aggression if I miss and he barrels big. If I hit my straight I’ll extract value later.

Strong hand (top pair with king kicker) on favorable flop. I have K♠J♠ on 5♥7♣J♣, giving me the strongest made hand. Minni checked, indicating weakness or a draw. Position advantage on the button allows me to control the pot with a value bet. Standard c-bet sizing of approximately 45-50% of pot to build the pot, extract value from weaker pairs, and protect against drawing hands (Minni may have flush draws with clubs or low pocket pairs). This is a clear value betting situation with strong equity and position.

I have 4♠6♠ on J♣7♣5♥ — an open‑ended straight draw (3 or 8 completes), 8 outs (~31.5% to hit by the river) plus a remote backdoor spade. Pot is 1,075 and Claude bet 425; calling 425 (pot becomes 1,500) requires about 425/1,500 ≈ 28.3% equity, and my draw (~31.5%) clears that. I’m out of position so I want to keep the pot under control and realise equity rather than inflate it with an OOP semi‑bluff; with deep stacks I also have decent implied odds if I hit. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to heavy, committed aggression if I still miss, and extract value if/when I complete the straight or get a favourable runout.

I have 4♠6♠ on J♣7♣5♥ K♦ — I'm still on an open‑ended straight draw (3 or 8 = 8 outs, ~17.4% to hit the river). Pot is 1500 and there is no bet to call. I'm out of position vs Claude (button) who opened preflop and continued on the flop, so leading here with only ~17% equity would bloat the pot unnecessarily. I want to realize equity cheaply and keep the pot controlled: check and see Claude's reaction. If he checks back I get a free river; if he bets the turn, I will only continue with a bet ≤ ~300 (threshold ≈316 chips) where the pot odds justify a call, and fold to larger turn/river aggression unless I improve or pick up a strong read. If I hit the straight on the river I will extract value.

Top two pair (Kings and Jacks) on the turn. Minni has checked twice, indicating weakness or a draw. The King improved my hand significantly and is unlikely to have helped Minni. Standard turn value bet to extract chips from his range of weak pairs and draws. Betting approximately 50% of the current 1500 chip pot to build value while maintaining pot control.

I have 4♠6♠ on J♣7♣5♥ K♦ — an OESD to 3/8 (8 clean outs ≈17.4% to hit the river). Pot is 2250 and Claude bet 750. Calling 750 to win 3000 requires ~25% equity, so my straight draw alone doesn’t meet the pot odds. The additional pair-outs (3 fours/3 sixes) are “dirty” — they’ll frequently lose to Kx, Jx or other value hands that fit Claude’s line. Claude opened from the button, c-bet the flop and barreled the turn with a large sizing; that line is value-heavy or polar and not enough bluff frequency to justify calling or risking a raise with only a draw and poor blockers. I had planned earlier to fold to heavy committed aggression on the turn — this is that spot. Fold and preserve stacks for better, +EV spots.

I am on the button with Ace-Four offsuit in a three-handed game. An Ace-high hand is generally strong enough to open for a raise from the button to attempt to steal the blinds or play the pot in position. I will follow my established pattern of raising to 300.

I will call to 300. Q6♣ is a suited Queen with backdoor flush/straight potential — marginal but defendable from the small blind against a wide button open.
