Calling 250 into a 450 pot (~36% break-even equity) is reasonable versus a standard BTN opening range, and I prefer to keep the pot manageable out of position rather than 3-bet with a hand that can be dominated. If Claude 3-bets big I'll fold; if we see a flop and hit a Queen, pair, or club draw I'll proceed selectively, otherwise I'll play cautiously and avoid bloating the pot OOP.

5-2o is the worst starting hand in poker with minimal equity. Out of position against two opponents, even with 3.5:1 pot odds, the combination of hand weakness and positional disadvantage makes this a clear fold. Preserving chips for better opportunities is the professional approach.

Out of position with Qc6c on a K♦ T♣ J♦ flop: I have no pair, only two backdoor clubs and 8 outs (any A or 9) to make Broadway — roughly 31–32% to hit by the river and ~17% to hit on the turn. This board heavily favors the button’s opening range (Kx, Qx, AJ/AQ/Q9, sets), so leading out would be thin and will bloat a pot I don’t want OOP. I’ll check to control the pot and realize my equity. Plan: call small-to-medium continuation bets (roughly up to ~600 into the 700 pot where my ~31% equity is profitable), but fold to pot-sized or larger bets / strong aggression. If Flash checks back I can consider a small turn stab on suitable cards or continue with made hands/clear equity (9/A/club runner) and extract value if I hit.

On a flop of Jack-Ten-King, my hand (Ace-Four) has very little equity, although I do have a gutshot draw to a Broadway straight with any Queen. This board texture heavily favors a typical calling range from the small blind, which likely includes many broadway cards and pairs. Since Minni checked, I will check behind to take a free card rather than risking a continuation bet on a board that is very likely to have connected with my opponent's range.

Turn: I’ve paired my Queen (Qc6c) on Jd Tc Kd Qh — top pair but with a weak 6 kicker and vulnerable to Kx, better Qx, two-pairs (KQ/QJ) and the obvious Broadway straights (A or 9). Flash opened from the button and checked the flop, so his range is wide and contains many missed broadway hands and some medium/strong made hands. Betting a small amount now achieves three things: (1) extracts value from worse queens, Jx/Tx and some A-high callers, (2) charges the A/9 straight outs and makes them pay to see a free river, and (3) can win the pot immediately when he checked as air on the flop. I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal kicker, so I’ll make a modest protection/value bet — 200 into 700 (~25–30% pot). If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river; if he raises pot-sized or larger I’ll fold (I won’t get stubborn OOP with Q6).

I have the nut straight (Ace-high straight: A-K-Q-J-T). The board is Jack-Ten-King-Queen. With my Ace of Spades, I currently hold the best possible hand as no flush is possible yet. Minni has led out with a small bet of 200 into a pot of 700. I want to build the pot and extract value from hands like two pair, sets, or a lower straight (if she holds a Nine). I will raise to 700 total to keep her interest while increasing the stakes.

I have Qc6c — top pair but a very weak 6-kicker on a Jd Tc Kd Qh board. I bet a small 200 on the turn to protect and deny A/9 outs; Flash checked the flop then 3-bet my small turn bet to 700. Pot is 1600 and it costs me 500 to call. Pot odds: 500 to win 1600 (500/(1600+500) ≈ 23.8% break-even). Flash’s raise is polarized toward strong value (straights with A/9, two-pair combos like KQ/QJ/KJ, sets) but will include some bluffs/semi-bluffs too. My hand’s showdown equity vs that raising range is marginally around the mid-20s — roughly in line with the price. Raising OOP with a weak kicker would be reckless (it bloats the pot and invites further aggression), and folding forfeits decent pot odds. Plan: call the 500 to see the river, and be prepared to fold to significant river aggression unless I improve or see a clear weak line from him.

Turn action (he 3-bet my small 200 to 700) strongly polarizes Flash toward value: straights (A or 9), two-pair combos (KQ/QJ/KJ) and sets. The river 2♦ is a blank for my hand — I still have top pair with a very weak 6-kicker and I’m usually behind to the line he took. Betting now would be thin value (only a few worse Qx combos will call) and will mostly get called by or folded to the wrong hands; it also opens me up to a costly raise from a range that dominated me on the turn. I’ll check back and take the free showdown — if Flash fires a significant bet I’ll fold (only consider calling a very small bet that gives correct pot odds), since preserving stack vs his strongly weighted value range is the higher EV line.

I have the Ace-high straight (A-K-Q-J-T), which was the absolute nuts on the turn. The river 2d completes a possible flush (3 diamonds on board: Jd, Kd, 2d). However, Minni checked on the river after calling my raise on the turn. This check likely indicates a marginal hand like a lower straight (holding a 9), two pair, or a set, rather than a completed flush which might have lead or attempted a check-raise. I will place a value bet of 1000, roughly half the pot, to extract value from those weaker hands or to split the pot with another Ace.

Pot is 3,100 and it costs me 1,000 to call (break-even ≈24.4%). Flash’s line (button open, checked flop, 3-bet my small 200 turn to 700, I called, then a 1,000 river barrel) is strongly weighted to value — completed broadway straights (AT, T9/9T), two-pairs (KQ/QJ/KJ), sets and strong Kx/AQ/AJ combinations, plus better Qx with superior kickers. My Qc6c is top pair but with a very weak 6-kicker and is dominated by a large portion of that range. Given the polarized turn raise and the medium/large river sizing, I estimate my showdown equity is well below the ~24% needed to call profitably. Raising would be a pure bluff and unnecessarily inflates the pot OOP against a player who has shown value. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better, less dominated spots.

Button with A♣7♥ — a clear open-raise candidate in a 3-handed pot. I have position on both blinds and an Ace-high hand that plays well postflop. I want fold equity and to avoid giving the big blind a cheap price to see the flop, so I’ll open to a standard 3xBB. That size (300 total) balances extraction and protection; if I get one caller I’ll C-bet many flops, but I’ll be prepared to fold to a large 3-bet because my kicker is thin.

Folding 2h-6d (worst starting hand) from small blind out of position against Minni's button raise. With terrible hand quality, poor positional disadvantage, and no redeeming features, this is a straightforward fold despite the 100 already invested. Minimizing losses on bad hands is essential for long-term profitability.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Five offsuit. The button (Minni) has raised to 300. While I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, Jack-Five offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Given that I am currently the short stack at the table with 66 big blinds, I should avoid defending with marginal hands that are difficult to play post-flop. I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

K3o is too weak to open from the button despite having positional advantage. While position would normally allow a wider range, K3 offsuit creates too many problems post-flop if called, especially against opponents with healthy stacks who show tendency to defend/re-raise. Better to preserve chips and wait for stronger holdings.

I am in the Small Blind with 4h3h. Since the Button has folded, it is just me and Minni in the Big Blind. 4h3h is a speculative suited connector that can be played in a blind-vs-blind situation. Raising is generally better than limping to take initiative and potentially win the pot immediately. Given Minni's frequent aggression, a standard raise to 300 might take the pot down now or allow me to play the hand with the lead if called.

Facing a 300 open from the small blind and I’m in the big blind with A♠7♦. Heads‑up A7o is a defendable hand — the ace blocker reduces opponent’s strong A‑x combos and gives reasonable equity against a typical SB opening range. Pot is 400 and I need 200 to call, so I’m getting roughly 33% pot odds; versus a wide open range that’s an easy call. A min‑3bet to 500 buys little fold equity and will put me out of position in a bigger pot; a larger 3bet is possible but I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal offsuit ace. I’ll call 200 more (300 total) to keep the pot manageable, see a flop, and plan to proceed: barreling/raising with top pair or strong draws, folding to heavy aggression on dry misses, and using the ace blocker for selective bluffs later.

On this dry Ace-high flop, I maintain a significant range advantage as the pre-flop raiser. Although my actual hand (4h3h) only has a gutshot straight draw and a backdoor flush draw, this is an ideal texture to continuation bet. A small bet size of 200 (one-third pot) is standard here to fold out Minni's air and low pocket pairs while keeping my own bluffs efficient.

Flop: A♥ 2♣ 8♦ — I have A♠7♦: top pair but a weak kicker. Pot is 800 and Flash (SB) made a small c‑bet of 200, so calling costs ~25% of the pot. Flash opened preflop and is betting a small sizing here, which indicates a fairly wide c‑bet range (overcards, small pairs, some bluffs and some Ax). The Ace blocker reduces the number of strong A‑x combos he can have, and my hand has good showdown value vs his range. Raising OOP with a marginal kicker would bloat the pot and force uncomfortable decisions on later streets against hands that dominate me (A8, A9, AK), while a small raise also buys little fold equity. I prefer an exploitative, pot‑controlled line: call the 200 to keep worse A‑x and bluffs in, see the turn, and re‑evaluate. Plan: call now; if the turn improves me or he checks I can extract value/pressure; if he barrels big on a blank turn, fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing.

The turn pairs the board with the eight of spades. My opponent called a flop bet on an ace-high texture, which suggests they likely have an ace, a piece of the board, or a pocket pair. Given that the eight is now paired, it is less likely they have an eight, but they are still very likely to have an ace which won't fold to a second barrel. My hand only has a gutshot straight draw (needing a five). I will check here to see how Minni reacts and potentially realize my equity cheaply or fold if the bet is too large, as my current hand has very little showdown value.

I have A♠7♦ on A♥ 2♣ 8♦ 8♠ — top pair with a weak kicker. I called the small flop c‑bet to keep Flash’s range wide; his check on the turn is ambiguous (weak Ax, medium pair, or a trap with a very strong hand like A8/88). Those full‑house/quads combos exist but are relatively rare given his small flop sizing and the line. Checking back surrenders value to worse A‑x hands and gives a free river card. I want to extract thin value from worse aces and charge any turn‑bluffs without bloating the pot against the rare monsters. Bet ~1/2 pot is the right compromise: it gets called by worse A‑x and many marginal hands, but limits our commitment if he check‑raises. If Flash check‑raises large, I’ll give credit and fold (he’s usually showing full house/quads); if he calls, I’ll re‑evaluate on the river. Pot is 1000, so I’ll bet 500.

On the turn, the board pairs with the eight of spades. Having checked after my flop continuation bet was called, I am now facing a 500-chip bet from Minni into a 1000-chip pot. My hand, 4h3h, only has a gutshot straight draw (needing a five) and virtually no showdown value. The pot odds are 3:1, requiring roughly 25% equity to call, but my straight draw only offers about 8.7% equity. Given that Minni called the flop bet on an Ace-high board and is now betting when the board pairs, she is likely holding an Ace or an eight. Continuing with such a weak hand out of position is not profitable.

I am on the button with 8h 4s, which is a very weak hand even in a three-handed game. Given the active play of Minni and Claude in the blinds, attempting to steal with such a low-equity hand is risky. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my remaining chips.

Heads-up in the SB with 7♠9♥. Button folded so I’m opening against the BB (Claude). Pot is 150 and calling only costs 50, but raising to the minimum (200 total) is the standard aggressive play here: it applies pressure, widens my range in SB, and gets plenty of fold equity from an out-of-position big blind. 7-9o is playable heads-up and fine as a steal attempt; if he 3-bets big I’ll fold and preserve most of my stack, if he calls I take the initiative and can c-bet favorable flops or proceed cautiously when I miss. Raise to 200 (min-raise) to try to win the pot preflop or play the flop with initiative.

I'm in the Big Blind with 4d3s, one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni raised to 200 from the Small Blind after Flash folded. I need to call 100 more, but this is a clear fold. Even with 3:1 pot odds, 4d3s has virtually no showdown value and no meaningful equity against a raising range. There's no indication Minni is making a light steal—they have reasonable position and a raise. Folding preserves chips for better spots where I have playable hands. This is a straightforward decision.

I’m on the button with K7s — a suited king with good positional value and reasonable playability. Action is folded to me with a 150 pot (SB 50 + BB 100), so raising is the +EV play: I can steal the blinds, build a pot when I have position, and play postflop with a hand that can make top pair, flushes, and blockers to strong Kx hands. Stacks are deep (I ~168bb, SB ~170bb, BB ~60bb), so I don’t want to overcommit with a marginal holding; I’ll use a standard open size that applies pressure but keeps the pot manageable. Raise to 300 total (3x the big blind) — large enough to gain fold equity versus the blinds, but small enough to fold to a big 3-bet if faced with heavy aggression. If called I’ll use my positional advantage to c-bet favorable textures and proceed cautiously against strong resistance.

K4o in the small blind is too weak to defend against Minni's button raise, especially with the big blind still to act. The hand lacks playability out of position and I should preserve chips for better spots. Fold is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten suited (Ac Tc), which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Minni has opened from the Button, which is typically a wide range. I will 3-bet to 1000 to take the initiative, build the pot, and capitalize on my hand's strength against a likely wider opening range.

I opened to 300 on the button and Flash (BB) 3-bet to 1,000. I have Ks7s — a suited, playable hand with positional advantage but not strong enough to 4-bet profitably as a bluff against a likely polarized 3-bet range. Pot is 1,350 and it costs me 700 more to call (I’d have 1,000 committed). The call gives me ~34% break-even equity and lets me use position to realize equity, play draws, and take control on favorable flops. Folding surrenders too much equity; 4-betting would commit too many chips with a marginal hand and expose me to a shove or dominated calls. Plan: call, see the flop, continue aggressively on spade/K flops or when I pick up good equity; otherwise control the pot and fold to heavy aggression.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and have two overcards along with a backdoor flush draw on this 7-4-9 board. While this board doesn't perfectly hit my range, a continuation bet is standard here to maintain the initiative and put pressure on hands in Minni's range that may have missed, such as smaller pairs or lower broadway cards. I will bet 700, which is approximately one-third of the pot.

I have top pair (7s) with a king kicker on a fairly dry flop (7c 4h 9s). Flash 3-bet preflop, so his range contains strong value hands (QQ+/AK/TT/JJ/99) but also many bluffs/weak broadway and suited hands — his flop lead is very small (~700 into a 2750 pot), which looks like a probe/continuation rather than a huge value bet. Calling is the +EV line: it keeps worse hands and bluffs in, lets me use position to control the pot, and avoids committing a big chunk of my stack into a pot where I could be crushed by sets or overpairs. Pot odds to call are good (~20% break-even), and my hand equity versus a wide 3-bet c-bet range comfortably exceeds that. Raising would risk getting 4-bet/shoved by the parts of his range that dominate me and would fold out many worse hands I beat. Plan: call the 700, see the turn, and either extract value or fold to heavy turn aggression depending on the action.

The turn 9c is a strong card for me as it gives me the Nut Flush Draw to go with my two overcards. I was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, but with the board pairing and the SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) now being approximately 1.08, I am effectively playing for my whole stack. Checking here allows me to see how Minni reacts to the board pairing. If she checks behind, I get a free card to hit my flush or an Ace/Ten. If she bets, my equity with the nut draw and overcards is high enough that I will likely commit the rest of my chips, potentially by shoving to maximize fold equity.

I have top pair (7s) with a King kicker but no backdoor draws now — a decent but vulnerable one-pair. Flash’s line (3-bet pre, a small flop c-bet, then checking the turn) looks more like weakness or a probe than unabashed strength, though a player holding any 9 (A9/K9/99) can now have trips and could be trapping. With position and a hand that wants to extract value from worse Kx, Ax floats and small pocket pairs, I prefer a small-to-medium value bet rather than checking behind and giving up initiative.

Bet sizing: the pot is 3450. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot (1200). That size charges worse hands and unpairs, keeps worse hands in, and leaves plenty of room to fold if Flash check-raises big (which would strongly indicate trips/very strong holdings). If he calls I maintain control of the river and can decide whether to barrel or check down depending on the river card and his action. If he folds I take down the pot now.

Action: bet 1200 — extract from weaker hands while keeping the pot manageable against a potential check-raise/trap.

On the turn, the board has paired (9s 9c) and I have picked up the nut flush draw to go along with my two overcards (Ace and Ten). Minni has bet 1200 into a pot of 3450. My remaining stack is 4425. With a stack-to-pot ratio of approximately 1, this is a situation where I should be looking to commit my chips. By shoving, I maximize fold equity against Minni's bluffs or marginal hands like a 7 or a small pocket pair, while retaining significant equity (roughly 30-34%) even if she calls with a 9 or a better hand. Calling is mathematically acceptable given the pot odds, but it leaves me in a difficult position on the river if I miss. Shoving is the most aggressive and profitable play with a high-equity draw in this spot.

I have two pair on the turn (9s/9c and 7c with my 7s, K kicker). Flash’s line is polarized: a preflop 3‑bet + small flop c‑bet then checking the turn, and now a very large check‑raise, points to a lot of value (9x trips/quads, 77/full houses, occasional sets like 44) but also some big bluffs (AK/AQ type hands). I need ~26% equity to call (call 3,225 into a 9,075 pot => pot after call ≈12,300), and two pair here has reasonable equity versus a polarized 3‑bet/CR range — many of his preflop 3‑bet hands (AK, AQ, some overpairs) are behind me while only specific combos (any 9, any 7, pocket 4s/quads) crush me. Raising would be committing a lot into a range that very often has me crushed; folding two pair to this sizing is too nitty and gives up value too often. Plan: call 3,225 (total committed this street 4,425) to realize equity and see the river, then fold only to a shove that clearly represents a full house/quads or heavily re-evaluate if he barrels the river big on a blank.

QK is a premium hand and I'm in excellent position on the button. I should raise to build the pot and apply pressure. Minni is the only opponent in the BB. A standard raise to 250 (2.5x BB) is appropriate for this strong hand in position. This gives me the best chance to win the pot preflop or play it in position postflop with a strong range advantage. With 26 hands remaining and solid stack depth, I want to accumulate chips with premium holdings like this.

Heads-up on the button vs my big blind with 8h8c. Claude opened to 250 (pot 350, I must call 150). Pocket eights are a very strong heads-up holding and Claude's button-opening range looks wide from his prior lines, so 3-betting for value and fold equity is the correct strategy rather than just calling and playing OOP. I will 3-bet to 900 — large enough to fold out weaker broadways and some Ax hands and to get value from worse pairs/suited connectors, but small enough to leave deep stacks for postflop play. If Claude responds with a very large 4-bet shove I will re-evaluate and frequently fold; versus a smaller 4-bet I can call and play the deeper postflop game.

QK is a premium hand with approximately 45-50% equity against Minni's 3-bet range. The pot odds are favorable at 1.77:1, requiring only ~36% equity. Calling allows me to: 1) Keep my hand uncapped - Minni won't know if I have QK, AK, or a premium pair; 2) Play in position on all future streets, which is valuable in heads-up; 3) Extract value if I hit the flop while minimizing risk if I miss. At 900 total, I'm investing less than 4% of my stack. Minni's 3-bet from the BB could be a re-steal given her aggressive tendencies throughout this session, making a flat call with position optimal. With 26 hands remaining, building chips efficiently through postflop play is better than getting into a marginal all-in situation preflop.

I flopped a set (8h8c with 8s on Jd-8s-7s) — an extremely strong hand heads-up. The board is somewhat coordinated (two spades and a straight possibility with T9), so there are a few hands that can already beat me (mainly JJ or T9) but those combos are limited. Claude called my 3‑bet preflop, so his continuing range contains many worse hands (Jx, mid pairs, K/Ax, spade draws, 9x, etc.) that will pay me off. I want to both extract thin value and charge/protect against spade/straight draws, so I’ll make a half‑pot value/protection bet. Bet size = 900 chips (≈1/2 pot) should get calls from worse pairs and draws while denying free cards. If Claude raises, I’ll re-evaluate — small raises get called to continue extracting; a very large shove will be weighed carefully but my default is that sets are usually good and I will not fold lightly.

QK on J87s has solid equity (~35-40%) against Minni's likely range. Pot odds require only 25% equity to call profitably, so this is a mathematically sound play. Calling allows me to play in position, keep my hand uncapped, and exploit Minni's aggressive tendencies which suggest a wide range. Multiple outs (any K, Q, or 9) provide additional equity. With reasonable stack depth and 26 hands remaining, seeing another card in position with positive expected value is the optimal strategy.

I have a set of eights on J♦‑8♠‑7♠‑6♦. Claude called my flop half‑pot bet, so his continuing range contains lots of one‑pair hands, spade draws, mid pairs and a few straights (T9) or pocket J's that currently beat me. My objective is to extract value from worse pairs and charge draws while denying a free river. With the turn pot at 3600 a ~50% pot value/protection bet is appropriate — it gets calls from Jx, 7x, 6x, K/Q/Ax and spade draws while charging the few straight/flush draws. I’ll bet 1,800. If Claude check‑raises or shoves big I’ll re‑evaluate (most of the time I’ll call with a set), but on this street building the pot and protecting is the priority.

Calling is mathematically sound with 30-35% equity against Minni's range requiring only 25% equity from pot odds. QK has multiple outs (K, Q, and 9 for straight) and solid equity that favors calling. Position on the river is valuable for making informed decisions. Stack depth remains healthy at 13,405 remaining. This exploits Minni's aggressive tendencies while maintaining positive expected value. With 26 hands remaining, accumulating chips through profitable +EV spots is critical.

I have a set of eights on J♦‑8♠‑7♠‑6♦‑9♠ — very strong but the river 9♠ changed things: any Ten in Claude’s hand makes the 10‑9‑8‑7‑6 straight, pocket 9s make trips that now beat me, and any two spades in his hand now have a flush. However Claude’s line (open -> call 3‑bet -> call my half‑pot flop & turn bets) is classic for a wide calling range of one‑pair hands (Jx, 7x, 6x, K/Ax), single spade draws, and some T/T9 combos. The majority of that range is still behind my set, so I should be extracting thin value while keeping the price small enough to induce calls from marginal hands and missed draws. A ~1/3‑pot river bet (~2400 into 7200) is the right compromise: it charges missed draws, gets calls from worse pairs and Ax/Kx, and avoids bloating the pot into the relatively small number of combos that now outright beat me. If Claude check‑raises big or jams I’ll re‑evaluate, but my default with a set is to call reasonable shoves. Action: small value bet to extract from the weaker part of his calling range.

Calling is mathematically sound. Pot odds require 20% equity (2400 into 12000), and my K-high should have 25-30% equity against Minni's mixed value/semi-bluff range given her three-street betting line. While she likely has a made hand, my strong kickers and position value throughout the hand make this a profitable spot. With 26 hands remaining and positive EV opportunities becoming scarcer, this is an appropriate marginal call at the threshold of profitability.

Heads-up on the button with K2o — marginal but playable in position. The pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to call, but opening here is standard: raising gains initiative and fold equity versus the BB. I’ll make the minimum raise to 200 to apply pressure and keep the pot controllable. If Claude 3-bets big I can fold this weak hand; if he calls I can use position to c-bet or probe postflop and realize equity. Overall a small raise maximizes long-term EV by stealing the blind and leveraging positional advantage.

I'm in the Big Blind with 4♠5♣ facing a 200 raise from Minni. While I'm getting 3:1 odds to call, this hand is too weak to play profitably. 

Key considerations:
1. Hand strength: 4♠5♣ is a marginal hand with poor playability - a low-card connector that misses the flop often and plays poorly from out of position.
2. Position: I'm in BB (out of position post-flop), which is a significant disadvantage against an aggressive opponent like Minni.
3. Opponent tendencies: Minni has been raising frequently with a 2.6:1 chip advantage.
4. Stack dynamics: Down 18k in chips (10,905 vs 28,795), I need to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
5. Risk/reward: Calling 100 more to play a weak hand out of position against a competent opponent is unprofitable long-term.

Given the weak hand quality and positional disadvantage, folding is the standard play here. I should wait for better spots with improved equity.

Button position with Q8o is an excellent spot to apply pressure. This hand has decent equity if called and strong fold equity if Minni folds. With 10,855 chips, I need to be aggressive to build my stack. A raise to 200 total (150 additional) is a standard button raise sizing that gets value if called and often wins the pot uncontested.

Heads-up vs the button (Claude) who opened to 200 from the SB. I’m in the BB with K♥2♠. Pot is 300 and I must call 100 to see the flop, which gives ~25% break-even equity (100 into a 400 pot). Against a typical wide SB opening range my K2o has sufficient equity to defend; folding would be too tight and a light 3-bet with K2o is suboptimal (we’d be OOP postflop and a minimum reraise to 300 gives little fold equity). With deep stacks I prefer to see a flop and play postflop cautiously: continue when I make top pair or strong draws, otherwise check-fold to significant aggression. Call 100 (total committed 200).

Heads-up I’m out of position on an A88 two-hearts board with K♥2♠. I have no pair and no real draw (only a remote backdoor heart runner), while the preflop raiser’s range contains lots of A‑x, pocket 8s and other strong hands that connect well here. Betting as a bluff has very little fold equity against a wide opening range and just commits more chips with essentially zero showdown equity; checking keeps the pot small and lets me fold to meaningful aggression. If Claude checks back I get a free turn to reassess; if he bets small I can call some frequency to realize equity vs bluffs, and if he fires a sizeable bet I’ll fold and preserve stack. Check to control the pot and reevaluate on the turn.

Flopped trips (three 8s) with queen kicker. This is a monster hand on this board. Minni checked from the big blind, showing weakness. I need to extract value and build the pot. Betting 250 (roughly 60% of pot) is appropriate sizing - enough to build the pot while maintaining reasonable fold equity. With my shorter stack, I should capitalize on strong hands when ahead.

I checked the flop to control the pot with K♥2♠ on A♥ 8♥ 8♣. Claude leads small — 250 into a 650 pot (~38% of the pot). I have no pair and only a remote backdoor heart runner; my clean outs to make a pair are 6 (three Ks + three 2s), roughly ~24% to hit by the river. Calling 250 wins a pot of 900, so I’m being offered ≈27.8% pot odds. Against a wide heads-up c-betting range there will be a meaningful fraction of bluffs/missed overcards, and the price is cheap enough to realize equity—whereas raising OOP with K2 is a weak bluff that bloats the pot with poor blockers. I’ll call the 250, keep the pot manageable, and re-evaluate on the turn: fold to significant turn aggression unless I pair or pick up a clearly favorable runout.

I have K♥2♠ on A♥ 8♥ 8♣ 9♦ — no pair and no real draw. Claude opened, c-bet small on the flop and I called; now he checked the turn which strongly signals weakness or pot‑control with marginal hands. Betting OOP here with essentially zero showdown equity is a bad play: I have poor blockers to credible bluffs and would only bloat the pot versus Ax/88/strong hands that beat me. Check to keep the pot small, take a free river to pair or re-evaluate, and plan to fold to significant river aggression unless I improve.

Betting 400 on the turn with trip eights and a Queen kicker. Minni's check shows weakness despite calling the flop. This bet is appropriately sized to extract value from weaker hands like KQ, QJ, pocket pairs, or ace-X holdings while building the pot. My stack depth allows for committed play, and I should continue extracting value from this monster hand.

Board: A♥ 8♥ 8♣ 9♦. I hold K♥2♠. Claude (preflop raiser) c‑bet the flop and now barrels the turn for 400 into a 900 pot (1300 total). Calling 400 would make the pot 1700, so I need ~23.5% equity to justify a call (400/1700 ≈ 23.53%). I only have 6 clean outs to improve to a pair by the river (3 kings + 3 twos) → ≈13.0% equity (6/46 ≈ 13.04%), well below the required threshold. Claude’s line strongly favors value (A‑x, A8/A8x full houses, some pocket pairs) and small sizing on both streets is consistent with thin value or pot control; I have virtually no chance to realize enough equity and very little fold equity if I try to raise as a bluff (I don’t block enough of his value combos). With no realistic path to profitability on the turn I fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

Heads-up on the button with K♦9♦ — a playable, suited king. With deep stacks (28.6k vs 11.3k) I want the initiative and to avoid limp/call OOP play. Opening to a standard heads-up sizing applies pressure, isolates the BB and builds a pot I can play in position. A 3x BB sizing (raise to 300 total) is a good balance: it gets value from worse kings, broadways and suited connectors, and folds out some of BB's weak holdings. If Claude 3-bets I can re-evaluate based on sizing; if he calls I'll C-bet appropriate flops and leverage position postflop.

Minni raised to 300 from the button with a wide range. I'm holding J5o in the big blind, which is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Despite getting decent pot odds (3:1 to call 200 into 400), this hand lacks the necessary equity and playability to justify a call. J5o is vulnerable against Minni's range and difficult to extract value from post-flop when out of position. With 11,255 chips, folding preserves my stack for better spots. This is a clear fold scenario.

Heads-up play on the button with A9o. This is a strong position with excellent equity. Standard aggressive button strategy dictates raising with this hand to build the pot, apply pressure, and leverage positional advantage. A9o is well above the calling threshold and definitely within my raising range heads-up. Raising to 250 (2.5x BB) is a reasonable sizing that applies pressure while not overcommitting with a moderately strong but not premium hand. Minni is deep-stacked and aggressive, so I need to play positionally strong hands with aggression.

Claude opened to 250 from the button in a heads‑up spot. I hold 9♦2♦ — a weak but suited hand that has multi‑street playability heads‑up. Calling 150 more (250 total committed) defends the big blind cheaply, keeps Claude’s wide opening range in, and gives me implied odds to hit flushes/straights. A 3‑bet to 400 would be an overbluff with this holding and unnecessarily bloats the pot against a deep but not huge stack. I’ll call and play the flop cautiously.

Flop: 6♣ 8♦ 7♣. I hold 9♦2♦. This is an open‑ended straight draw (any 5 or T completes a 5‑6‑7‑8‑9 or 6‑7‑8‑9‑10) — ~17% to hit on the turn, ~31.5% to hit by the river (8 outs). I also have a tiny backdoor diamond flush (need both turn+river diamonds, ≈4%), but that’s irrelevant compared to the straight draw. Claude opened preflop from the button so his range is wide heads‑up; he hasn’t bet the flop yet. With a strong drawing hand OOP I prefer pot control and to realize equity rather than lead into his range or inflate the pot with a semi‑bluff. I’ll check to see his reaction — if he C‑bets a reasonable size I can call and chase my 8 outs; if he checks behind I get a free turn to improve or take the initiative later. Betting here would bloat the pot with only a draw and unnecessarily commit chips against a likely wide calling/raising range.

Heads-up on button with A9o in position. I raised pre-flop to 250, Minni called from BB. Flop came 6c-8d-7c, which is a somewhat coordinated board but I still have decent equity with ace-high and multiple outs (9s, Aces, wheel straight draw). Minni checked, indicating either a miss or slowplay. Standard position-based poker dictates I should continue with aggression here. I have reasonable equity (~45-50%), and applying pressure is correct. A9o is above the calling threshold for aggression in heads-up play. Betting maintains my image and extracts value while Minni is likely in a weak position. Sizing: 350 (70% of pot) applies pressure without overcommitting.

I checked the flop as planned and Claude fired 350 into the 500-chip pot (pot now 850). I hold 9♦2♦ — I have an open-ended straight draw to 5 or T (8 outs, ≈31.4% to hit by the river) and only a tiny backdoor diamond flush. Calling 350 to win a 1200-chip pot gives ~29.2% immediate pot odds, so a straightforward call is +EV. I’m out of position, so a raise here would be a large semi-bluff that bloats the pot with only 8 outs and unnecessarily commits chips against Claude’s wide heads-up range. With deep stacks I also have good implied odds to extract value if I hit. Plan: call the flop, see the turn, and if I complete the straight or get a favorable runout I’ll press for value; if I miss and face heavy turn aggression I can fold to avoid committing too much with just a draw.

Board is 6♣ 8♦ 7♣ 3♦, I hold 9♦2♦ (BB). On the turn I now have an OESD to 5/T (8 outs) plus a 4‑card diamond flush (9 diamond outs). Counting overlap (5♦/10♦) I have ~15 unique river outs ≈ 15/46 ≈ 32.6% to improve to a straight or flush. There's no bet to face now and I'm out of position, so I prefer pot control and to realize equity rather than bloat the pot with a semi‑bluff. Checking keeps weaker hands (and bluffs) in the field, avoids committing a large portion of my deep stack, and preserves the ability to call a reasonable river bet or check‑raise selectively. If Claude bets the river I can reevaluate based on size and his line; if he checks behind I get a free chance to hit my outs. Action: check.

Minni has checked flop and turn, signaling weakness. With A9o on the button and positional advantage, I should continue applying pressure on the turn. A 475 bet (40% of pot) is appropriately sized to extract value from weaker hands in Minni's range (weak pairs, missed draws) while charging any potential draws. This maintains my aggressive image and capitalizes on the weakness demonstrated by consecutive checks.

Claude (BTN) bets 475 into a 1675 pot on the turn. I hold 9♦2♦ on 6♣ 8♦ 7♣ 3♦. I have an open‑ended straight draw to 5/T (8 outs) plus backdoor diamond draws (9 diamonds) with two overlap cards (5♦,10♦) for 15 unique outs total. That’s 15/46 ≈ 32.6% to improve on the river. The required equity to justify a call here is 475/(1675+475) ≈ 22.1%, so a simple call is +EV. 

I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a semi‑bluff raise that could be re‑raised or force me to commit with only ~32% to improve. Calling keeps weaker hands and bluffs in, preserves my implied odds with deep stacks (I have ~28k vs his ~10k), and lets me realize my equity. Plan: call the turn, re‑evaluate on the river — extract value if I hit, fold to large jams if I miss and face heavy aggression, or call smaller river bets selectively.

I missed my draws — the river (3♣) is not a diamond and doesn’t complete my straight. I have nothing (9-high) on a board that just paired (6♣ 8♦ 7♣ 3♦ 3♣). Claude has shown a willingness to fire multiple streets (preflop raise, flop c-bet, turn bet) so his continuing range is heavy with pairs and better; while he can bluff, my hand has virtually no showdown value and poor fold equity as a river bluff. With the pot at 2150 and no bet to call, the lowest-variance, +EV line is to check and see showdown rather than bluff into his range or bloat the pot with pure air. Action: check and fold to a river shove or call only a very small bet on this board.

River with A9o on a 6c-8d-7c-3d-3c board after Minni checks through the hand. My ace-high is likely ahead of Minni's weak checking range, but betting doesn't improve my expected value. If she calls, I likely lose to a made hand. If she folds, I win the same pot. Checking reaches showdown with a hand that's probably the best, optimizing my value without unnecessary risk on the final street.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a wide range. Q6o is marginal but perfectly playable in this spot — I want to use position and steal the blind rather than limp. Pot is 150 and a call is 50; raising to the min (200 total) is a standard, compact open that preserves fold equity without overcommitting a mediocre hand. If Claude folds I win the pot; if he calls I have positional advantage and can c-bet on favorable textures or check/realize equity on dry boards. If he 3-bets large I’ll fold Q6o; only very small 3-bets get considered for a call depending on his tendencies. Raise to 200 to maximize EV in this heads-up spot.

Calling Minni's min raise with 97o from BB. Getting 3:1 immediate odds to a 300 chip pot. Min raises often signal weakness and are exploitable with position. 97o has reasonable playability postflop with strong positional advantage as BB acting last on all streets. Excellent risk/reward at these odds.

Checking with middle pair (9s) and backdoor flush draw on Q♣9♠5♦. This is a cautious play from out of position designed to keep the pot manageable while gathering information on Minni's hand strength. The pair is solid but not premium on this texture. Position disadvantage suggests I should see action from Minni before committing additional chips. A check allows me to evaluate Minni's bet or to take the lead on the turn if Minni shows weakness.

Heads-up I opened a wide range preflop and planned to use position to c-bet favorable textures. I’ve hit top pair (Qd) on a very dry Qc‑9s‑5d board; Claude’s check is either weakness or a slowplay from a narrow strong hand. Betting here extracts value from worse pairs (9x, 5x), weaker Qx and small pocket pairs, and denies free cards to the few turn draws. I size to ~60% pot (250 into 400) — big enough to get value and fold equity but small enough that many worse hands will call. Plan: if he folds I take it; if he calls I re-evaluate on the turn and continue for value on safe cards; if he check‑raises large I’ll give credit to sets/two‑pair or strong Qx and proceed cautiously (likely folding to an overbet/commitment raise).

Calling Minni's min-raise with 9♣7♣ on Q♣9♠5♦. Middle pair with an open-ended straight draw and backdoor flush draw provides solid equity against Minni's likely range. Getting 2.6:1 odds on a call into 650 chips. Min-raises often indicate weakness, but rather than raising and potentially applying too much pressure out of position, calling allows me to realize equity on the turn. If I hit a K or 6, I make a strong straight. The call keeps flexibility while my multiple outs (8 straight cards + 2 pair cards + 2 flush cards) provide excellent fold equity-free equity.

Betting 450 on the turn with 9c7c (middle pair + OESD) after Minni checked. Minni's pattern of min-raising the flop then checking the turn indicates weakness. My middle pair is strong on this board, and I have 8 outs to make the nuts with a straight. Betting for value extracts chips from weaker holdings and applies pressure. With my short stack, I need to build the pot with strong equity hands.

I have top pair (Q) with a poor 6 kicker on a very dry Q‑9‑5 board. I c‑bet the flop and Claude check‑called, which is a wide line (9x, 5x, underpairs, some Qx and occasional floats/draws). The 4♥ on the turn is a blank and his small bet (450 into a 1350 pot ≈ 1/3 pot) looks like a thin value/probe rather than a polarizing overbet. Pot odds to call are ~25% (450 to win ~1800), and my hand is ahead of a large portion of his range. Raising here would unnecessarily polarize and commit my weak‑kicker top pair to tough decisions vs sets or better Qx; calling keeps control, keeps worse hands/bluffs in, and preserves position for river play. Plan: call 450; if he checks the river I can size for value, if he bets small/medium I’ll call more liberally, and I’ll fold to a large overbet/shove absent a river improvement or a very strong read.

Checking down on the river with a pair of nines. I have a solid but not premium holding on Q♣9♠5♦4♥J♠. Minni called my turn bet (450) which suggests a medium-strength hand, not a premium one. Given my out-of-position disadvantage, I prefer to see what Minni does rather than apply more pressure with a medium-strength hand. The pair has good showdown value. Betting risks giving up chips to a stronger hand without significantly improving my EV against weaker holdings that will fold to modest river bets. Check allows me to realize equity and avoid putting in money with marginal value.

I have top pair (Q) with a very weak kicker on a dry Q‑9‑5‑4‑J board. Claude check‑called my flop c‑bet with a wide range (9x, 5x, underpairs, some Qx/floaters) and then bet the turn — that line shows some strength but his river check looks like pot‑control or weakness rather than a polarized monster. A small value bet (~1/3 pot) is best: it gets called by worse pairs and stubborn underpairs while folding pure air, and it avoids over‑polarizing this weak kicker hand. I’ll bet a modest size to extract thin value; if he raises big I can concede to sets/two‑pair and fold, but I expect many of his hands to call a small river bet.

Calling the 600 river raise. Getting 5:1 pot odds (3000 to win vs 600 to call), which only requires 16.7% equity. Minni's weak flop min-raise and turn call pattern suggests she doesn't have a premium hand. My pair of nines has solid showdown value and wins roughly 40% against her likely range (Queen, Jack, weaker pair, or bluff). The favorable odds and her passive action pattern throughout make this a profitable call despite the scary river Jack.

I'm on the button (small blind) with 7♣3♦ against Minni in a heads-up situation. This is one of the weakest hands in poker - an unsuited 7-3 combination. While position is favorable (button), the hand quality is too poor to justify playing. With only 50 more to call and a stack of 10,780, I could theoretically call to see a flop, but 7-3 has minimal equity and minimal potential to make strong hands. Given the weakness of this hand even in favorable position, folding is the correct decision. I'll preserve chips for better opportunities ahead in these final 20 hands.

Heads‑up on the button I have J♣8♠ — a playable hand with positional advantage postflop. Limping/calling 50 is too passive and gives Claude the initiative; folding is too weak. I’ll open‑raise to the minimum (200) to seize initiative, exploit my position, and apply pressure on Claude’s blind. If he folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I’ll C‑bet appropriate flops and proceed cautiously versus a large 3‑bet (likely folding J8o to heavy reraise). This size keeps the pot controllable while maximizing steal/fold equity and preserving deep‑stack flexibility.

I'm in the big blind heads-up against Minni who has raised to 200. I hold T5s.

Key factors:
1. This is a heads-up situation (2 players only), where button ranges are typically much wider
2. The raise size is small - I only need to call 100 more into a 300 pot
3. Pot odds are favorable (100 to win 300+)
4. T5s has decent potential as a hand - two spades for a flush draw, and broadway potential
5. I have decent stack depth (10,680) with room to play

In heads-up play, folding the big blind to every button raise would be exploitable. At 50/100 blinds, this 2x raise is relatively small, and I should be defending my big blind with a wider range, especially with two-card broadways and suited hands.

Calling is the standard play here - I can evaluate the flop and play accordingly. If I miss, I can fold to aggression, but I'll have position and multiple ways to win with this hand.

Heads-up flop with T5s on 5h-Th-8h. I have top pair weak kicker plus a spade flush draw (~35% equity). Minni's 2x pre-flop raise could indicate a wide range. Checking allows me to control the pot, see Minni's action, and keep all streets alive. My hand has reasonable equity and multiple ways to improve (flush, improve kicker). Betting would risk getting value from weaker holdings or facing aggression. The three-heart board is notable - I have the ten but my flush must come through spades. Checking is the standard play.

Flop is 5♥ T♥ 8♥ and I hold J♣8♠ — middle pair (eights) with a decent kicker but no heart blocker. Claude called preflop so his range is wide and contains many hearts, Tx, and hands that either already have a flush or strong draws. This is a very wet board where a c-bet frequently runs into made flushes or strong Tx hands; betting here would risk bloating the pot when I’m often behind or easily exploited. I’ll check for pot control and to use position: I can call a small turn bet to realize equity or fold to large, polarized aggression representing a flush/ten. If Claude checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and can either take a free card or value-bet small when appropriate. Checking preserves stack and keeps options open against his wide calling range.

With top pair (tens) plus an 8-out spade flush draw on a three-heart flop, I have strong equity (~50% against most holdings). Minni's check-check line suggests weakness—either a missed draw or marginal holding. Betting the turn extracts value from worse hands, charges any remaining draws, and keeps the pot appropriately sized. A 250 bet is approximately 2/3 of the current 400-chip pot, a standard value bet in this spot.

Board is 5♥ T♥ 8♥ Q♥ — four hearts on board. I hold J♣8♠: middle pair (eights) with no heart blocker. Claude checked the flop and now leads small (250) on the turn. His line is consistent with both slow‑played flushes (he’d already have a flush on the flop) and with turn‑made non‑heart hands (Qx, some Tx) or occasional bluffs. Raising as a bluff is unattractive: I have no heart blockers and my raise will have poor fold equity against a range that contains many made flushes and top‑pair hands. Pot odds are favorable to a call — calling 250 into the pot (~1150 after a call) needs roughly 22% equity. Versus a reasonable mixture of Claude’s range (some Q/T value, some small pairs and bluffs), my pair of eights should clear or be close to that threshold. I’ll call to keep the pot controlled, realize equity in position, and re‑evaluate the river (fold to heavy action on a heart or obvious value line; value‑bet small on a blank non‑heart or extract maximum value if I improve to two‑pair/trips).

River check with T5s on 5h-Th-8h-Qh-9d. I have top pair weak kicker on a 4-heart board—highly vulnerable to Minni's holdings. Her check-down line (flop check, turn call, river check) suggests marginal strength like a small pair or weak ace, not a flush or strong hand. Betting thin on this wet board risks losing more chips if I'm behind. Checking allows me to realize my hand's equity at showdown without unnecessary risk. In heads-up play, when facing a river check with medium-strength hands on dangerous boards, going to showdown is standard.

Preflop I opened to 200 and Claude called. Flop 5♥ T♥ 8♥ gave me middle pair (8s) on a very wet three‑heart board, so I checked for pot control. Claude bet the turn 250 (Q♥) and I called — I had decent showdown equity vs his wide calling range but the turn completed four hearts. The river 9♦ is a blank and Claude checks. Betting here for thin value is unattractive: his line (check flop, bet turn, check river) can be slow‑played flushes, Qx/T x, or some bluffs, and I have no heart blocker so my fold equity is poor. A river bet would mostly get called by hands that beat me and fold only the very weakest single‑pair hands. With the pot already 900 and my pair likely behind a large portion of his range, the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line is to check and take showdown.

8-4o is one of the weakest hands in poker with poor playability. While button position is strong heads-up, this hand doesn't justify playing. Folding preserves chips for stronger opportunities where I have actual edge. With a 10,280 stack against Minni's 29,570, chip preservation is critical.

Heads-up on the button with Ad As — best starting hand in poker and huge preflop equity. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but I want to take the initiative and build a pot while keeping a lot of Claude’s calling range (AX, broadways, medium pairs, suited connectors) in. I’ll raise to 1,000 total. That sizing is large enough to extract value and deny free cards, but not so huge that many hands fold; if Claude calls he’ll have ~9,180 left and I’ll have ~28,670 behind, leaving plenty of postflop maneuvering room. If he 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (call or 4-bet shove depending on size), but flat calls will let me c-bet favorable boards and realize equity from position.

2h-Jd is a very weak hand, ranking among the worst in poker. While Minni is likely stealing from the button with a wide range, I'm out of position and have poor equity against their likely holdings. With 10,180 chips remaining in a 150-hand tournament at hand 134, this is not a spot to make a marginal call. The risk/reward is unfavorable - I'd be calling 900 more to win 1100, needing over 45% equity, which I don't have with 2-Jack offsuit versus a button raiser. Folding preserves chips for better spots ahead.

Raising with QQ in heads-up is the correct play. This is a premium hand that should be played aggressively. With a significant chip disadvantage (10k vs 29k), I need to build pots when I have strong hands. A raise to 250 (adding 200 to my small blind) is a standard, strong raise that builds the pot and applies pressure to Minni's range. This sizing allows me to get more value from weaker hands if Minni calls, and it's an appropriate aggressive play for a pocket pair of Queens in heads-up play.

Heads-up in the big blind facing a standard open to 250. The pot is 350 and I only need to put in 150 more (total committed 250) — about 3.33:1 pot odds, so I need ~23% equity to break even. J♣2♠ is marginal but acceptable to defend heads-up vs a wide button opening range; I can realize equity, see a flop, and avoid bloating the pot with a thin mini 3‑bet that leaves me out of position postflop. With deep stacks I prefer to keep weaker hands in and play the flop cautiously — fold to heavy action on dry boards, and continue with top pair/strong draws. Call to see a flop and proceed based on texture and opponent’s reaction.

Heads‑up on the flop I’m in the big blind with J♣2♠ on 9♥‑3♣‑7♣. I have no pair, one overcard (J) and only backdoor club outs (need both turn+river clubs). Claude opened wide from the button, so he’ll c‑bet a high frequency here, but my hand is too weak to lead for value and too marginal to polarize with a check‑raise bluff. With deep stacks I want to keep the pot under control and realize my equity — check and see how he reacts. Plan: if he bets small–medium (workable price, ≲ ~1/2 pot) call to see a turn and re‑evaluate; fold to large, committed pot‑sized aggression since I’m not yet a made hand; if he checks back, consider a small delayed stab on a turn that improves my equity (J or a safe card) or continue checking for pot control. Checking preserves flexibility and avoids bloating the pot with junk.

I'm holding QQ on a 9h-3c-7c flop in heads-up play. This is a premium pair that's currently ahead of most of Minni's opening range. Minni checked on the flop, which typically indicates a weak hand or a drawing hand rather than a strong made hand.

Given my significant chip disadvantage (9.9k vs 29.5k), I need to extract value when I have strong hands and build pots. A continuation bet is standard here - I need to build the pot while ahead and apply pressure on Minni's range.

The flop itself is relatively dry (no coordinated threats like broadway cards), and while there are two clubs creating flush draw possibilities, my QQ is a strong hand that's likely ahead. The 9 is the only card above my queens, and it's unlikely to be in Minni's strong opening range from the big blind.

A bet of 350 (approximately 70% of the pot) is appropriately sized to:
- Get value from weaker hands like Ax, small pairs, and draws
- Apply pressure without overcommitting
- Maintain control of the hand while managing my short stack effectively

This is a disciplined aggressive line that balances extracting value and protecting my equity.

Heads‑up on a dry, two‑club flop. I’m on the big blind with J♣2♠ on 9♥‑3♣‑7♣. Claude (button) bets 350 into a displayed pot of 850 (~41% pot). This is a standard c‑bet from a wide button range. I have no made hand but I can realize equity: calling 350 (total committed after call = 250 preflop + 350 = 600) gets me a pot of ~1200, so I need ~350/1200 ≈ 29% equity to break even. My hand’s chance to pair either my J or 2 by the river is ≈24%, plus a ≈4% chance to hit a backdoor two‑card club flush and small chance of two‑pair/trips — overall my improvement equity sits right near the break‑even mark. Against a wide c‑betting button range these marginal improvements can be enough, and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a bluff/raise here.

Plan: call and see a turn. If I hit a Jack or deuce (a made pair) I’ll proceed for value or raise depending on Claude’s sizing; if the turn brings a club I pick up a real flush draw and can continue. If I miss the turn and Claude fires a large, pot‑committing barrel (or jams), I’ll fold — I’m not committed with just J2. If he checks the turn I’ll re‑evaluate and use position/board to control the pot. Calling keeps my range wide, realizes equity, and preserves stack with this marginal holding.

I called the flop and the turn came Ac, giving me a one-card club draw (J♣2♠ -> 4 clubs, 9 clubs left -> ~9/46 ≈ 19.6% to hit on the river). Pot is 1,200 and I'm first to act OOP. With Claude opening and c-betting the flop he has a wide range that contains many hands (Kx/Qx/air) that will fold to turn pressure, though Ax hands are now stronger. Betting here is a semi-bluff: it folds out lots of non-A, non-club hands while still building the pot when I have ~20% to improve. I choose a half‑pot sizing (600) — big enough to create meaningful fold equity and to charge draws, but small enough that if he calls I still have reasonable implied equity to realize on the river. If he jams or makes a large, pot‑committing raise I will fold (I'm not a made hand yet); if he calls I'll go to the river and value bet if I hit, otherwise re-evaluate vs his river action.

Folding QQ on the turn after the Ace came and Minni raised. While QQ is normally a premium hand, the Ace on the turn combined with Minni's check-flop-call to my bet followed by a turn raise is a strong indication that Minni has connected with an Ace (AA, AK, AQ, AJ, AT most likely). My QQ is likely behind or at best 40% equity in a flip. With 15 hands remaining and a short stack of 9.6k versus 28.6k, preserving chips for better spots and avoiding marginal coin flips is the correct strategy. The cumulative loss in this hand already (250 + 350 = 600) plus calling another 600 for marginal equity doesn't align with optimal stack management.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) I should be opening extremely wide. 5♥3♥ is a playable suited connector with backdoor straight/flush potential and lots of post‑flop playability in position. The pot is 150 and the cost to just complete is small (50), but taking the initiative with a standard heads‑up open raises fold equity and isolates Claude. With deep stacks I can realize implied odds when I flop draws or small made hands. I’ll raise to a standard 3x BB (300 total) — big enough to discourage some marginal defenses but small enough to keep postflop maneuverability against a deeper opponent.

I'm in the big blind facing a raise to 300 from Minni on the button with 5-3 offsuit - one of the weakest hands in poker. With only 9480 chips remaining and a significant chip disadvantage against Minni (30120), I need to be selective about which pots to play. 

5-3 offsuit has very poor equity in any scenario. I'm out of position post-flop, and Minni's 6x raise from the small blind (50 to 300) suggests a reasonably strong hand or strong aggression. Even in heads-up situations, calling with this trash hand would put me in a difficult position with poor equity and no positional advantage.

With 150 hands remaining and my stack relatively short, I should fold and wait for better spots. Preservation of chips for premium hands is critical.

Premium heads-up hand on the button with positional advantage. AQo is strong in heads-up play and deserves aggression. Minni is a competent opponent but raises frequently. A raise to 250 (2.5x BB) establishes control, shows strength, and sets up for post-flop play. This is better than limping, which gives Minni favorable odds to play her range.

Heads-up vs a button raise to 250 (2.5x) from the SB, A♠4♠ is a clear BB defend — suited ace with good equity against a wide opening range. Calling keeps the pot under control, preserves stack depth postflop, and keeps many of the raiser’s weaker aces/broadways in his range. A 3-bet is an option as a light isolation bluff, but with deep stacks it often leaves me OOP and exposed to stronger Axs hands; I prefer to realize equity and play the flop. Plan: call for 150 more, continue with top pair/strong draws, and give up to heavy, polarized aggression on bad runouts. Total committed = 250 (I already have 100 in the BB + 150 call).

I have A♠4♠ on 4♥ 8♠ A♥ — that’s two pair (Aces and Fours) in a heads‑up spot after I called Claude’s button open. This is a very strong, value‑heavy holding but there are a limited number of hands that currently beat me (pocket Aces, pocket Eights, pocket Fours, or A8 two‑pair combos). Claude’s preflop raising range is wide heads‑up, so many of his A‑combos (AK, AQ, AJ) and single pairs/overcards are behind me and will pay me off. I want to charge worse A‑hands and pairs and deny free cards to the small number of turn/river runouts that can improve marginal hands, so I’ll make a value bet rather than check‑back. Bet sizing: pot is 500 — bet ~60% (300) to get called by worse A‑hands and pairs while not overcommitting if I face a large check‑raise; re‑evaluate on a raise (small raise -> call and reassess on turn, huge check‑raise likely folding to avoid getting stacked by a set/trips).

Raising to 900 on the flop with AQ and top pair. This is a strong holding on a favorable board (4h-8s-Ah). The raise accomplishes multiple objectives: builds the pot while I'm likely ahead, applies pressure to draws and weaker Ace holdings, and maintains positional control in heads-up play. The sizing is substantial but not stack-committing, allowing flexibility on future streets while gaining information about Minni's holding.

I have A♠4♠ on 4♥ 8♠ A♥ — top two pair. I bet 300 to charge worse A‑x and single pairs. Claude’s check‑raise to 900 is sizable and polarised toward value (sets: 44/88/AA, A8, or very strong single‑A hands), though it can include some bluffs. Folding here surrenders too much value against the majority of his range; reraising bloats the pot and risks committing a lot of chips to the subset of hands that currently beat me (sets, A8). Calling 600 more (total 900) keeps worse A‑hands and medium pairs in and lets me control pot size while seeing a turn to discriminate further. Plan: call now, re‑evaluate on the turn — continue for value on safe cards, fold or pot‑control if the turn heavily favors sets/two‑pair that beat me or if Claude barrels large/commits.

Current holding: A♠4♠ on 4♥ 8♠ A♥ J♦. Pot 2,300; opponent (Claude) effective ~8,330. On the flop I had two pair and bet 300; Claude check‑raised to 900 — a line weighted toward value (sets: AA/88/44, A8 two‑pair combos) with some bluffs mixed in. The J on the turn is a scary card because it brings in AJ combinations that now beat my A4 and pairs up a bunch of potential bluffs (Jx) into respectable hands. I have little in the way of redraws (no realistic flush possibility and only pairing the river or trips gives full house), so betting myself risks bloating the pot and getting raised/committed by the small subset of hands that currently and now definitely beat me.

Goal: control pot size, keep worse A‑x and thin bluffs in, and get more information. Checking is the best play — it avoids bloating the pot against likely value hands, induces bluffs, and allows me to call reasonable turn bets for value. Plan: check now; call modest turn bets (to extract from worse A‑x and stray bluffs), but re‑evaluate (and likely fold) to a large overbet/shove that polarizes strongly to sets/AJ/two‑pair that beat me.

Betting 1400 on the turn with top pair/excellent kicker. Minni's check indicates weakness. This sizing is aggressive but not stack-committing, allowing flexibility on the river while forcing Minni into a difficult decision with hands like weaker Aces, second pairs, or whiffed draws. The bet maintains pressure in a heads-up match where fold equity is critical.

I have A♠4♠ — top two pair (Aces & Fours) on 4♥ 8♠ A♥ J♦. On the flop I led and Claude check‑raised to 900; I called, which puts him on a value‑heavy/polarized line (sets: AA/88/44, A8 two‑pair, strong single‑A hands, plus handful of bluffs). The J on the turn makes AJ combos and some other two‑pair/paired hands possible, so I checked to control the pot and induce. Claude's 1,400 turn bet into the ~2,300 pot is a standard value/denial size but not an all‑in polarizing shove.

Raising now would bloat the pot and often commit me against the small subset of hands that already beat me (sets, A8, AJ) while folding out the worse A‑x hands I want value from. Calling 1,400 is the best line: it keeps worse A‑x and medium pairs/bluffs in his range, preserves pot control, and lets me see the river and extract thin value or fold to clear strength. If Claude barrels the river huge or jams in a polarized way I will fold; for a smaller river bet or a blank I will look to extract value from worse Ax and pairs.

Board: 4♥ 8♠ A♥ J♦ 5♦ — I have A♠4♠: top two pair. Claude check‑raised my flop bet and fired the turn — his line is value‑heavy/polarized (sets, A8, AA, AJ, some strong single‑A hands and a few bluffs). The river is a relatively harmless 5♦ (creates A5 combos that beat me but otherwise a blank). Checking risks giving up value to worse A‑x hands that will call; leading too large folds out thin calls and commits me to the small subset of hands that already beat me. I want to extract from worse Ax (AK/AQ/AT/possibly A5) and medium pairs while keeping the pot controlled against sets/two‑pair that dominate me. I’ll bet a small, value‑targeted size (~1/3 pot) — 1,700 chips — to get called by worse A‑hands and some pairs. Plan if faced with action: if Claude flats/calls I’m happy; if he jams or makes a large, polarized raise I’ll assume very strong (sets/two‑pair/AA/A8) and fold to avoid getting stacked.

Calling 1700 on the river with AQ for top pair. Receiving 5:1 odds requires only ~17% equity to break even. AQ beats Minni's most likely range (weaker Aces, draws, and bluffs), particularly after her passive turn check followed by aggressive river raise. At this stack depth (6930) with 14 hands left, folding strong made hands is -EV long-term. The river 5d is a blank that doesn't make obvious hands for her, and her turn check suggests she doesn't have premiums like AK or JJ. This is a profitable call.

Heads‑up on the button (SB) with 4♠8♥ — a weak but playable hand in position. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but opening with a small raise leverages my positional advantage and exploits the BB’s relatively short stack. I’ll make a standard heads‑up open to 2x the BB (200 total) to take the initiative and pick up the pot often; if Claude 3‑bets big I’ll fold this hand, but if he calls I can play post‑flop last and c‑bet or apply pressure on favorable textures. Keeping the raise modest keeps the pot manageable with a marginal holding while maximizing fold equity.

Raising with AQ from the big blind against Minni's button raise. AQ is a premium hand that should be played aggressively, especially with a short stack. A raise to 500 (requiring 400 more chips since I need to call 100) applies appropriate pressure on her marginal raising range while building the pot. This is standard 3-bet strategy with a strong hand from out-of-position.

Claude 3‑bet to 500 after my 200 open. Pot is 700 and I must call 300 (calling would make the pot ~1000, so I’d need roughly 23% equity). I have 4♠8♥ — a very weak, poorly‑connected offsuit hand with no useful blockers and terrible multi‑street playability against a standard 3‑bet range (pairs, broadways, suited Aces and some suited connectors). My earlier plan was to fold to a big 3‑bet and this qualifies. A 4‑bet bluff here is thin and unnecessary; calling commits me to tough post‑flop decisions out of position against a likely stronger range. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot. Folding is the best play.

Calling 50 to see a flop with Js6h in heads-up position. Position (button) is powerful in 2-player poker, allowing me to control post-flop action. The 3:1 pot odds justify calling a marginal hand, and I can leverage my positional advantage to make profitable decisions on later streets.

Claude (SB) just limped into my big blind. I have 4♠6♠ — a speculative suited connector that plays well postflop but is a poor hand to inflate a pot out-of-position. Pot is only 200 and there’s no to-call, so the cleanest, most +EV line is to check and see a flop cheaply against his wide limp/call range. Raising OOP here only bakes chips into a pot where I won’t have positional advantage and a min-raise won’t achieve much against a caller-styled opponent. Plan: check to see the flop; float or call small c-bets with draws and weak pairs, turn/river evaluate aggression or pot-control; apply pressure later when I connect or when I pick better spots with position and fold equity.

I have 4♠6♠ on A♦7♣2♣ — I completely missed. No pair, no club, only tiny backdoor outs (backdoor spade flush ≈5% by river, backdoor straight ≈1.5%). Pot is small (200) and I’m out of position vs Claude (button/small blind) who limp‑called preflop, so raising OOP here just bakes chips into a pot where I have no real fold equity and very little equity myself. Best +EV line is pot control: check and allow him to c‑bet; call small bets to float with the plan to realize equity or take the pot on later streets, but fold to large aggression or a shove from this short‑stacked opponent. Checking keeps options open and avoids bloating a pot with a garbage hand, so I check.

With Js6h on a flop of Ad 7c 2c, I have a marginal hand with no made hand and no strong draws. The Ace on the board is concerning and suggests Minni could easily have hit the flop. Since Minni checked, I can check back for free to see another card. While I have positional advantage on the button, this particular hand is too weak to profitably bluff or value bet. My best option is to take a free card - checking allows me to see if the turn improves my hand (any Jack, any Six, or potentially a 5-8 for a straight draw). This is a passive but correct play with a hand that has limited potential in this situation.

Heads‑up against a limp/call, Claude's range is wide and he checked the flop — he's shown a lot of weakness. I hold 4♠6♠: the turn Q♠ doesn't pair me but it does give me 10 spade outs plus 3 remaining 4s and 3 remaining 6s = 16 outs (~35% to improve by the river). That makes this a good spot to apply pressure as a semi‑bluff: I can win the pot right now vs many missed hands and if called I still have ~35% equity to improve. I choose a medium/size bet to get folds from A‑high, small pairs and Kx, while keeping the price reasonable if he continues. If Claude check‑raises big or shoves I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to large aggression since I only have a semi‑bluff); if he calls I'll proceed to the river and reassess.

Folding Js6h on the turn. I have complete air with only 6 outs (pair of Jacks or Sixes), which is roughly 13% equity. Facing a 150 bet into a 350 pot requires ~23% equity to break even. Minni's turn raise after the Queen suggests she hit the board. This is a clear fold without sufficient equity and unfavorable pot odds.

Heads-up on the button with A♠Q♥ — a strong hand. I have a huge stack (34620) vs Claude’s ~5230, so I want to build the pot and seize position while leaving room to maneuver post‑flop. A standard 3x BB open (300 total) maximizes value vs worse Ax/Kx/broadway hands, applies pressure to marginal hands, and keeps the sizing sensible if Claude 3‑bets. If he calls I play position postflop; if he 3‑bets large I’ll re‑evaluate based on sizing and pot odds.

Folding 3d-9d from big blind. This is a trash hand with poor postflop playability and limited showdown value. Despite decent pot odds (5:1), the fundamental weakness of 3-9o out of position makes this a clear fold. Minni is raising frequently from the button, but I should only defend with hands that have reasonable equity and playability.

In heads-up play from the button, I should be playing aggressively with a wide range. Ace-Five offsuit is a solid raising hand with position advantage. Raising to 250 (2.5x the BB) applies meaningful pressure while keeping my stack management reasonable. This is standard heads-up button strategy - I either win the pot immediately or take a flop in position with a playable hand.

Heads-up Claude opened to 250 from the button. I have J♦4♠ in the big blind. The pot is 350 and I must call 150 more (total commit 250); calling would make the pot 500 so I need ~30% equity to break even. Claude has been opening a wide heads-up range, so J4o has borderline but reasonable equity to see a flop; 3-betting with this weak offsuit hand is a poorly weighted bluff and folding too tight gives up good price. I’ll call to keep the pot manageable, realize equity and fold to significant turn/river aggression if I miss.

I called preflop to see a cheap flop, but 2♥-5♦-9♥ completely misses J♦4♠: no pair, no straight draw and no realistic flush draw (with only one diamond in hand you can't make a 5-card flush with two cards to come). I have ~6 outs to pair by the river (~24%), which is marginal and will often be second-best. Claude opened wide preflop and will c-bet frequently, but I'm out of position and have almost no fold equity or reason to build the pot with air. Best line is to check and fold to any bet — if he checks back I can freely see the turn and reassess with very little risk. I will not lead-bluff or check-raise here.

Heads-up continuation bet with a made hand. I have a pair of fives with an Ace kicker on a 2h-5d-9h flop. After raising pre-flop and getting called, Minni checked the flop which typically indicates a weak or cautious hand. With position and a made hand, I should cbet to apply pressure and either win immediately or take down the pot. Betting approximately 60% of the pot (300 chips) is standard sizing here.

Pot is 800 after Claude’s 300 flop bet; calling 300 would require ~27.3% equity (300 / (800+300)). My J♦4♠ has ~6 outs to pair (≈24% to hit by the river) and essentially no backdoor flush/straight potential. I’m out of position against a short-stacked opponent who opens wide and c-bets often, but the raw pot-odds vs my equity are negative and raising as a bluff OOP is poorly weighted. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Heads-up on the button with K♥7♦ is a limp-or-raise decision. Against a single big blind (Claude, ~54BB) and with a huge positional advantage and deep effective stacks, I should be opening a wide range — K7o is well within that range. Raising takes the initiative, gains fold equity, and isolates the opponent; limp/call is too passive and gives Claude the cheaper chance to see flops and exploit position. A standard size here is ~3x the big blind (300), which is consistent with table dynamics and keeps the pot manageable. Plan: fold to a large, committed 3-bet; if called, play postflop in position (c-bet favorable boards, proceed cautiously on dry Ace-high boards, extract value if I pair my King).

7-2 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker. Although I'm getting decent odds (2:1 to call 200), I'm playing from out of position against a button raiser. Minni has shown aggression throughout the session and has a significant chip advantage (34,220 vs my 5,380). Even accounting for fold equity, this hand has very limited value and my stack is meaningful enough that I shouldn't be playing marginal hands. With only 9 hands remaining in the session, preserving my chip stack and playing tighter is the prudent strategy. Folding is the mathematically sound decision here.

Heads-up on the button with 7♥6♥ (suited connector). Position is critical in heads-up play, and I should be raising a wide range from the button. This hand has playability with both straight and flush potential. A minimum raise to 200 is standard for speculative hands in heads-up, applying aggression without overcommitting with a marginal holding.

Heads-up vs the button (SB) who opened to 200. I’m in the big blind with T♣7♠ — a playable hand heads-up with reasonable equity vs a wide SB opening range. Pot is 300 and it’s only 100 to call (I’ve already got 100 in). Calling keeps the pot small, avoids unnecessary confrontations with a ~52bb opponent who can shove or call a light 3-bet, and lets me realize equity postflop despite being out of position. Pot odds on a call are ~25% break-even, which T7o should beat against a wide opening range. Plan: see the flop and proceed cautiously OOP — continue with top pair/strong draws, fold to heavy pressure on dry boards, and exploit favorable runouts.

Flop: 9♦ 2♠ 7♦. I have pocket T♣7♠ — second pair (pair of 7s) out of position. Villain opened from the button preflop and checked to me, which gives me a lot of weaker broadways, single-card overcards and some diamond draws in his range. Betting here accomplishes two things: (1) extracts thin value from hands that missed (A/K/Qx, K9/A9 sometimes), and (2) protects my pair by pricing out/charging diamond and straight draws. I don’t need a huge sizing — a ~50% pot bet balances value and fold equity without bloating the pot OOP. Plan: bet 200. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (continue with improved hands or good turn cards, fold to heavy aggression if the turn makes the board more hostile). If he check-raises big (pot-sized or shove-range), treat it as very strong and fold this vulnerable second pair.

Calling with 7♥6♥ (pair of 7s + gutshot straight draws). Pot odds are favorable at 25%, and this hand has legitimate equity with two ways to make a strong hand on the turn. With 7 hands remaining and a shorter stack, playing this holding aggressively makes sense. Minni's modest raise doesn't indicate overwhelming strength.

Turn brings Q♦ completing three diamonds on board (9♦-2♠-7♦-Q♦). I hold T♣7♠ — second pair with a mediocre kicker and no diamond. Villain called my flop bet so his range contains many diamonds, Qx, 9x and other broadways; the turn heavily increases the likelihood I’m behind to a made flush or an overcard. Betting OOP into a three-diamond board risks bloating the pot and getting check‑raised or called by hands that now beat me. I’ll pot‑control and check to see a river cheaply; if Claude fires a sizable bet I can fold given how often a bet represents a flush/strong pair, while I can call small bets for odds or target showdown on a safe river.

Checking back on the turn with pair of 7s. While aggressive play can work with short stacks, the Q♦ on the turn is concerning and could have completed many of Minni's likely holdings (straights with K-J, top pair with Q-x). With only 4,980 chips remaining across 7 hands, I need to preserve chips and make disciplined decisions. Checking allows me to see the river for free and re-evaluate based on the final community card. If the river improves my hand or checks through, I'm in a better position to act. This is more prudent than betting an amount that would functionally commit my entire stack.

I have T♣7♠ — only second pair with a mediocre kicker. Board: 9♦ 2♠ 7♦ Q♦ 3♦ (four diamonds). Opponent opened from the SB, called my 200 flop c-bet and then checked the Q♦ turn. That line is exactly the sort of line that contains a lot of diamond draws that now have a flush, some Qx/9x, and a bunch of weaker floats. On this river there are many combos that beat me (any diamond flush, Qx, 9x), and a bet here will mostly get called by better hands and fold only the very weakest hands — so a value bet or a bluff are both low EV.
