Pot-control is optimal: checking gets to showdown cheaply and preserves stack against a made flush. If he leads out I can fold to a big bet and only call small bluffs. Decision: check to showdown.

Value betting pair of sevens on a run-down board. Minni's aggressive flop raise followed by turn and river checks indicates a missed draw or weak hand. This is a profit-maximizing spot with a made hand where Minni is showing weakness. The 300 chip bet (37.5% of pot) is appropriately sized to extract value without overcommitting my short stack, with 7 hands remaining in the tournament.

Board is 9♦-2♠-7♦-Q♦-3♦; I have T♣7♠ (second pair). Opponent opened BTN, called my flop c-bet, we checked a very wet Q♦ turn and he now leads small (300 into a 800 pre-bet pot, pot now 1100). Calling 300 gives me ~21.4% break‑even equity (300/(1100+300)). His line and the river diamond make many of his call‑flop hands (any hand with a diamond) into a completed flush, plus Qx/9x that also beat my pair — so he has a lot of value here. However the bet is small and his range still contains non-diamond overcards and weak pairs that I beat or that will bluff sometimes. Raising is bad (I’d get value only from better hands and shove myself into stronger ranges) and folding would be too passive given the excellent pot odds. I’ll make the cheap call to see showdown; call 300.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind). J♦4♠ is marginal but perfectly playable in heads-up — I want to be aggressive and take the initiative. Claude’s effective stack (~4.6k) is small relative to mine so a standard 3xBB open will fold out weaker big-blind hands and give me position postflop if called. I’ll raise to 300 total: large enough to apply pressure and define ranges, small enough to keep my stack flexible. Plan: c-bet many flops in position; fold to a large 3-bet/shove unless odds make a call correct.

I'm facing a raise to 300 from Minni in the small blind with 2h 5d in the big blind. This is a weak hand (deuce-five offsuit ranks among the worst starting hands). While the pot odds are reasonable (needing to call 200 into 400), I'm out of position and the hand has poor equity against most of Minni's reasonable opening ranges from the button. Even accounting for Minni's aggressive tendencies, 2-5o doesn't warrant playing out of position. The long-term value of folding weak holdings in bad spots is significantly better than playing them. I have a decent stack size and should avoid marginal/losing situations.

Calling 50 to see the flop. With 90:1 pot odds and positional advantage on the button, I can profitably call with a weak hand and make decisions post-flop based on board texture and Minni's action. This allows me to play exploitatively with position.

I’m in the big blind with 7♠4♣ — a weak, unsuited holding with very little playability out of position. Claude (button/SB) just completed to the big blind (pot 200) so there’s no free value to be gained by inflating the pot preflop with a limp-raise. With ~45BB effective and a poor hand, the correct plan is to keep the pot small and see a flop cheaply where I can fold to aggression or continue only with real equity (pair, two pair, or decent draws). Checking preserves stack and lets me evaluate the flop before committing chips.

Heads-up on a Qc Td 6d flop with 7♠4♣ — I have no pair, no meaningful straight draw and only a backdoor club possibility. Pot is small (200) and Claude completed preflop, so his range is wide but includes many hands that currently beat me (Qx, Tx, overcards that c-bet, and diamond draws). With a weak, unplayable hand out of position I should keep the pot small and not invest chips — check and fold to a standard c-bet. Only continue if I improve or face a very unusual tiny bet I can justify calling; I will not bluff-raise here given stack dynamics and lack of equity.

Checking with 7-3o on Qc-Td-6d. While I have a 4-card diamond flush draw (4 outs), my hand strength is minimal. Minni's check suggests weakness. By checking, I keep the pot small, get a free card to potentially improve to a flush on the turn, and avoid committing chips with a weak hand. This is the optimal risk management play with position.

Out of position with 7♠4♣ on Qc Td 6d 2s — I have no pair and essentially zero equity. Claude completed preflop and checked the flop, so his range is wide but contains many hands that currently beat me (Qx, Tx, small pairs, and diamond draws). Pot is tiny (~200) and I have no reason to create action or risk chips with a naked bluff; best play is pot control. I will check, see the river, and fold to any meaningful bet (only consider calling an extremely small probe if it offers correct odds, which is unlikely here).

Check on the turn with 7♦3♥. Currently holding ace-high with 9 diamond outs (18% equity). Minni's check indicates weakness. The pot is small (200 chips), making a semi-bluff inefficient. By checking, I get a free card to improve my flush draw on the river while keeping the pot manageable. This maximizes equity realization with proper risk management and positional leverage.

Both players checked to the river on Qc Td 6d 2s 2h and I hold 7♠4♣. I have no pair, no meaningful blocker or showdown equity, and Claude completed preflop then checked flop/turn — his range is wide but contains many hands that beat me (Qx, Tx, small pairs, and some slow-played 2s). Betting here would be a pure bluff with poor blockers and limited fold equity given his stack relative to the tiny 200-pot. I preserve my stack and realize showdown by checking. If Claude now fires a meaningful bet I will fold — this spot is not worth manufacturing a bluff for marginal EV.

River showdown decision with 7♦3♥ on Q♣T♦6♦2♠2♥. I have 7-high, the worst possible hand. Minni has checked, indicating weakness, but that doesn't mean I can win by bluffing with 7-3 - one of the worst hands in poker to represent anything with. A bluff here is terrible because: (1) 7-3o has virtually zero showdown value anyway, so checking down is my best option, (2) the pot is small (200 chips) making a bluff inefficient from an EV standpoint, (3) Minni's call would lose money but I have no credible range with this hand, and (4) at this stack depth with 6 hands remaining, I should preserve chips and take the free showdown where I might get incredibly lucky or Minni might check with even worse (though unlikely). Checking down minimizes losses while maintaining my conservative, tight-aggressive strategy.

Heads-up on the button with A3s — suited ace with good blockers and strong heads-up playability. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50 to complete, but I want to be the aggressor and take the initiative from the BB. Claude has ~4,480 (~44 BB) and will defend a wide range, so sizing matters: a min-complete/call keeps too many hands in cheaply; a small raise (to 200) is predictable and gives him good odds. I raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to get fold equity, isolate, and play position postflop while keeping the pot a reasonable size. Plan: if he calls I’ll C-bet favorable flops and use position to fold to heavy resistance when I miss; if he 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (A3s can continue vs small 3-bets but fold to extreme aggression). Raise to 300 to seize control and maximize value/fold equity heads-up.

I'm holding As3c in the Big Blind against Minni's button raise to 300. While A3o is a weak hand, the immediate pot odds are favorable (risking 200 to win 400, approximately 2:1). At Hand 146 of 150, we're in the critical final stage of this cash game. My stack of 4480 is moderate, and with only 4 hands remaining, I need to generate chips when opportunities arise. Minni's min-raise (300 into 150) from the button could indicate a wide, speculative range rather than a premium holding. If I flop an Ace, I have potential to win a significant pot. The cost to call (200) is small relative to my stack, and I'll have good positional information post-flop as the Big Blind. Folding here would be overly conservative given the immediate odds and tournament situation.

With As3c on a QhKhQc board, I have virtually nothing - no pair, no draw, no value. Minni has checked to me (To Call: 0), indicating weakness or caution. With only 4 hands remaining and a significant chip disadvantage, the prudent play is to check back and take a free look at the turn card. I'll minimize losses with this weak hand while preserving chips for the final hands.

Flop: Qh Kh Qc. I have Ah3h — the nut heart draw plus the Ace blocker. Heads-up Claude checked; his calling range preflop is wide and contains many Kx, small pairs and missed broadways that will fold or pay to see a turn. With ~35% to make the nut flush by river and the valuable Ace blocker, a proactive C-bet is correct to build the pot when I have equity and to get folds from weaker hands. Pot is 600 — betting ~1/2 pot (300) charges Kx and worse draws while keeping the price reasonable for my draw. Plan: bet 300; if he folds I take it down; if he calls I realize equity on turn/river; if he check-raises small I’ll usually keep calling to see the turn with the nut draw and blocker, but I’ll re-evaluate/fold to an uncomfortably large shove that represents sets/trips.

Folding As3c on QhKhQc. While pot odds show 3:1, my hand has minimal equity against Minni's flop-raising range (likely 10-15% at best). Out of position with a 4280 stack and only 4 hands remaining before the end of the game, preserving chips for premium opportunities is the correct strategy rather than making a marginal call with complete air.

Folding T3o from SB in short-stack endgame situation. This is a weak hand with poor equity, especially out of position. With only 4 hands remaining and a 42 big blind stack, I should fold marginal holdings and wait for stronger premium hands. Preserving chips is more valuable than calling with a weak hand.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 3♦6♥ — a very weak hand for showdown but position gives me large leverage. With only one opponent and a comfortable stack I should open-raise a very wide range to seize the initiative and pick up the blinds/fold equity rather than limp and allow cheap boards. Claude has ~4,130 (≈41bb) and will defend sometimes, but a standard heads-up open to ~3x the big blind is appropriate here. I’ll raise to 300 total. Plan: if Claude folds I win the 150 pot; if he calls I play the flop in position and use c-bets to take the pot on many textures; if he 3-bets to a large size or shoves I’ll fold this hand rather than get involved with garbage.

With only 2 hands remaining and pocket fives in the big blind vs Minni's button raise, raising is the superior strategy. This applies pressure, has a chance to win immediately, and gives me decent equity if called. Simply calling allows the aggressive button player to control postflop play with his larger stack. Raising to 700 (600 more than my BB) is appropriately sized to apply meaningful pressure without overcommitting my 4,130 stack.

I opened wide to 300 heads-up to seize initiative, but Claude responded with a 3‑bet to 700. With 3♦6♥ this is a clear fold — my hand is very weak, is frequently dominated by his 3‑betting range (pairs, broadways, strong Ax), and has well under the ~28–29% equity required to profitably call here. The pot is 1,000 and I must call 400 (400/(1000+400) ≈ 28.6% break‑even), so calling would be a marginal, losing play vs a standard 3‑bet range. 4‑bet bluffing to 1,100+ is unnecessarily risky and not optimal with trash. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

AJ offsuit is a premium hand in heads-up play and from the button position I have a strong initiative. Minni is in the big blind with a deep stack but I should play aggressively with my strong hand. Raising to 250 (200 chips more than the current 50 to call) applies appropriate pressure, builds the pot in my favor, and gives me control of the hand. This is a standard aggressive play with a premium hand from a favorable position near the end of the session.

Claude opened to 250 from the button heads-up. I hold A♦4♣ — a playable ace but marginal out of position postflop. His sizing is small and likely indicates a wide stealing range, but I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP or give him an easy shove spot against his ~42 BB stack. I’ll call the extra 150 (250 total) to keep the pot controllable and realize equity. Postflop I’ll continue on A-high or very favorable boards and pot-control or fold to heavy aggression on coordinated/face-card heavy textures.

Heads-up on Kc 2d Jh with A♦4♣ — I have only ace‑high and I’m out of position. Claude’s small open preflop indicates a wide, often Kx/Jx/Ax heavy range; this board is face‑cardy and vulnerable to top pair. I want to keep the pot small and realize equity rather than bloating the pot OOP, so I’ll check and allow him to c‑bet. Plan: call small/medium c‑bets to continue as a bluff catcher and fold to large sizing/raises that represent Kx, two‑pair or better. If he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the turn and look for safer spots to extract value or fold to aggression.

I hold AJ on a K-2-J flop, giving me top pair with the best kicker. Minni checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise, indicating weakness. With only 2 hands left to play in this session, I should play this strong hand aggressively. A bet of 300 applies appropriate pressure, maintains my position, and builds value while keeping enough chips in reserve for the final hand. This is a standard continuation bet in heads-up play.

I checked the flop with A♦4♣ to keep the pot controllable OOP. Claude’s preflop raise from the SB and a relatively small 300 c‑bet into the 500 preflop pot represents a wide range (Kx, Jx, Ax, broadways and many bluffs). Calling 300 into an 800 pot gives me ~21% break‑even equity, which is well within reason for Ace‑high as a bluff‑catcher. I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with only Ace‑high by raising — that would turn hands like Kx or JT into uncomfortable decision spots and invite a shove from his ~3980 stack. Plan: call to realize equity and to evaluate the turn; fold to very heavy turn aggression if it clearly represents Kx/two‑pair, or extract value if I improve or he shows weakness.

Turn comes 5♦ on K♣ 2♦ J♥ 5♦. I have A♦4♣ — only ace‑high, no pair, and with two diamonds on board I cannot make a flush anymore (need two more diamonds). Claude has a short ~3.98k stack and opened/CB’d small on the flop, so his range is wide (Kx/Jx/Ax/small pairs and bluffs). He checked the turn (action is on me with To Call = 0), which usually indicates either weakness or a probe to induce a bluff‑bet. Betting OOP with just ace‑high would bloat the pot and get called by better hands (K/J) or turned into an awkward shove spot. I’ll keep the pot controlled and realize my equity by checking. If Claude shows aggression I’ll fold to large sizing that strongly represents Kx/two‑pair, but I’ll call reasonable bluffs/small bets depending on sizing and river texture.

I hold AJ making top pair with the best kicker (pair of Jacks with Ace). Minni has checked both the flop and turn, demonstrating weakness. This is the second-to-last hand, so maximizing value from strong holdings is crucial. A bet of 500 applies appropriate pressure, builds the pot when I'm likely ahead, and allows me to maintain sufficient chips for the final hand. This is a standard value bet with a premium holding against a checking opponent.

Claude opened preflop and barreled a small c‑bet on the flop, then fired another relatively small bet (500) on the turn into a 1600 pot. I hold A♦4♣ — ace‑high only, but heads‑up this has reasonable showdown value and it blocks some of Claude’s A‑x bluffs. The turn bet is too small to credibly represent only strong two‑pair/sets given his earlier lines; it’s consistent with a wide range (Kx/Jx/Ax and bluffs). Folding now would be too nitty; raising OOP with just ace‑high would bloat the pot and create awkward shove spots. Pot odds on a 500 call into 1600 are ~24%, which A‑high can clear against a wide barreling range. I’ll call to realize equity, keep the pot controllable, and re‑evaluate on the river — folding to a large shove/raise that clearly represents Kx/two‑pair, but calling small river bets or extracting if I pair up or he shows weakness.

Claude led small on the flop and turn showing a wide but weighted-to-value heads-up range (Kx/Jx/Qx/AX and some bluffs). He then checked the Q on the river, which is ambiguous but more often indicates showdown/control or a trap rather than pure air — with two streets of value‑seeking bets he has plenty of K/J/Q combos that beat Ace‑high. I only have A♦4♣ (ace‑high, no pair) and I block some A‑x but not enough to credibly represent a hand that will get worse hands to fold. Betting here would be a thin bluff: my fold equity is low against his two‑street aggression and a small bet would simply get called by K/Q/J and a larger bet risks a check‑raise shove I should fold to. With a big effective stack advantage and a moderate pot (2100), the lowest variance, highest EV line is to check and take showdown — call a reasonable bet but fold to aggressive shoves that clearly show value. Checking preserves chips and realizes my equity against his bluffs/missed draws.

Minni has checked three consecutive streets, indicating weakness. I hold top pair with best kicker (JJ + A), a premium holding on this K-2-J-5-Q board. With the second-to-last hand approaching, extracting value from strong hands is essential. A 400-chip bet applies appropriate pressure, builds the pot when likely ahead, and maintains the aggressive position I've held throughout this hand. This value bet extracts chips from weaker holdings while leaving sufficient reserves for the final hand.

Claude has shown a steady, small‑sizing value/bluff line (preflop open, small flop c‑bet, small turn bet, small river lead). I hold A♦4♣ — only ace‑high but I block some A‑x bluffs and the hand has reasonable showdown value heads‑up. Pot = 2500; his river bet = 400, so calling 400 to win 2900 gives me ≈13.8% break‑even equity. Against his wide, thin line I beat a meaningful portion of his range (missed K/J/Q draws, small pairs and pure bluffs) and folding here would be too nitty. Raising OOP with just ace‑high would bloat the pot, invite a shove, and is unnecessary — I’ll preserve stack and realize equity. Plan: call the 400 to take showdown, and fold to any large raise/shove that clearly represents value.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with A♦T♠ is a clear raising spot. Pot is 150 and my to-call is only 50, but I want initiative and to play postflop in position. Raising to 3x the big blind (300 total) builds the pot, isolates Claude’s wider heads-up range, and charges speculative hands. The size is big enough to pressure weaker holdings but not so large that a 3-bet automatically commits my entire deep stack; I can comfortably continue against typical 3-bets and use position to extract value or fold to very large 3-bets. Plan: open to 300, c-bet favorable flops and pivot if Claude shows heavy resistance.

Calling with Q-8 offsuit from the big blind against Minni's button raise. I'm getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win 400), which is sufficient to justify the call in the big blind. The hand has some showdown value, and with deep stacks I can play profitably post-flop. Minni has been raising frequently from the button, so my call is justified by pot odds alone.

Checking with Q-8 offsuit against Minni's preflop button raise. This is a marginal hand (Q-high) that doesn't merit aggression. By checking, I preserve pot control, allow Minni to show strength if she has it, and gain information for the turn. I have reasonable outs with any Queen completing top pair, and my stack management is better served by seeing another card at minimal cost.

I have top pair top kicker (A♦T♠) on a J♥ T♥ 5♣ flop in position after my preflop raise — a very strong showdown/value hand heads-up. Claude checked, so this is a standard c-bet spot to extract value from worse tens, Jx, medium pocket pairs and to charge the two-heart draw present. I prefer a mid-sized bet to both get value and deny favorable odds to draws; ~1/2 pot balances these goals and keeps many worse hands and bluffs in. Plan: bet 300. If called I’ll re-evaluate the turn; if face a small check-raise I’ll likely call and reevaluate (many check-raises here are bluffs or thin value), but I’ll respect a very large raise that represents two pair/sets.

Q8o on a 5-J-T board is merely queen-high with minimal outs. Minni's flop raise indicates genuine strength. Without meaningful equity against her range, calling out of position only puts more chips at risk with an inferior hand. Fold is the mathematically and strategically sound decision.
